parents.page_7: Four essential elements must be present for a survival situation to have a chance of a positive outcome: knowledge, ability, the will to survive, and luck. While knowledge and ability can be learned, the will to survive is hard-wired into our survival mechanism and may not be recognized until tested. People who are fully trained and well-equipped have sometimes given up hope in survivable conditions, whereas others who were less prepared have survived against all odds by refusing to give up. Anyone venturing into the wilderness should understand basic survival principles to carry out correct beforehand preparation, choose the right equipment, learn how to use it, and practice necessary skills. Knowing how to survive in a particular situation allows you to assess your situation, prioritize your needs, and improvise items of gear that you do not have. For example, while you may be able to start a fire using a lighter, you must know what to do if it stops working. Similarly, anyone can spend a comfortable night inside a one-man bivi shelter, but you must know what to do if you lose your pack. Survival knowledge and skills must be learned and practiced under realistic conditions because starting a fire with dry materials on a sunny day teaches very little. The real survival skill is in understanding why a fire won't start and working out a solution. The more you practice, the more you learn, as finding solutions and overcoming problems continually adds to your knowledge and helps you deal with similar problems should they occur again. Always apply the principle of expending the least amount of energy for the maximum amount of gain.
parents.page_8: Research the environment you are preparing for to understand both its attractions and its dangers, as there is a thin line between awe and being at nature's mercy. Fully understanding an environment allows you to select appropriate equipment and know how to use it effectively when needed. Never underestimate the power of nature regardless of your equipment quality or skill level. If plans fail, stop immediately to reassess your situation and priorities. Do not hesitate to turn back and try again later, as the challenge will remain available tomorrow. The most effective survival strategy is to avoid entering a survival situation in the first place.
parents.page_11: Never assume that the amount of prior preparation required is directly related to the length of a trip or its perceived danger. Most survival situations arise either from being thrust into an uncontrollable scenario or developing from a sequence of events that could have been avoided by recognizing danger signs early. Unfortunately, many such situations occur due to ignorance, arrogance, or underestimating the forces of nature. Whether preparing for an overnight camping trip or a year-long journey, the more prepared you are for the specific environment, the more likely you will be able to cope physically and mentally if confronted with a survival situation. It is equally important to choose the correct equipment and clothing. When selecting gear, consider what you would need to survive if the worst happens. This constitutes your "first-line" equipment, which you should carry at all times. It must enable you to address the basic principles of survival relevant to your environment and consists of the clothing you wear and your basic survival equipment, such as a survival tin and belt order. Before heading off, double-check that your gear works and ensure you know how to use it properly. The more you understand how and why a piece of equipment works, the better able you will be to improvise if it gets damaged or lost.
parents.page_12: A walking stick is one of the simplest yet most important survival aids you will ever need and should be the first piece of equipment to improvise if you find yourself in a survival situation. Known as the survivor's third leg, it increases your ability to support yourself by allowing you to have two points of contact with the ground at any one time. This reduces the chance of slipping, which is crucial because your ability to walk may be your main means of rescue; reducing your mobility seriously reduces your ability to survive. Your walking stick can be employed in many different survival situations including supporting you as you walk, protecting your face when walking through thickets or gorse, checking for adequate support when crossing marshy ground, testing the ground ahead for obstructions, checking the depth of water when crossing streams and rivers, protecting you against wild animals, forming a ridgepole for your shelter, helping with pace counting, spearing fish or catching game, and digging up roots or plants.
parents.page_13: The pre-impact period involves recognizing threats and warnings where individuals may respond with denial or over-activity. The impact period is life-threatening, during which about ten to twenty percent of people remain calm and aware, up to seventy-five percent become stunned and unable to react rationally, and ten to twenty-five percent exhibit extreme behaviors like screaming. If the recoil period fails to restore normal reasoning and emotional expression within three hours to three days, individuals may develop psychiatric disorders. The post-trauma period follows directly after impact, often lasting between three hours and three days, characterized by a gradual return to normal abilities but also marked by guilt, depression, anxiety, and aimlessness known as Post-Traumatic Stress Disorders. Understanding these psychological reactions is crucial because mental breakdown compromises survival chances; contrary to popular belief, panic does not normally occur unless contagious, though psychogenic shock from moving into unknown situations requires mental preparation to manage stress effectively.
parents.page_14: Panic arises from the fear of potential outcomes and often occurs when individuals are trapped or face a time limit for escape. Depressed victims may sit amidst chaos, gaze vacantly, fail to respond to questions, remain unaware of their situation, and be unable to help themselves, thereby risking further injury. Hyperactive victims are easily distracted, talk excessively, generate many ideas, and frequently offer unhelpful suggestions. Aggression, anger, and hostility are common reactions that can be irrational and may even be directed at rescuers or medical staff. Some survivors feel guilty for having survived while others did not, and some irrationally blame themselves for causing the incident. There have been cases where disaster victims committed suicide immediately after being rescued, so all rescued individuals must be monitored closely. Most people who die from psychological trauma do so within the first three days following the event. Individual reactions to survival situations vary, but common emotional responses include panic, depression, hyperactivity, anger, and guilt, which may occur during or after any survival experience.
parents.page_15: Mental preparation is essential for survival, as reactions to disaster stem from direct psychological shocks or aggravating factors that must be identified and avoided to maximize chances of overcoming problems. Hunger initially poses little threat but leads to significant psychological changes after a long-term lack of food, resulting in apathy, irritability, depression, and an inability to concentrate. Thirst is a more acute problem than hunger, particularly for survivors at sea or in the desert, often causing agitation, irrational behavior, delusions, and visual hallucinations. Seasickness can create an overwhelming desire to give up, which can become fatal; to combat it, keep a fixed point like the horizon in sight and take small sips of fresh water if supplies allow, though these must be rationed in life-or-death situations. Both hunger and thirst produce physical and psychological effects that lead to loss of concentration, memory impairment, motor dysfunction, faulty decision-making, and irrational behavior. Physical exhaustion may exist from the start or develop due to sleep deprivation and prolonged hardship; survivors often find themselves unable to relax when they need sleep, causing fatigue to overwhelm them and deteriorate both mental and physical performance.
parents.page_16: Mental preparation is a critical component of survival, involving the development of coping strategies and psychological readiness. Prior knowledge serves as power, enabling individuals to handle survival situations more effectively than those who are unprepared. The will to survive is defined as refusing to accept death and involves overcoming extreme emotional and physical discomfort while setting and working toward specific goals. Maintaining hope is vital despite negative information or perceptions, as positive thinking helps ward off psychological trauma. Before acting, monitor your own condition to ensure you are capable of the task and observe others closely to identify those who are genuinely disturbed; offer simple words of comfort to help numb individuals become more responsive, but avoid administering sedatives. Acceptance of a situation does not mean giving in to it; instead, knowing when to be active or passive can improve survival chances. A powerful motivating force is the desire to reunite with principal figures of attachment, such as spouses, partners, children, grandchildren, and close friends.
parents.page_17: Plan your journey carefully regardless of whether it is a short day trip or a long expedition, and maintain a basic planning outline for regular trips that you can expand with supplementary details as needed. Minimize regret by anticipating potential problems and risks, planning to avoid them, and equipping yourself with the necessary knowledge and tools to handle them. Prioritize preparation to prevent poor performance, as understanding survival principles and knowing how to improvise can determine your fate in a crisis. Start organizational planning with essential show-stoppers such as money, passports, visas, vaccinations, tickets, and insurance. If traveling with a group, consider team dynamics by practicing on shorter trips beforehand to refine equipment usage and group organization. Research the social customs of every country you visit to avoid penalties, punishment, or imprisonment. When your team includes both men and women, plan specific arrangements for sleeping, washing, carrying loads, and responsibilities rather than assuming traditional gender roles like women cooking and men building shelters.
parents.page_18: Train yourself mentally and physically before departing on any journey to ensure you can operate within your capabilities, which allows you to fully enjoy the experience. Practice with all equipment under realistic conditions that mimic the actual trip environment to identify strengths, weaknesses, and personal limitations. Before setting off, assess the specific skills required and confirm you are capable of meeting those demands. When testing new gear, simulate real-world scenarios such as operating a GPS while wearing gloves in cold weather or pitching a tent in darkness and rain to verify functionality. Replicate your intended trip conditions during training by duplicating the environment, distance, weight load, and language barriers you will face. Gradually increase the weight you carry until it matches what you plan to take on the trip, as this conditions your body and helps you determine essential items. Research weather extremes for your destination, noting that areas like deserts can be hot during the day but drop below freezing at night. Train for the specific daily distance you intend to cover to assess if it is achievable and sustainable. If traveling to a country where your native language is not widely spoken, learn useful phrases beforehand or bring a phrase book and electronic translator.
parents.page_19: Even meticulously planned trips can encounter unexpected difficulties such as strong winds or injuries, so it is essential to prepare an emergency plan of action. Rescue services operate more effectively when they possess all relevant information about your situation. You should keep people informed of your intended whereabouts and itinerary dates so they can raise the alarm if you deviate from your plans. Write down your trip details including pertinent places and dates, create a standardized emergency plan of action that considers your worst-case scenario, give a copy to your next of kin and group members, keep a copy for yourself, and inform local services like park ranger stations of your plans. Your emergency plan should contain up-to-date information about you, such as a recent photograph, passport details, the equipment you are carrying, and the languages you speak, especially if traveling in another country. If you go missing, this information allows rescue groups to understand your intent and plan effectively, enabling them to find and help you quicker.
parents.page_20: Before departing, ensure you have a complete emergency plan of action including your full name as it appears on your passport, current contact details such as phone numbers and email addresses, and the names and locations of next of kin. List all necessary medications, such as anti-malaria tablets, and note any severe allergies like reactions to amoxicillin. Record specific trip details including designated campsites with their grid coordinates, vehicle descriptions with license plate numbers, and the names of group members. Establish a clear communications plan that outlines when you will contact family or friends, acknowledging potential limitations in cell reception on the trail, and provide alternative contact numbers for yourself and your companions. Define a daily itinerary specifying which campsite to visit each day and whether to park vehicles at specific locations. Identify foreseeable problems or intentions for each day of the trek and designate emergency rendezvous points such as ranger stations with their grid coordinates and telephone numbers. Include personal identification details like passport number, expiration date, distinguishing marks including scars and tattoos, date of birth, driver's license information, languages spoken, height, weight, hair color, and swimming ability. Document your outdoor skills and experience, noting any military survival training or bushcraft courses attended to ensure readiness for living outdoors in the field.
parents.page_21: Before heading into the wilderness, fully prepare by researching how native inhabitants dress, work, and eat to understand their adaptations. This knowledge helps you select the best gear, adopt effective techniques, and learn correct survival skills, which is crucial since many survival situations result from avoidable sequences of events. Always prioritize water over food because your body needs water to digest food.
parents.page_22: The four basic principles of survival are protection, location, water, and food, which should generally be prioritized in that order. Water is critical; while you can survive a few days without it, your mental and physical performance will decline significantly within 24 hours. If you are injured, exposed to hot weather, or have heavy workloads, you may only survive a few hours without water. Your location determines your chances of survival and rescue. You typically have two options: stay put or move. Staying is preferred if you can mark your location to aid rescuers. If staying is unsafe due to imminent danger, move to a location that offers better survival odds or rescue opportunities. Choose a spot that maximizes your ability to attract attention. Food becomes crucial as the duration of the survival situation increases. You can survive five to seven days without food even with moderate workload, though you will feel hungry, become tired, experience slowed movements, and lose the body's ability to repair itself. Unless you are already malnourished, starvation is unlikely within a week. Protection involves staying in a condition that allows you to remain proactive for continued survival and rescue. Physically, protect yourself from injury, environmental elements, and wildlife. Mentally, guard against fear, guilt, and depression that can diminish your will to live. Lighting and maintaining a fire is the best way to achieve protection, as it offers physical safety while providing a sense of security and familiarity that helps normalize dire situations.
parents.page_23: Surviving in temperate areas is generally favorable due to mild climates and abundant natural resources, though rivers, lakes, and swampy wetlands are common where drainage is poor. The greatest threat in these regions is hypothermia, particularly during winter and at night. In rainforest environments, while resources are plentiful, heat, humidity, dangerous animals, and dense vegetation create challenging conditions. Mosquitoes pose a significant fatality risk, making the identification of edible plants crucial to avoid poisonous species. Navigation is difficult due to dense undergrowth, increasing the danger of getting lost. Essential survival practices in rainforests include making noise to scare away most animals who wish to avoid you, boiling or treating all water to prevent illness, keeping covered and washing frequently to combat infections caused by high humidity, always building shelters and sleeping off the ground, keeping any found dry tinder protected from moisture, and following rivers downhill as they typically lead toward civilization and the coast.
parents.page_24: Climate and terrain can vary widely, so prepare for a range of eventualities. Weather can change quickly, so check the local forecast before you set off and carry an AM/FM radio to listen to local weather reports. Plan a realistic route and prepare an EPA, being ready to reassess your route during the trip. Take clothing suitable for all possible conditions. Carry a survival can, knife, emergency equipment, cell phone, and first-aid kit, and learn how to use them. Carry adequate water and the equipment needed to purify more if necessary. Carry some form of basic shelter even if only going out for the day. Take a map and compass, and consider using a GPS as an aid.
parents.page_25: In cold environments, natural resources are often scarce, making survival dependent on your equipment and supplies. While shelters can be dug from snow, fire essentials are limited in polar and tundra areas. The greatest dangers include hypothermia and polar bears in northern regions. Survival is more feasible in the taiga where wood, fresh water, and edible flora and fauna are available. To prevent hypothermia and exposure, dress in loose-fitting layers of clothing to avoid overheating while ensuring your clothes stay dry and clean. If your hands become cold, do not warm them with your breath as this will make them wet; instead, tuck them under your armpits. Avoid sitting directly on the ground, snow, or ice; sit on your pack or create a sleeping platform using boughs to conserve body heat. Regularly check your face, toes, hands, and ears for frost nip, which is the first stage of frostbite. Take shelter from the wind whenever possible, especially during survival situations. Ensure your shelter is ventilated by keeping vent holes clear and checking them frequently, particularly during heavy snowfalls. If fire is your primary source of warmth, triple the amount of firewood you think you need to ensure it lasts the night. Survival in the desert requires full preparation due to hostile temperatures and scarce natural resources. Always prepare an Emergency Plan of Action (EPA) to notify someone of your plans before entering a desert area. Carry extra water and bring equipment designed to maximize your chances of procuring more water. If your vehicle breaks down, leave it only if staying there is no longer safe or feasible. When venturing into remote areas, augment your map and compass with a GPS, and consider taking a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) or satellite phone. The greatest dangers in the desert are dehydration and heat exhaustion. While African savanna areas may contain dangerous mammals, desert regions are also home to a range of venomous snakes.
parents.page_27: Surviving at sea requires relying on your supplies since natural resources in the open ocean are virtually nonexistent. Improvising shelter or location aids is limited to what you carry. Seasickness, particularly in a small liferaft, can impair your ability to stay hydrated and motivated. Your only drinking water supply comes from rain or mechanical desalination devices. Reaching shipping lanes greatly increases your chances of rescue. To increase survival chances at sea, pack sea-survival equipment as if your life depends on it. Take emergency immersion-survival suits to protect yourself and aid flotation and location. Always carry a Personal Locator Beacon. Bring several means of obtaining water. Do not abandon your vessel until absolutely necessary. Carry anti-seasickness tablets. In the mountains, survival prospects are good at lower elevations where trees, rivers, and edible plants and animals are likely present. At higher elevations, resources are fewer, and the risk of avalanches, crevasses, and cold-related injuries poses the greatest threat.
parents.page_28: High altitude and lower oxygen levels place higher-than-normal demands on your body and equipment. Respect nature and err on the side of caution, as rescue is unlikely to be quick or easy on a mountain. Plan an achievable route and prepare an emergency plan. Dress in layers; start a walk lightly dressed for cold conditions and add or remove layers as necessary. Wear a hat and gloves. Take a flashlight because weather changes and unforeseen problems may mean you are on the mountain in darkness. Carry an avalanche transceiver when traversing avalanche-prone slopes.
parents.page_29: Before departing on a trip, weigh your specific gear requirements against the limitations of your chosen mode of transportation. Forward planning is essential when deciding what equipment to take; assess your personal needs, the likely weather and terrain, and the amount of gear you can transport. The environment of the region will dictate the type of equipment needed, but your proposed mode of transportation is the main constraint on the quantity of gear. Additional weight makes traveling uncomfortable, consumes valuable energy, slows progress, and limits the distance you can travel each day. Organize and prioritize your gear so that any items needed in a survival scenario are always close at hand. Carry essential items such as a compass and flashlight around your neck for easy access. Attach a neck cord to sunglasses for security. Wear a hat for protection from the sun. Always keep a map on hand and store a waterproof copy in a survival tin. Keep the survival tin in a secure pocket. Strap a water bottle outside your day pack for easy access. Keep a cell phone in a secure pocket or in a waterproof bag attached to a neck cord.
parents.page_30: Before venturing out, you must categorize your equipment into three distinct lines based on their criticality to survival. First-line gear constitutes your basic survival equipment and includes essential outdoor clothing, navigation tools, and safety items. This category specifically comprises your bushcraft knife, firesteel, and belt pouch, which should be worn on your person so that if you must abandon the rest of your load, these are the only items available to help you survive. You must constantly risk-assess your situation and adapt your gear priorities as conditions change. Second-line gear is required to stay safe under normal conditions and should be carried at all times. Examples include a spare set of clothes, a bivouac sack, cordage, emergency rations, a first-aid kit, a hexamine stove with items for making hot drinks, a matchless fireset, and a metal cup. Third-line gear, also known as your sustainment load, is equipment needed to keep yourself alive and function for longer periods. The amount of third-line gear you carry depends on your mode of transportation and carrying capacity. This category includes a form of shelter like a tent or tarpaulin, cooking utensils such as a stove or pot, a backpack, food stores, a sleeping bag and mat, large water containers or hydration packs, and wash kits with sanitary items. Regardless of the total amount of gear you take, organize every item into these three categories according to their importance to ensure essential items are always accessible. When packing your gear, follow the rule of reverse order: pack the things you will need first as the last items in your bag.
parents.page_31: Do not be tempted to overdress, as sweat can soak you just like rain and sudden temperature changes can cause hypothermia. Instead, wear layers to control your body temperature. Modern outdoor clothing is lightweight, hard-wearing, and versatile; choose fabrics suited to your specific environment. Layering traps air between layers to help you stay warm in any condition. Wearing the correct layers in the right order is essential. Use wicking fabrics like polypropylene for both hot and cold weather. Several light layers are better than one heavy layer because multiple layers give you the flexibility to fine-tune your temperature by adding or removing them. Good insulators include wool, fleece, microfleece, and down. The layering system consists of three parts: the outer layer repels rain while allowing sweat to escape, the mid-layers provide warmth with materials like fleece that retain heat even when wet, and the base layer wicks moisture away from your skin. Wear lightweight, full-length pants to shade your skin from the sun. Choose the right boots and wear a hat to prevent excessive loss of body heat through your head.
parents.page_32: Footwear selection should prioritize personal needs such as foot shape and required support, while also considering the distance, terrain, and cost. Before purchasing boots, wear them around the house and go on short hikes to ensure they are properly broken in. A good boot sole features a treaded outsole for traction, cushioning under the heel and toe for comfort, and shock-absorbing properties. Lightweight boots combine fabric and leather hybrids to offer the support and traction of heavy shoes with the flexibility of sports shoes. Hiking boots are typically made of durable, water-resistant leather that balances weight, durability, and protection. Jungle-trekking boots utilize rot-proof leather and canvas with a directly molded sole; they often include holes in the instep for ventilation and moisture drainage, along with high uppers to keep mud off pants. When packing a backpack, place items needed first at the bottom of the pack and heavy items close to your back to prevent the pack from pulling away from your shoulders. Store daily essentials in outside pockets, use a cloth bag for personal items, keep rain gear at the top of the pack, and carry an upright, accessible water bottle. The lower compartment should hold sleeping bags and mats. Additional features to look for include flexible breathable uppers that dry quickly, cushioning pads to absorb shock, insoles that keep feet in position, padded ankles for comfort and support, wide deep studs for grip, and full-length shank plates for rigidity.
parents.page_33: If you are in a group, try to help any others who are in danger. If no one knows you are missing or where you are, you must notify people of your plight by any means available. If you are missed, a rescue party will almost certainly be despatched to find you. If you have a cell or satellite phone, let someone know your predicament immediately. If your situation calls for an emergency rescue and you have a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), consider using it. You must address the principles of survival: Protection, Location, Water, and Food. Evaluate whether you can survive where you are; if no one knows your location and you cannot survive there without physical reasons to stay, move to a location that offers a better chance of survival or rescue. If you face extreme elements, find or improvise shelter immediately. Avoid confrontation with animals. Stabilize any injuries and apply first aid.
parents.page_34: Do not leave food at your campsite as it attracts predators. Avoid sheltering too deeply in the woods; your location must remain visible for rescue. Do not eat unidentified food to prevent illness, especially since food is not a priority in short-term survival situations. Before building a shelter, inspect the site for hazards such as insects, flooding, rock falls, wild animals, and deadfalls. Inventory and ration all your supplies. Keep a fire going to purify water, maintain warmth, and signal for rescue. Fill plastic bags or spare clothing with dry foliage to use as an insulated mattress or pillow against cold or damp ground. If in a group, assign tasks to everyone to keep them occupied and reduce worry. Do not ignore your fire; instead, constantly look for dry tinder, kindling, and fuel. When moving, walk faster than the slowest person in your group but be extremely careful walking downhill to avoid fatal twisted ankles. Regulate clothing to avoid overheating while moving and hypothermia when stationary. Use or improvise a walking stick to reduce trips and falls. Improvise shelter when not moving. Plan your route around potential or known water sources and filter or purify all water where possible. Keep location aids accessible while moving and when deployed statically. If you cannot survive in place but also cannot move due to injury, do everything possible to attract rescue. If your situation changes from staying put to moving to find help, consult the alternative survival guidelines.
parents.page_35: A basic survival kit is an essential item for any outdoor expedition and should be compact enough to carry at all times. Its contents must address the key principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food. You can buy ready-made kits, but you should always adapt the contents to your specific needs and the environment you will be in. Items in your kit should be high-quality and multi-purpose. Choose a tin with a waterproof seal and locking clasps for storage. Your kit should contain blister medical pads and bandages, including a range of both waterproof and fabric options in various sizes; waterproof bandages can also mend holes in tents and tarpaulins. Include antiseptic wipes for treating wounds and cleaning bites, noting that they can be ignited by a spark to start a fire. Carry water purification tablets, choosing between puritabs or iodine, but avoid iodine if you are allergic to it or to shellfish; remember that water must be filtered before being purified. Include petroleum jelly to apply to chapped lips, rashes, and sores, and store it in a small resealable plastic bag as it can also be smeared onto tampons to make them burn longer. Pack waterproof notepaper for drawing maps or leaving messages. Include a photograph of loved ones as a psychological incentive in a survival scenario. Carry a credit card which helps extract insect stings, and include money wrapped in cellophane.
parents.page_36: Space blankets or aluminum foil serve multiple purposes including signaling, providing shelter, carrying water, and cooking. Many are dual-sided with one silver side and another green for camouflage or orange to stand out. Plastic bags can function as water carriers or transpiration bags. Medicines such as painkillers and antibiotics provide essential basics if separated from main gear. A small candle offers a reliable flame to build fires around. Nylon tights provide warmth or act as improvised water filters, mosquito nets, or fishing nets. Small AM/FM radios, whether battery or solar-powered, are useful for communication. Surgical tubing can be used to procure water. Fire cans or matchless fire sets offer self-contained methods for starting fires. Sailmaker's needles are multi-purpose tools that can serve as arrow points or mend tents and tarpaulins; wide eyes are preferred. Safety pins secure clothing or mend sleeping bags and tents. Mini glowsticks provide emergency lighting and location aid. Non-lubricated condoms can carry water or act as waterproof covers for smaller items like cell phones. Mini fishing kits are useful near water, with fishing line adaptable for other tasks including hooks, flies, swivels, and split-shots. Waterproof matches and tinder balls start fires and should be stored in small resealable plastic bags. A pencil sharpened at both ends is versatile. Potassium permanganate dissolves in water to sterilize it, clean wounds, and make fire. Flashlights, specifically small white and red Photon lights taped in the off position, provide illumination. Mini multi-tools include useful features like compasses and saws. High-viz cards and signal mirrors act as location aids. Compasses serve as emergency back-ups. Flint, fire steel, and tampons are used for starting fires. Survival saws or pocket chainsaws can be wrapped around the inside of a tin or cut in half if space is limited. Single-edged razors function as multi-purpose tools. Needle and thread sets using strong waxed cotton pre-threaded through the needle are essential for repairs.
parents.page_39: Before setting off on an expedition, ensure you have a basic understanding of how to read a map and use a compass. Assessing a map of your intended area allows you to make informed decisions while preparing for your trip. Understanding the local area and terrain reduces your chances of getting lost and enables you to continually evaluate your progress, allowing you to alter your plans as necessary. You will be able to plan the safest and most appropriate route, locate water and shelter, and identify areas where you can use location aids properly. Proficiency with a map and compass eliminates worry about getting lost or straying off-track, freeing you to enjoy your outdoor experience. In a survival situation, effective navigation—whether using a map and compass or natural features—plays a major role in decision-making regarding whether to stay put for rescue or move to a safer area. While a Global Positioning System (GPS) is an excellent aid, it relies on batteries and technology that can fail, so do not rely on it exclusively. Before venturing into an unfamiliar environment, carefully research the type of terrain you will encounter and investigate the best methods for traveling safely and efficiently across it. Checking the weather forecast before you go allows you to evaluate conditions and make informed decisions prior to travel. The ability to assess your situation and modify your plans means that you will be able to avoid many potential survival situations.
parents.page_40: To test your ability to navigate using bearings and pace counting, select an open area where you can walk at least 330 feet or 100 meters in any direction without cheating by heading directly toward a marker. Begin by placing a marker on the ground as your starting point. Dial a specific bearing onto your compass, such as 110 degrees, and walk along this line while counting your paces until you estimate you have traveled the full distance, then stop. Next, add 120 degrees to your original bearing to calculate a new direction, dial this updated bearing onto your compass, and pace out another 330 feet or 100 meters before stopping again. Repeat this process one final time by adding another 120 degrees to the previous bearing, dialing the resulting angle, such as 350 degrees, and walking the same distance. If you have maintained your bearings accurately and counted your paces correctly, you should arrive back at your original starting point.
parents.page_41: Maps serve as two-dimensional representations of three-dimensional terrain, allowing you to determine distance and elevation while helping you visualize an area for easier navigation. Every topographic map includes a legend or key that explains the symbols used; this is essential for interpreting what features are shown. The scale indicates the ratio between the map size and actual ground distance, with a 1:25,000 scale offering the most useful level of detail for hikers, where 4 centimeters on the map equals 1 kilometer on the ground. A smaller scale like 1:50,000 provides only a basic overview. Contour lines depict height above sea level, colors indicate ground type, and specific symbols represent natural features such as water, mud, vegetation, vertical faces or cliffs, boulders, outcrops, coniferous trees, non-coniferous trees, bracken, heath, and rough grassland. Additionally, hiking paths are clearly marked on these maps to aid in route finding.
parents.page_42: Maps display height above sea level in meters using contour lines, which connect points of equal elevation to show detailed topography. The vertical distance between these lines, known as the contour interval, is typically 5 meters on scale 1:25,000 maps but may be 10 meters on mountain maps. To estimate distances accurately, use the map's scale; for curved routes around obstacles, curve a piece of string along the path and transfer it to the scale line, or use solder wire for greater accuracy as it maintains its shape while remaining flexible. Alternatively, lay a piece of paper between two points, mark the route, and measure it against the scale. On 1:25,000 maps, each grid square represents 1 kilometer, with diagonal travel covering approximately 1.5 kilometers. To plan energy-efficient routes, follow contour lines to bypass hills rather than walking up and down steep slopes. Grid references are determined by reading easting numbers first, followed by northing numbers; for example, a shaded area might have a four-figure reference of 2046. For greater precision, divide the grid square into tenths mentally to create a six-figure reference, such as 185445. Vertical grid lines are called eastings because their values increase toward the east, while horizontal lines are called northings.
parents.page_43: To navigate effectively, you must be able to orient your map to the land using a compass. A compass works by using a magnetized needle that rotates freely to align with the North and South magnetic poles. The four cardinal points are North at 0° or 360°, East at 90°, South at 180°, and West at 270°. Always hold the compass level to allow the needle to rotate easily. Never use a compass near metal objects, other magnetized items, electric currents like overhead power lines, or inside buildings and vehicles as these cause interference.
parents.page_44: Setting your map allows you to read the terrain and predict features as you move, helping you realize quickly if you are heading off course. Rotate the compass dial so that "N" sits under the index line. Lay your map flat on the ground and ensure nothing nearby affects your compass. Place the compass on the map so its edge runs parallel with a vertical grid line, known as an easting. Keep the orienting lines aligned with the grid lines on the map and rotate the entire map until the north magnetic needle sits inside the orienting arrow. The map is now set to magnetic north and should align with features around you. If the magnetic variation in your area exceeds 5 degrees, compensate accordingly. Map legends refer to north in three ways: true north, grid north, and magnetic north. True north is the direction of a meridian of longitude converging on the North Pole. Grid north runs parallel to vertical grid lines on a map and differs from true north because maps are flat. Magnetic north is the direction indicated by a magnetic compass. The angle between magnetic north and grid north is called magnetic variation, which is given in the map legend. When converting a magnetic bearing to a grid bearing or vice versa, you must adjust for magnetic variation. If the variation is west, use the phrases "Mag to grid—get rid" or "Grid to mag—add." If the variation is east, the opposite applies. Check your map's legend to establish the magnetic variation, which depends on your location and whether it is east or west of grid north. If the variation is 0 degrees, make no adjustments. When converting a magnetic bearing to a grid bearing with a 12 degree west variation, subtract 12 degrees. With an east variation, add it on. When converting a grid bearing to a magnetic bearing with a 12 degree west variation, add 12 degrees. With an east variation, take it off.
parents.page_45: Always give your full attention to any compass work, as rushing can lead to navigational errors that may cause you to get lost. To navigate using a map and compass, lay your map on a flat surface and ensure nothing nearby interferes with the compass reading. Use your compass as a protractor to calculate your bearing by laying the edge of the compass between your starting point and your destination. Ensure the direction of travel arrow on the compass points toward your destination on the map. You can also find your position by taking a bearing from a known feature back to yourself; this is done by taking a normal bearing and either adding or subtracting 180 degrees, or by reading the bearing exactly opposite to the index line. Never underestimate the combined power of a map, compass, and the skills and knowledge required to use them well, as your life may depend on it.
parents.page_46: To take a bearing using a map, first align the orienting lines on your compass with the vertical grid lines on the map. Read the bearing between your two points from the index line on the compass. Convert this grid bearing to a magnetic bearing by consulting the magnetic variation information in the map legend; add or subtract the appropriate figure and adjust your compass accordingly. Hold the compass level at chest height so you can look squarely down at it. Turn your body until the north end of the compass needle sits inside the orienting arrow. The direction of travel arrow will now point exactly in the direction you need to walk.
parents.page_47: You may need to take a bearing to a specific point to navigate toward it, even if you can see the feature initially but might lose sight of it later due to terrain. You can also plot bearings on a map to determine your own position. To transfer a compass bearing from a feature onto a map, first adjust for magnetic variation. For example, if the magnetic variation is 12 degrees west and your magnetic bearing is 45 degrees, subtract the variation to get a grid bearing of 33 degrees. Dial this revised bearing into your compass. When taking a bearing on a feature, point your compass at the feature, hold the base plate steady, and rotate the housing until the orienting arrow sits directly under the north needle; this gives you the magnetic bearing. To transfer the bearing onto a map, place the top left corner of the compass base plate over the feature on the map, ensure both the map and compass are level, then rotate the entire compass until the orienting lines are parallel with the vertical grid lines on the map. Finally, draw a line from the feature along the left side of the base plate to mark your bearing on the map.
parents.page_48: If you are unsure of your location but can recognize features on the ground that appear on your map, use a compass to take bearings on these features and transfer them onto the map to determine your position accurately; this method is known as triangulation or resection in military terminology. If you are not at a known feature, identify at least two recognizable features on the ground that are at least 0.6 mile (1 km) apart and separated by an angle of at least 40 degrees. Take compass bearings to these features, convert them into back bearings, and draw lines from the corresponding points on your map extending them until they cross; the intersection point indicates your exact location. For greater accuracy, repeat this process using a third feature. If you are already located on or near a known feature such as a river, road, or track, take a bearing on another recognizable feature like a church and mark it on the map; the point where this line crosses your known feature reveals your specific position.
parents.page_49: There are several methods for calculating distance while hiking, and experienced hikers typically use at least two simultaneously. Pace counting involves knowing the number of paces required to cover a specific distance and then counting them during travel. You can mark progress by cutting notches in a walking stick or moving a rubber band down a notch every 100 meters, or by placing pebbles in a pocket for every 100 meters traveled; most people take about 60 paces or 120 steps per 100 meters. Alternatively, use your map to identify key features along your route and calculate the distance between them. As you pass these features, mentally check them off or mark your progress on the map to track total distance traveled. To estimate hike duration using Naismith's rule, allow one hour for every three miles or five kilometers of travel. Add 30 minutes for every 1,000 feet or 300 meters of climbing. Subtract 10 minutes for every 1,000 feet or 300 meters of descending, but add 10 minutes instead if the slopes are very steep. Break your route down into small sections for focused navigation and ensure each section includes a water source and a safety point.
parents.page_50: If a straight-line path is blocked by an obstacle, use a compass to calculate four ninety-degree turns to walk around it. Count your paces during the first and third detours so you can return to your original route. When aiming for a specific destination like a footbridge, deliberately aim off to one side rather than directly at it; this ensures you know which direction to turn once you reach the target area. To conserve energy while crossing hilly terrain, walk along contours by maintaining the same height around features instead of climbing up and down repeatedly. Follow long linear features such as rivers or roads that run in your general direction of travel, as these simplify navigation. When navigating to a difficult-to-locate point, aim for a nearby prominent feature first, calculate the distance and bearing from it, and use pacing to reach the exact destination.
parents.page_51: If you lack a compass, you can improvise one using magnetized ferrous metal such as a needle, razor blade, straightened paperclip, small nail, or staple; thinner materials are easier to magnetize. To magnetize the item, stroke it repeatedly along its length in one direction with a magnet, ensuring the end that attracts to the south pole of the magnet will point north. Once magnetized, the needle must be able to turn freely without interference from wind or other elements. You can suspend the magnetized blade by attaching it to a cotton thread and placing it inside a plastic bottle, removing the bottle base if the neck is too narrow for the blade. Alternatively, allow the needle to float freely in water while protecting it from external disturbances. Always carry a magnet with you but keep it away from your actual compass to prevent accuracy issues. Essential tools for this process include a knife, a magnet, or a battery with wire and tape.
parents.page_52: To magnetize a needle using electricity, wrap it in a small piece of paper to insulate it from electrical current. Wrap insulated wire tightly around the full length of the needle and attach it to a battery with at least 1.5 volts until the battery becomes warm, indicating the process is complete. Ensure no loose wire touches the needle and do not touch the wire once it is connected to the battery. Alternatively, you can use brass snare wire insulated with non-conductive material like paper. For the floating method, place the magnetized needle on a small dry leaf, piece of paper, bark, grass blade, or inside a shortened straw in a sheltered body of water such as a puddle or a non-magnetic container. The needle will rotate and align itself; allow it to float freely to determine which end points north using natural aids like the sun. Mark the north end with a pen or a small scratch. To verify sufficient magnetization, hold the needle against another metal object; if it is attracted and can hold itself against the metal, it is strong enough. Generally, the longer you work on magnetizing the needle, the stronger and more long-lasting the magnetization will be.
parents.page_53: Using the sun as a natural signpost, you can determine the four cardinal directions and approximate time. The sun rises in the east and sets in the west. At noon, the sun is due south in the northern hemisphere and due north in the southern hemisphere. To find direction using a shadow stick, drive a stick upright into level ground. Place a stone at the tip of the shadow. Wait three hours and place a second stone at the new position of the shadow's tip. Draw a line between the two stones to establish an east-west line; the first stone marks due west and the second marks due east. Mark a line perpendicular to the east-west line to find the north-south direction. In the northern hemisphere, the shadow falls north of the east-west line, while in the southern hemisphere, it falls south. The shortest shadow indicates noon. This method works between the Arctic Circle at 66.5 degrees North and the Antarctic Circle at 66.5 degrees South. A stick approximately three feet high is recommended for this technique.
parents.page_54: Once you have established the east-west and north-south lines, place a stick at their intersection to create a sundial. Tie a cord to this central stick and attach a smaller stick to the other end of the cord. Use the smaller stick to draw a 180-degree arc between two marker stones on the ground. Divide this arc into 12 equal sections and mark each division with a notch, where each notch represents one hour from 6am to 6pm. The central notch indicates noon, while the position of the shadow cast by the stick shows the approximate time; for example, if the shadow aligns with a specific notch, it indicates that time, such as roughly 5pm.
parents.page_55: To determine direction using an analog watch in the Southern Hemisphere, point the 12 o'clock marker at the Sun and bisect the angle between that marker and the hour hand; this line indicates due north. In the Northern Hemisphere, point the hour hand at the Sun and bisect the angle between it and the 12 o'clock marker to find due south. Ensure your watch is set to the correct local time and accounts for daylight savings time before using this method. If you lack a physical watch but know the time, draw a watch face on paper with marked hour and minute hands to simulate the tool. Note that this technique becomes less effective as you approach the equator. You can also study natural signs for orientation if you know the predominant wind direction of an area. Windswept trees typically point away from the wind, while their growth is fullest on sides facing the Sun. Moss and lichen grow in shaded areas. Some plants, such as barrel cacti, twist toward the Sun as they grow. In very windy areas, small animals and birds tend to nest or burrow on the lee-side of hills, away from the wind. Spiders spin their webs out of the wind. In powder-snow conditions, snow dunes often form parallel to the prevailing wind, and frost erosion is most severe on slopes facing the Sun.
parents.page_56: To navigate using stars in the northern hemisphere, locate the Big Dipper and draw a line from the two front stars at the top of the dipper. Extend this line outward until you reach Polaris, which is situated approximately four times the distance between those two stars away from the Big Dipper; Polaris marks due north on the horizon. In the southern hemisphere, find the Southern Cross and project a line along its longer axis toward a dark area of sky. Then, draw a second line at a 90-degree angle from the midpoint between the two bright stars in the Centaurus constellation. The point where these two lines intersect indicates due south, with the horizon below that intersection marking your southern direction. Alternatively, you can orient yourself by noting that the crescent moon provides directional clues: drawing a line between its two horns points roughly south in the northern hemisphere and roughly north in the southern hemisphere. Since the moon reflects sunlight, it rises in the east and sets in the west, making it useful for orientation on clear nights, particularly during a full moon when using a shadow stick method.
parents.page_57: Hiking is an effective method for exploring wilderness terrain, but it requires a decent level of fitness and appropriate clothing and equipment. To ensure safety and efficiency, you must master basic hiking skills. When ascending hills, lean forward slightly while maintaining momentum, shorten your stride, keep your feet flat on the ground as you push upward, and move your legs forward from the hips. Swing your arms to aid in balance and generate momentum. Always carry a walking stick for support during climbs. Ensure your backpack fits properly for comfort and that its deep tread grips the ground to reduce slipping. Aim for a slow, even pace that all group members can maintain throughout the hike by developing a consistent hiking rhythm. Take regular rest breaks and ensure everyone in the group knows the route. Additionally, break in new boots before undertaking long hikes to prevent blisters.
parents.page_58: Negotiating difficult ground such as scree requires specific techniques to advance confidently and safely. Scree is a mass of small rocks that slides underfoot, making travel uphill and downhill challenging with a slippery surface that slows progress. When traversing scree, choose a zigzag route and look for paths containing similar-sized rocks. Walk sideways across the slope, taking small steps and testing rocks for stability before placing your full weight on them. Walking downhill places significant strain on your thighs, knees, and ankles, especially when carrying a heavy backpack. To avoid losing control or gathering too much speed, use your arms for balance and maintain a steady rhythm. Unless you are in the desert where it is cooler to move in darkness, avoid walking at night due to nocturnal predators and navigational difficulties. If walking at night is unavoidable, use a flashlight or improvise a torch by setting alight birch bark or similar material. If lighting is not possible and time permits, close your eyes for 20 minutes to allow your eyes to adjust to night vision. Use a walking stick to feel for obstacles, tripping hazards, or sudden drops. Keep your pace slow and deliberate and check your compass regularly.
parents.page_59: River crossings are dangerous and should only be attempted when absolutely necessary, as getting cold and wet can lead to hypothermia, making it difficult to recover warmth and dryness. Always consult your map to find routes around the river and select the safest available crossing point. Trees on the opposite bank can provide shelter from wind once you exit the water. Avoid crossing downstream of debris or fallen trees. Lean on a pole while moving your feet for stability. Unusual variations in water flow may indicate submerged rocks, and exposed rocks can be slippery. Do not cross on the outside of river bends where water flows faster; instead, look for shallow banks to make entering and exiting easier. Walking upstream generally leads to shallower water, but remember that even shallow water can have strong currents, as can water that appears calm on the surface. Always check for bridges farther up or downstream. Before entering the water, ensure you have a change of clothes or a way to get dry on the other side. In cold conditions, collect everything needed to start a fire, such as tinder, kindling, and dry wood, and keep these items dry while crossing. Wear footwear when wading to protect your feet from rocks and other hazards. Remove pants to keep them dry and decrease resistance in the water. Use a walking stick for added support.
parents.page_60: To cross a river in a huddle, position the strongest person upstream to bear the main force of the current while others provide stability and support. Link your arms tightly together and take short, deliberate steps across the water. When crossing in a line, maintain balance by moving perpendicular to the current, proceeding slowly and placing each step carefully to avoid being swept away. Wading with a group is safer than crossing alone; link arms to create a stable structure that offers backup if someone falls. Loosen backpack straps and pass only one arm through them so you can release the pack quickly in an emergency. Arrange the group so the person bearing the main force of the current takes the lead, the lightest person crosses in the middle, and the strongest person handles the water's force. Place arms on each other's shoulders for added stability. Never cross white water or flooded rivers, as it is safer to avoid crossing entirely than to risk injury. Use a walking stick to assess changes in the depth of the riverbed before proceeding.
parents.page_61: Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location. If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger. You must decide between staying put or moving based on specific conditions: move only if you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons to remain; otherwise, stay. If you have a cell or satellite phone, contact someone immediately to report your predicament. If an emergency rescue is required and you possess a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), activate it as an option. Address the core principles of survival in this order: protection, location, water, and food. In mountain environments, find or improvise shelter immediately to protect yourself from elements, avoid confrontation with animals, stabilize any injuries by applying first aid, and get out of dangerous situations quickly.
parents.page_62: Do not allow your extremities to get too cold as these areas are most susceptible to frostbite. Overlook the dangers of carbon monoxide poisoning in cramped shelter; do not let candles, cookers, or fires burn all night. Breathe on your hands to warm them because you are exhaling warm air that you will need to replace with cold air. Select a shelter site that offers protection from the elements. Fill plastic bags or spare clothing with dry foliage and use them as a mattress or pillow for insulation from the cold, damp ground. Deploy all your aids to location and prepare for immediate use. Check upstream for the quality of your water source. Light a fire and take turns tending it if in a group to keep it going all night. Continually reassess your situation and adapt your actions accordingly. Be alert for signs of rescue. Descend hills carefully by zigzagging across them to put less strain on leg muscles. Travel at a fast pace because high altitude means less air and places greater demands on even a fit person. Do not sweat too much because the moisture will chill you further. Ignore opportunities to collect dry tinder, kindling, and fuel only if necessary. Keep all clothing dry and clean. Collect drinking water from fast-moving streams and purify it if you have the means. Watch out for signs of cold-related injuries such as frost nip, frostbite, and hypothermia. Regulate your clothing to avoid overheating when moving and hypothermia when static. Use a walking stick to aid safe movement. Watch the weather closely and be prepared to change your plans quickly because mountain climates are highly unpredictable. If you cannot survive where you are but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything you can to attract rescue. If your situation changes, consult the alternative instructions for moving versus staying.
parents.page_63: When hiking over frozen terrain, physical fitness is essential because the conditions result in slow and exhausting progress. You must have the right equipment and know how to use it effectively. Wearing snow shoes or skis along with breathable, layered clothing is mandatory. Ski poles can be used to test the snow ahead of you. Snow shoes prevent sinking into the snow, while waterproof pants or gaiters keep your legs dry. The ability of a shoe to pivot reduces drag and improves maneuverability. Wear a backpack to keep both arms free for balance and movement. A ski mask protects your eyes from glare. Snow shoes and skis work by spreading your body weight over a larger surface area, allowing you to walk on the surface of the snow rather than sinking into it. Always set off when it is cold because you will warm up quickly; add or remove layers as required. Walking over snow without snow shoes is dangerous because sinking into the snow leaves you exhausted and wet, which in cold conditions can quickly lead to hypothermia, particularly when you stop and are no longer generating heat.
parents.page_64: If you lack pre-manufactured snow shoes, you can construct simple ones using a knife, green wood, and cordage to move more effectively over snow. Begin by cutting five lengths of green wood that are as thick as your thumb and as long as the distance from your foot to your armpit. Next, cut three shorter pieces to serve as cross-pieces. Lash the ends of the five longer pieces together securely with cordage. Determine where the ball of your foot will sit on the shoe and lash the first cross-piece across the five lengths at that position, ensuring the shoe balances. Securely lash the five loose lengths of wood together at the back of the shoe. Fix the second cross-piece roughly two inches behind the first one. Lash the third cross-piece where your heel will rest. Repeat these steps to create a second shoe before moving on to assembly. Place your foot on the snow shoe so that the ball of your foot sits directly over the front cross-piece and your heel rests on the back cross-piece. Tie your boot to the snow shoe using available cordage, but ensure the heel remains free to pivot for proper movement.
parents.page_65: To move over snow without gaiters, tie plastic bags around the bottom of your legs to keep them dry. For short distances or to reach a road, attach branches to your feet using cordage to spread your weight and lift you out of deep snow. Select a tree like pine with strong, close branches. Choose saplings that have some flexibility; heating them over a fire makes them easier to bend. Gently bend the longest branch into a teardrop shape and lash the two ends together. You can also add cross-pieces and twigs or cordage to create a base for your feet. Tie the front of your boot to a bough as an alternative method. Ensure the back of your foot can lift slightly when walking, and use cross-pieces to strengthen your shoe.
parents.page_66: Using a pulk is an efficient method for carrying equipment or a person over snow by pulling it behind you with human power. Pulks are small, low-slung toboggans typically made of lightweight plastic and come in various sizes. To use one effectively, attach it to your body using a harness. Wear breathable clothing to prevent overheating and ensure you know how to release your harness quickly in an emergency. When traveling downhill in a group, designate one person to be harnessed to the rear of the pulk to act as a brakeman. Pulks pick up speed easily, so exercise caution when descending slopes. When ascending slopes, kick firmly into the slope and test your weight before moving upward. For descending, you can ski without skis, known as boot skiing. Always test the snow ahead with a walking stick. If you encounter melted depressions called sun cups, cross on the rims to avoid sinking. In deep snow, walk in single file within a group and take turns leading, as the front position is the most strenuous because it creates the path. Avoid rocks in spring conditions since they absorb heat and make the snow above unstable. To navigate ice safely, use a walking stick to test the surface, especially over rivers and lakes. If traveling in a group on ice, rope yourselves together for safety. Wear crampons for extra grip and use an ice ax to cut steps on steep slopes or to halt a fall. Never cross glaciers without a guide, as they require specialized skills.
parents.page_67: Four-wheel-drive vehicles allow you to manually switch between two-wheel-drive for paved roads and four-wheel-drive for low-traction conditions, with the key difference being the number of powered wheels. When driving over rough terrain, avoid hooking your thumbs around the steering wheel because hitting a rut can jerk the wheel and cause injury. Lower your tire pressure slightly before tackling difficult terrain. In four-wheel-drive systems, all four wheels are powered by the engine simultaneously; gasoline engines typically offer more power while diesel engines provide better durability at low speeds. The primary purpose of four-wheel-drive is to reduce the likelihood of getting stuck rather than to extend travel distance until you become stuck. If unsure about conditions ahead, walk the route first to identify potential problems and options for self-recovery or escape, then mark the path with visible markers to follow while driving. Use four-wheel-drive only on rough terrain and avoid it on hard roads to prevent damaging tires and gears, as this mode consumes significant fuel. On soft sand, tires tend to push sand from the front to the rear of the tire; if movement stops completely, the wheel can dig itself into a hole. To prevent this, steer continuously from side to side so the tire exits its own ruts and avoid rapid changes in speed. Driving off-road over rough terrain is mentally and physically demanding, so take regular breaks and share driving duties if traveling in a group. Always have at least two people in the vehicle: a driver and a spotter responsible for navigation. Ensure the vehicle is fit for the journey and carry spare water, wheels, fuel, survival equipment, sand ladders, a 12-volt heavy-duty tire inflator, winching straps, and a first aid kit. On soft surfaces, lowering tire pressure improves traction; to find the correct pressure, place a brick half an inch from the side of a rear tire on flat ground, deflate the tire until it touches the brick, measure that pressure, and apply it to all four tires while ensuring you can reinflate them afterward.
parents.page_68: Driving on deep mud requires concentration and adaptability; use wide tires or lower tire pressure in deep mud, but avoid driving over hard surfaces beneath the mud as this worsens traction. Steering outside existing track ruts usually provides the best grip. When crossing water, always walk your route first to assess depth and current speed. Do not cross if the water is too deep or the current is too fast. Maintain a correct driving speed: going too fast splashes water everywhere, while driving too slowly may flood the engine bay. Driving on snow and ice demands very smooth methods; apply gradual pressure to the accelerator and brakes to prevent wheel spin. Use low gears, particularly when descending hills. Practice fitting snow chains before they are needed in emergency situations. Ensure vehicle drain holes are fitted if applicable and keep the water level below the top of the wheels.
parents.page_69: If you get stuck, avoid aggressive attempts to free your vehicle as this may dig you into a deeper rut. Instead, calmly evaluate options such as reversing, pushing, digging, using traction aids, or winching. Ideally, travel with at least two vehicles so one can assist with winching, handling breakdowns, or driving for help. If four-wheel drive fails to free the vehicle, alternate between reversing and driving forward in first gear. If this does not work, have passengers exit the vehicle while you drive forward as they push. If pushing and reversing are ineffective, dig down in front of each tire to create an upward slope that allows gentle acceleration out of the rut; avoid revving the engine during this process. When the vehicle still will not move, place branches, wooden planks, sand ladders, or blankets in front of the wheels to increase traction. Gently ease the vehicle forward onto these materials at a slow, steady speed without excessive engine revving. Essential recovery equipment includes a winching mechanism with strap, cable or tow rope, an inflated spare tire, shovel and saw, sleeping bag or blanket, branches or stakes, and sand ladders or mats.
parents.page_70: To winch a vehicle out of a hole using a stake, attach the cable fairly low down on the main stake and push supporting stakes into the ground at a slight angle, lashed together for added strength. You can use a natural hollow to save energy when digging a hole for an anchor. Place a large log or rock to provide an angle for the winch. Cover the winch cable with a blanket to reduce whiplash if it breaks and secure the anchor further by refilling the hole. When winching to another vehicle in a convoy, be aware that both vehicles risk getting stuck. Use natural anchor points such as trees, large rocks, roots, or deadfalls whenever possible. If using a tree, place the strap or cable near the ground and use a winch strap to prevent damaging the tree; if one tree cannot provide enough support, tie it to others nearby. For buried anchor points, dig a hole at least 3 feet deep, attach your winch cable to an object like a log or spare wheel, bury the anchor in the hole, and refill the hole to secure it further. If using a tire as an anchor, use the lever behind the spare wheel as an attachment point for the cable. Never stand within range of the winch cable while operating it, as a breaking cable can cut a tree or a person in half. Always place a blanket or sleeping bag over the cable to mitigate injury from snapping cables.
parents.page_71: To create a survival poncho float, gather a large waterproof material such as a poncho or any other suitable sheet. If you have two ponchos, place the first one face down on the ground and lay your equipment kit in the center. Fold the four corners of the poncho inward over the pile to form a rectangular package that prevents water from entering. Secure the edges tightly using ropes, bootlaces, vines, or any available cordage. For added buoyancy, fill the float with brushwood before sealing it completely. If you have a second poncho, repeat the process by placing the first sealed float face down on the second sheet and securing it again. Before entering the water, push your poncho hood to the inside and tighten the neck drawstrings or cordage to ensure the package remains watertight. Gently lower yourself into the water with the float attached and pull it along as you cross the river obstacle. This improvised raft keeps your belongings dry and provides essential flotation when a major water barrier lies between you and rescue.
parents.page_72: To build an effective raft, use naturally buoyant materials such as logs, bamboo, or discarded oil drums. Logs should be dry standing deadwood for better flotation and must be of the same diameter. Bamboo rafts are lighter due to their hollow, compartmentalized sections. Oil drums are ideal but require caution as they may contain toxins. Most improvised rafts will float half-submerged, so construct an extra raised platform. For a raft made of logs, cut notches into them so cross-pieces can sit snugly above the water level. To create a paddle, select a suitable length of green wood that is wide but comfortable to grip. Make a split at one end of the handle and insert smaller branches into it, lashing each branch until you have a sufficient paddle area. Lash the ends of the handle together to make the paddle more rigid. The length of the split determines the size of the paddle face. Trim the edges with a saw to make them even. If there is no river current, you will need to improvise a paddle for propulsion; otherwise, you can use the current and a walking stick to maneuver the raft.
parents.page_75: Whether you plan to wait for rescue in place, move to a safer location, or walk out of the situation yourself, selecting an appropriate site for your shelter is essential. This decision applies whether you need accommodation for a single night or a longer-term stay. A solid understanding of good location principles ensures survival actions are safe and effective. Organizing your campsite creates a sense of purpose and order while providing a secure environment for both yourself and your equipment. Designating specific areas for storing tools and equipment prevents loss of vital items and reduces the risk of injury to you or your team members. Fire is an integral component of any campsite, serving multiple critical functions including warmth, water purification, cooking, signaling, protection from wild animals, and providing light after dark. It also offers a profound sense of security; the psychological impact of successfully starting a fire should never be underestimated, nor should the negative effects of failing to do so. Even in a survival scenario, establishing a simple camp can restore a sense of normality and home. Continuously learning wilderness skills is an ongoing process. Mastering the correct method for using a knife improves safety and conserves valuable energy by ensuring tasks are completed efficiently. Similarly, knowing how to tie simple knots allows you to utilize available cordage in the most effective manner possible. The more knowledge you possess about how things work, the greater your ability to improvise solutions when resources are scarce, which can be the difference between continued survival and despair.
parents.page_76: On any expedition, carry equipment such as a basic bivouac shelter or tarpaulin to form protection from the elements if needed. A space blanket is an essential piece of gear that should be packed and carried at all times even if nothing else is included. Space blankets pack down very small and typically feature one silver side to reflect heat and one bright orange side to aid location. They provide immediate protection from the elements in any environment and can be converted into a basic shelter. To produce a spark with a firesteel, use a rod made from ferrocerium or magnesium alloy paired with a sharp striker device such as a knife blade or short piece of hacksaw blade. When the striker is drawn over the rod, a spark is produced. To control the spark and direct it accurately to reduce scattering of tinder, place the rod in the center of the tinder. Position the striker onto the rod and lock your hand in position. Pull the rod up and away from the tinder while drawing it against the striker. To direct the sparks, alter the angle at which you pull the rod up and away.
parents.page_77: Always choose a campsite based on the four principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food. Ensure there are no obvious dangers and that you can signal for rescue. Assess your environment for potential threats such as animals, unstable rocks or trees, and flooding risks. Look for signs of animals, especially near water sources, and try to pitch your camp against a rock face so it can only be approached from one direction. Position the entrance to your shelter at an angle to the wind. Avoid gullies due to the risk of flash floods or avalanches. Do not camp near standing water where insects like mosquitoes swarm. Place your latrine downwind of the camp and downstream from any water source. If predators are present, hang unused food 10 feet (3 meters) off the ground and 3 feet (1 meter) away from tree trunks or branches. Keep noise-making items on hand to scare off prowling predators. In case of an encounter with dangerous wild animals, stay calm as most will avoid confrontation unless provoked, cornered, or surprised, particularly when protecting young. If you see a bear, make yourself look as big as possible by raising your arms and walk slowly backward without running. If the bear follows, hold your ground; if it attacks, play dead or fight back depending on the situation. Organize your site by designating specific areas for storing equipment and firewood, cooking, and sleeping. Establish routines for activities to ensure camp safety and reduce the risk of accidents.
parents.page_78: Avoid camping under standing deadwood, as these trees have not yet fallen but may collapse due to heavy wind or the weight of rain and snow; they are excellent sources for kindling and fire fuel. Steer clear of campsite locations on outside river bends, which are prone to erosion and flooding, especially if a river bursts its banks during heavy downpours. Be cautious of deadfalls, which are dangerous branches that have broken off trees like beech, ash, and yew without warning. When camping near rocks, inspect for cracks because fires below them can trigger rockfalls, and in cold conditions, watch out for sudden ice falls. Pitch your camp near a source of wood and designate an open area to serve as a location aid and landing site for rescue helicopters. Dig a small runoff trench around your shelter to reduce flooding risks during heavy rain and divert water away downhill. Build three signal fires on open ground and always locate a nearby water source, checking the upstream area for contamination before use.
parents.page_79: Caves offer ready-made shelters that are typically dry and secure, but they present specific dangers including animals, poor air quality, and water risks. Never venture deeper into a cave than you can clearly see to avoid hidden drops or slippery surfaces, and strictly avoid old, disused mines due to the risk of collapse. To maintain body heat, cover the floor with bedding made from the driest available materials such as feathers, pine boughs, spruce boughs, dry leaves, moss, bracken, or grasses; collect twice as much bedding as you think necessary and surround it with logs to prevent spreading. Be aware that caves harbor bears, bats, insects, spiders, and snakes, noting that vampire bats can carry rabies and bat droppings are highly combustible. When considering coastal caves, watch for signs of flooding during high tide such as lines of seaweed, driftwood, or debris, damp smells, and rock pools. If you experience light-headedness, nausea, an increased pulse, or rapid breathing, leave the cave immediately; additionally, if a flame dims or turns blue, it indicates a lack of oxygen and requires immediate evacuation. When building a fire inside a cave, place it at the rear rather than the front to prevent smoke from blowing back into your exit path, ensure adequate airflow, use fuel that produces minimal smoke, and secure side logs with wooden pegs while utilizing rocks to retain heat.
parents.page_80: To construct a corded A-frame shelter using a poncho, blanket, sheet, or tarpaulin, locate two trees spaced approximately two feet farther apart than the length of your material. Attach a cord to one tree three feet off the ground using a double chain fastening knot. Secure the other end of that cord to the second tree with a taut line hitch, adjusting it until tight. Peg the middle loop on each side and pull the material taut. Peg out the corner loops while pulling them tight.
