Place long boughs along the sides of the shelter and shorter boughs at each end to retain bedding material. Attach one-foot loops to the grommets on the longer sides and three-foot loops to the end ones. Tie a knot around the hood to seal it and prevent water from pooling, then raise the hood for protection.
parents.page_81: A natural hollow in the ground can be converted into a trench shelter that protects you from the wind if you lie inside it. If the hollow is too shallow to lie in, remove some soil using a digging stick. Be wary of wet soil as it could be prone to flooding. If the ground is too hard to dig, build up the sides with logs. Lay bedding material on the ground inside the shelter. Use a saw to cut several sturdy poles or branches long enough to place across the hollow to create a supporting roof. Place a thicker, longer log on top of the cross poles for a pitched roof. Add shorter branches, sticks, or poles on the log and cross them to complete the pitched roof structure. Pack all materials tightly together. Insulate the pitched roof with lots of foliage, starting the final layer at the ground and working upward. Leave an entrance at one end oriented 90 degrees to the wind. Useful tools include a sturdy digging stick with a pointed end, strong branches and logs, and a saw such as a pocket chainsaw. Suitable materials for the roof include tree bark, turf, leaves, forest mulch, moss, bracken, and pine boughs.
parents.page_82: To construct a Forest A-frame shelter, select a ridgepole and smooth one side with a knife while leaving the other side bare with branch stubs. Hammer two poles into the ground to form an "A" shape, resting one end of the ridgepole on top of this frame and the other end on the ground. Weave alternating rows of saplings through the poles to create a framework for the covering, ensuring you leave an entrance near the front. Cover the framework with a thick layer of natural materials such as pine boughs, branches, and twigs to form a thatch. Position additional poles against the stubs on the ridgepole, decreasing in height as you work from the front "A" toward the back ground level. At each junction, rest the side poles against the stubs or tie them securely to the ridgepole using an arbor knot. Cover the first layer with leaves, moss, and mulch, starting from the ground and working up to the ridgepole. Ensure the ridgepole is at least 3 feet longer than you are for proper coverage. Build a fire reflector at the entrance of the shelter. Use tools such as a knife, saw or ax, cordage, saplings, poles of various lengths, and covering materials like pine boughs, leaves, and moss to complete the structure.
parents.page_83: To build a forest lean-to, select a flat area between two trees or vertical supports firmly secured in the ground. If constructing for a group, determine the required width by having everyone lie down next to each other and adding six inches per person. Position the ridgepole against the trees at your desired height and lash it securely using an arbor knot. Place a support pole beneath the ridgepole and lash it to the trees, then peg a log where you want the foot of the shelter to create space for your feet. Drive five roof poles evenly along the ridgepole so they slope back toward the foot of the lean-to. Keep the outside poles between the trees to prevent them from moving outward, lash each roof pole to the ridgepole, and hammer side stakes alongside the outside poles to form walls. Gather necessary tools including a saw, knife, cordage, saplings, foliage for covering, poles, and stakes. Ensure the ridgepole is longer than the distance between the trees, use pegs to secure the log position in the ground, and make sure roof poles are at least as long as the shelter needs to be.
parents.page_84: To construct a forest lean-to shelter, weave saplings in and out through the roof poles starting from the ridgepole, alternating the weave pattern between horizontal and diagonal for each row. Before beginning construction, gather all necessary materials while there is still daylight. Ensure your location aids, such as signal fires or flares, are visible or can be activated quickly. Wear gloves when clearing leaves and debris to protect against spiders and snakes. Layer down clothing during work to prevent overheating. Make the shelter as waterproof as possible by applying large materials first to the roof and walls. Ensure the bedding raises you at least four inches off the ground to stay dry. Establish a watch system to maintain and control the fire.
parents.page_85: An A-frame shelter is relatively easy to construct using a modified poncho, tarpaulin, shelter sheet, or groundsheet as the roof and sleeping surface. Repeat this process to create a second "A" frame at the opposite end, ensuring both frames are aligned in a straight line. Place a ridgepole across the top of the two "A" frames and tie it securely to both junction points for added stability. Finally, wedge the legs of the frame into the ground and secure all junctions with cordage and pegs.
parents.page_86: Place a tarp or sheet over the ridgepole and secure it by tying a guyline to each corner, running the line to a peg or tree, and fastening it with a taut line hitch. Clear the ground around your shelter using a makeshift brush rather than bare hands to avoid bites from snakes or spiders. Construct your shelter high enough off the ground to deter insects and animals, especially those active at night. Prevent dehydration and heat-related injuries like heat stress or heat stroke by drinking water frequently, taking regular breaks in the shade, and working at a moderate pace. Inside the shelter, use a full mosquito net along with a head-net. Build a fire to keep insects and animals away. parents.page_87: Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or your location, whether you are in danger, and if you have any means of communication. If you are missed, a rescue party will almost certainly be dispatched to find you. To get yourself out of immediate danger, slow down your pace regarding elements, find or improvise immediate shelter, avoid confrontation with animals, stabilize any injuries, and apply first aid. Address the core principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food.
parents.page_88: Do not let the oppressive nature of the jungle overwhelm you; instead, slow your pace to match the environment. parents.page_89: To improvise immediate desert shelter from the sun, use a shelter sheet, tarpaulin, poncho, or space blanket. Avoid building your shelter during the hottest part of the day and ration your sweat rather than your water; if you start to sweat, take a break. Avoid low-lying areas, dry riverbeds, and wadis due to the risk of flash floods, and avoid the tops of large hills because of lightning and extreme winds. Build your shelter on a small rise where it can be warmer at night. parents.page_90: To construct a desert shelter, first unpack your essentials before setting up camp. If you cannot find a natural hollow, erect a poncho shelter in a location that remains cool during the day, such as under trees or bushes, or at the top of a slight rise to catch a breeze. parents.page_91: To build a quinzhee, find a relatively flat area covered with snow and mark out a circle for the shelter with walls about 10 inches thick, stamping down the snow to compact it. Gather soft snow using a mess can, pan, snow shoe, or similar item. Pile snow on the core to form a dome of the required height, building up layers until you have a covering of at least 10 inches while smoothing out each layer. Smooth the entire surface of the snow dome and leave it to sinter or harden for one to three hours. To maintain the right temperature inside, remove layers of clothing as you work, keeping only base layers with a waterproof layer on top, and ensure all clothing stays dry so it can be worn when you stop working. parents.page_92: To build a quinzhee, mark guide-sticks at 12 to 18 inches before inserting them into the snow. Push sticks of equal length through the snow toward the center of the dome to ensure an even thickness in the roof and walls. Construct a small compact mound in front of the dome, then burrow into it to remove your bag and tarp while excavating snow from the core. Use the guide-sticks to maintain wall thickness of at least 10 inches. Smooth the interior snow surface to prevent drips. Build a raised sleeping platform to keep cold air away from your sleeping area. Create a ventilation hole one foot up from the ground and another at the top of the dome. For general snow shelter construction, remember that fresh uncompacted snow is an excellent insulator because it traps 90 to 95 percent air. Always check your site for hazards like snowdrifts, freezing winds, avalanches, cornice collapse, and large animals. Keep tools inside the shelter in case you need to dig out, brush snow off equipment and clothing before entering, and tie all vital gear to yourself to prevent loss in deep snow. Prevent carbon monoxide poisoning by creating holes 3 to 4 inches in diameter at both the base and top of your shelter to ensure free airflow. Carbon monoxide is an odorless gas produced when insufficient oxygen allows fuel to burn improperly; it can be fatal in well-insulated, non-ventilated environments. Signs of poisoning include fatigue, faintness, flu-like symptoms, severe headaches, nausea, and decreased mental coordination, which worsen over time. If poisoning occurs, get into fresh air immediately and breathe it for at least four hours to reduce carbon monoxide levels in your system by half.
parents.page_93: The ability to make and maintain a fire is a significant psychological factor in survival; those who can do so are more likely to succeed than those who give up. Always carry some form of firelighter and practice your skills under different conditions with various materials. Collect tinder as soon as you enter the forest, and if it is wet, dry it in your pockets before use. Gather more than enough quantities of all necessary materials to ensure success. When selecting a location, clear the ground in advance and never light a fire directly on bare earth. Use a green wood platform to protect the fire from ground moisture and limit heat dissipation into the soil. Position wet tinder, kindling, and larger pieces of wood close to the main fire to help them dry out. Build the fire away from your shelter to prevent sparks from blowing in and igniting it. Contain the fire using green wood or dry, nonporous rocks that will not explode when heated. Use a container of water, sand, or soil to fully extinguish the fire before leaving the site. You can hang a pot from a hook for cooking and use a fire reflector to increase heat efficiency.
parents.page_94: Heat is required to ignite fuel and can be generated through sparks, chemical reactions, or friction. Once a fire starts, you must add fuel by beginning with small, dry pieces that generate enough heat to burn increasingly larger pieces. To build a fire reflector for a shelter, check that airflow crosses the reflector properly. Hammer two poles into the ground spaced one pole-width apart and add a second set of poles. Tie the tops of the upright poles together to form a wall as long as the shelter's entrance to retain heat and block wind and rain. Construct the reflector so you can build a fire about three feet from the shelter entrance, using an L-shaped end to retain more heat. Stack poles to reach the required height. A matchless fireset can offers a one-stop solution for starting fires in any weather; it contains a sparking device, tinder, kindling, and fuel. Hexamine is scraped onto cotton, which is then ignited by a flint and steel spark long enough for fuel blocks to catch fire. Keep a plastic Ziploc bag with electrical tape on the can to keep it waterproof and use the taped can as kindling. Store dry tinder like cotton and tampons in a separate plastic bag. Use a quarter of a hexamine fuel block to light the fire. Oxygen, heat, and fuel are crucial for a strong fire, and achieving the best balance between them is key. If you smother the fire with too much wood, oxygen may not reach the flame. If the fire is dying, fan it with paper or your hand to create a draft that feeds oxygen to it. Use green wood so heat does not set the reflector alight.
parents.page_95: To build a fire, you need three dry material elements: tinder, kindling, and fuel, collected in sufficient quantities. Tinder is the first element used to start a fire and can come from your equipment or natural and man-made sources. Experiment with available materials before they are needed and ensure all tinder is dry by leaving damp items in the sun. Natural tinder sources include feather sticks, bark, bamboo shavings, plant and animal down, fine wood dust, pine pitch, dead moss, and dry grass. Man-made sources include cotton balls, lint, tampons, tissue paper, camera film, rubber, and candle wick. To prepare tinder for a spark or coal, create a tinder bundle by vigorously teasing, rubbing, and pulling the fibers until they form a grapefruit-sized ball. Push the finest combustible material into the interior of the bundle and mix it thoroughly. Carry items like cotton balls or tampons in a 35mm film canister or Ziploc bag, using some for each fire. Cotton balls smeared with petroleum jelly help make flames last longer. Kindling is added to burning tinder once it is dry and ranges from the thickness of a match to a finger. If kindling is damp, remove the outer bark and break it into six-inch sticks. Soft-wood twigs are highly combustible, while wood with flammable resin burns hot and for a long duration. Some tinder types like bark, palm leaves, pine needles, and grass can serve as kindling but require larger quantities. Fuel should be added once the fire sustains itself for five minutes after initial tending to create a bed of hot coals known as a good heart. Fuel pieces should be as thick as your wrist or forearm. Hard woods from deciduous trees like oak and maple produce good coals, while soft woods from conifers like pine and fir are easier to light but generate less heat. Additional fuel options include peat, charcoal, and dried animal droppings.
parents.page_96: To make a feather stick, select a piece of standing dead wood with an even grain and no knots. Ideally, use wood from a tree that has died but not yet fallen, as it lacks green foliage and sheds bark easily; avoid the bottom few inches where moisture wicks up from the ground. If using small branches snapped off a tree, remove the bark first. Lay your knife blade flat on the stick and run it down ten times without cutting to feel for the grain. Then, run the blade down to cut a shaving, stopping before the end so the shaving remains attached to the stick. Turn the stick slightly and repeat the process along the edge created by the first cut, working around the entire circumference until the rest of the stick is feathered with curled shavings. Keep the stick firmly on the ground to steady your action, angle the blade toward the wood to ensure shavings curl as they are made, and collect any shavings that fall off. A finished feather stick consists of a thin piece of wood with attached curled shavings, providing tinder, kindling, and fuel in one unit. Four to six such sticks provide sufficient combustible material to start a fire.
parents.page_97: To make char cloth, turn the lid of a small can with a tight-fitting seal upside down and hammer a nail into the center to create a small hole; smaller holes are preferable. Cut pieces of 100 percent cotton cloth that fit inside the can without folding and place them securely within the container. Position the can over a fire to burn off all the oxygen inside, allowing pyrolysis to occur in the absence of oxygen. Once the smoke stops rising from the hole, the process is complete. Allow the can to cool down before removing the cloth. The finished char cloth should be black, semi-rigid, soft, and not brittle. Test a piece by striking it with a spark or flame; if it creates an ember, it is ready for use as lightweight tinder that requires minimal space and ignites easily from weak sparks. Ensure the char cloth remains dry to function properly.
parents.page_98: To make a fire can, cut out a long, thin piece of cardboard that is one-quarter inch wider than the depth of your can. Roll this cardboard tightly along its length until the roll fits snugly inside the can. Light a candle and allow the melting wax to drip into the can, soaking the cardboard and filling it up. Stop when the can is nearly full and let the wax harden. Once cooled, hold the can at an angle and light the top of the cardboard with the candle. Allow the flame to spread across the top surface. A fire can burns for hours with a concentrated, controllable flame that produces no smoke, making it useful for starting stubborn fires in poor conditions, boiling water, basic cooking, or warming hands. Essential tools include a can with a tight-fitting lid such as a small shoe-polish can, ribbed or plain cardboard, a candle with matches or a lighter, and a knife or scissors. Position the candle so the flame melts the wax rather than burning into the air. Remember that an extra quarter-inch of cardboard will burn down slightly and act as a wick. The can becomes hot quickly, so do not touch it when lit. When the flame starts to fail, either add more wax or replace the cardboard and start again.
parents.page_99: Once you have collected dry tinder, kindling, and fuel, you can begin building your fire. Select a fire type based on your specific needs such as warmth, cooking, signaling, drying clothing, or waste disposal. Always estimate the amount of fuel required and double it to ensure sufficiency. Choose a fire design that requires the least effort for maximum gain. It is more efficient to build a small fire and sit close to it rather than building a large fire and sitting far away. If your wood is wet, remove the bark and split the wood because the center will usually be dry. Once the fire is established, place damp tinder, kindling, and larger pieces of wood near the flames to dry them out. To light the fire, place a tinder ball on a green wood platform and ignite it. Gently lay kindling by the tinder ball and arrange it in a teepee shape to allow air circulation where the heat is strongest. As the kindling catches fire and flames grow, slowly add larger pieces of kindling until you can introduce split logs. Start with small amounts of kindling to ensure the fire establishes itself before adding more fuel.
parents.page_100: To build a teepee fire, surround the tinder ball with kindling arranged in a teepee shape and place small, medium, and large fuel logs in a square at its foot. For a hole-in-the-ground fire, create a hole in the side of a bank with a chimney extending up through the ground; light it inside using any available fuel. Alternatively, line a hole three feet deep with non-porous stones, place tinder and kindling inside, and rest large logs against the sides so they drop down as they burn. A simple fire can be made from tinder, kindling, and any type of fuel by placing two long logs on either side in a V-shape. Another method involves arranging large non-porous stones in a circle with a tinder ball in the middle and kindling around it, adding fuel logs once the fire is established. To build a log cabin style fire, arrange four logs so they meet in the middle and push additional logs in as they burn. For a long-lasting fire, place tinder, kindling, and fuel logs in a depression six feet long and lay two long fuel logs on top of the burning fire. A snake hole fire requires digging a large hole for the fire and a slightly smaller hole for the chimney with a tunnel linking the two, using small logs as fuel. This configuration allows cooking at ground level, is quick to light, can burn wet wood because the inner fire dries it, creates a draft for high temperatures, provides shelter from the elements, becomes self-feeding once lit, uses hardwood logs or stones to shelter the fire from wind, produces great heat, reduces environmental impact by using existing fire rings, lasts long enough to stay alight all night, emits concentrated heat, and keeps flames below ground so the fire remains hidden.
parents.page_101: Lighting tinder is the first stage of making a fire when matches or lighters are unavailable. You can create sparks using various ignition devices such as flint and steel, a magnifying glass, a battery, or chemical reactions involving potassium permanganate. To use a magnifying glass, focus bright sunlight onto dry tinder to create a hot spot and hold the glass steady until the tinder catches fire; reading glasses with lenses can also serve this purpose. For matches, strike them away from you on the box and cup them in your hands to light them commando style; waterproof matches are typically standard matches coated with wax and varnish. Always carry a lighter around your neck on a piece of cord for safety. You can generate sparks using a battery by laying wire across the terminals or by removing the bulb from a flashlight, placing wire wool over the terminal, and switching the flashlight on. Thinner wire works better for this method, especially with lower voltage batteries like 1.5v, but use it only for short periods to avoid draining the battery. Potassium permanganate is a strong oxidizer that can create an explosive mixture when mixed with certain chemicals and may stain skin and clothes.
parents.page_102: To strike a match, hold the box in one hand and position the match between your thumb and first two fingers of the other. Strike the match firmly away from yourself. When it lights, cup your hands to protect the flame and allow it to burn slightly along the stem before touching the tinder. Draw the firesteel back across the striker to direct sparks onto the tinder, ensuring the firesteel moves rather than the striker. Potassium permanganate devices last for about 12,000 strikes and reach temperatures of 5,400°F (3,000°C), functioning in all weathers and at any altitude. To create sparks manually, mix potassium permanganate and sugar in equal amounts on a hard surface, then press down on the mixture with your knife and drag the blade along to generate a spark. Alternatively, mix a small amount of potassium permanganate with glycol or antifreeze, quickly wrap it in paper, and place it on the ground while standing clear due to the risk of sudden and dangerous combustion.
parents.page_103: Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location. Force the pace by traveling at the speed of the slowest person in your group. Shelter in dry riverbeds due to the risk of flash floods. If you cannot survive where you are and cannot move due to injury, do everything possible to attract rescue. If your situation changes from moving to staying, consult alternative survival guidelines. When moving, make an informed decision on the best location and leave clear indications of your intent if abandoning a vehicle. Keep location aids accessible while moving and distributed when static. Protect yourself against glare from the sun and windburn. Seek or improvise shelter when not moving and seize all opportunities to collect fuel for a fire, as deserts get cold at night. Be on constant lookout for signs of water or civilization such as green vegetation, converging animal or human tracks, or circling birds.
parents.page_105: To construct a bow drill, cut a stick as long as your arm and create a notch near each end. Secure one notch with an arbor knot and the other with a double chain fastening knot, leaving enough slack to wrap around your finger. For the drill itself, select a straight piece of wood free of knots, ideally measuring about 1 inch in diameter and 8 inches long. Carve one end to fit into the bearing block with a dull point to minimize friction, and carve the opposite end to fit into the hearth board with bluntness that maximizes friction. This method is highly efficient for generating fire by friction but requires specific knowledge, skill, practice, appropriate wood selection, time, and effort. Essential tools include a knife, dry wood, and cordage such as paracord, fishing line, shoelaces, or bailing twine.
parents.page_106: The bow drill set functions best when the drill and hearth board are crafted from the same dry wood, ensuring both parts wear down evenly. Suitable woods include sycamore, hazel, ivy, lime, willow, and sotol, though sotol is not found in Europe. Slight ridges or bumps on the drill help prevent the cord from slipping. Construct the bearing block from hard wood approximately 3 to 4 inches across and 4 to 5 inches long; split it in two and create a notch. Make the hearth board from flat wood about 1 inch thick. Hold the drill so it sits about 3/8 inch from the edge of the board. Notch a small hole to help start the drill, then cut a V into the center of that hole through the hearth board; the bottom of this V is where char collects and the ember forms in the ember pan. Use a knife point to make the notch, insert the drill's pointed end into the hole, twist the cord once around the drill with the cord on the side of the bow, and ensure the V is about one-eighth of the hole's circumference. Set up the bow, drill, and bearing block, then bow the drill into the hole in the hearth board until the hole is as wide as the drill, but do not go all the way through.
parents.page_107: Before starting, ensure your dry tinder bundle, kindling, and fuel are within reach. Be aware that this process requires significant energy, so take precautions against overheating and dehydration. It is advisable to practice making tinder bundles and lighting them with a small coal from an existing campfire before attempting the bow drill. To begin, hold the bow horizontal to the ground and parallel to your body. Lean forward and back to apply and reduce downward pressure on the drill. Start by bowing slowly and evenly using the full length of the bow while breathing steadily. Gradually increase speed and pressure until smoke appears. Once smoke is visible, vigorously bow with maximum effort for about ten more times to produce a coal in the ember pan. Place the ember pan on a flat, dry surface aligned with the V on the hearth board. Loop the bow cord once around the drill from the inside. Insert the drill into the hole on the hearth board and slot the block on top, ensuring a green leaf is placed in the notch for lubrication. Lock your wrist into your shin to use it as support while steadying the hearth board with your foot. Use your arm to bow and bear down on the block with your body weight. If the drill slips, move your grip farther along the bow to increase tension in the string. Keep your head still above the bow and maintain the drill upright. Friction heats the wood as the drill turns, which is essential for creating a coal.
parents.page_108: Gently remove the drill and lift the hearth board away from the coal. If the coal sticks to the board, tap it with your knife to loosen it. Lift the ember pan off the ground and hold it in the air to check if the coal is glowing. If the coal is not glowing but just smoking, gently fan it with your hand until you see it glow. Transfer the glowing coal into your tinder bundle. The coal must touch the tinder to transfer heat, so squeeze the tinder around the coal without crushing it or starving it of oxygen. Gently blow into the bundle to help fan the coal and encourage it to catch. You can swing the bundle down and back up in between breaths to let dry air fan the coal. Continue as the amount of smoke increases until the bundle bursts into flames. Do not throw the burning bundle on the ground; calmly place it where you intend to have your fire. Start building the fire around the bundle once kindling is alight. Use green wood as a base and hold the tinder bundle up, away from your face, with your back to the wind to help combustion.
parents.page_109: Man-made cordage is a vital survival item used for building shelters, repairing gear and clothing, creating traps and nets, and generating fire with a bow drill. Carry ample supplies in both your backpack and on your person to ensure availability if separated from your pack. When using a survival canister, verify that the needle has an eye large enough to pass the inner strands of the cordage. Paracord is a lightweight nylon kernmantle rope consisting of 32 braided strands; each strand contains smaller threads useful for secondary tasks. It is highly recommended due to its strength, small pack size, and widespread availability. To prevent unraveling after cutting, coil paracord into palm-sized hanks or cut it into 30-foot lengths for storage in pockets. Before crafting cordage from natural materials, check existing supplies like shoelaces, belts, clothing strips, hat brims, guylines, draw cords, towropes, and dental floss. For shoelaces, wrap twice the required length around your boots before tying them on. Adopt a scavenger mindset to collect useful items such as plastic bottles and bailing twine. Always use cordage efficiently by tying simple, strong knots that can be released without cutting, preserving the material for future tasks. Red cordage is ideal in survival situations because its color stands out.
parents.page_110: If you lack man-made cordage, you can create a natural substitute using roots, stems, bark, or sinews found in your immediate environment to erect shelters. To prepare nettle fibers, sit astride a log and lay a nettle stem in front of you. Roll a smooth, rounded stick back and forth on the stem while pressing down hard until the stem is crushed. Use your thumbnail to tease open the crushed tissue along the entire length of the stem to expose the spongy pith inside. Bend the stem in the middle; the inner pith will break away from the outer skin, allowing you to carefully peel them apart. The long, fibrous strips of skin can then be used for short bindings or natural cordage. When selecting plants, old stinging nettles or wood nettles with long stems offer the most fibers. Wear gloves or cover your hands while stripping leaves by grasping the stem at the base and pulling your clenched fist up the full length of the stem. Ensure the plant material meets specific requirements: the fibers must be long enough to avoid weakening the line if spliced together, rougher fibers are preferable for braiding as they bite together better than shiny or smooth ones which tend to unravel, and the fibers must be strong and pliable enough to withstand bending and tying without breaking.
parents.page_111: To make natural cordage, first prepare a sufficient quantity of fibers and let them dry before use, though they can be utilized immediately in survival situations. The most effective method involves twisting the fibers. Grip the first length of fiber a third of the way along its length between the forefinger and thumb of your left hand. Grasp the fiber with your right thumb and forefinger one inch from your left hand and twist it until a tight loop forms. While maintaining tension on the cord, move your left thumb and forefinger forward to clamp down on the loop. Start your first length of cordage closer to one end rather than in the middle to offset subsequent splices, as splices are potential weak points. If the twisted fibers are thin enough, they can be braided to create a thicker, stronger cord. To dry the fibers, hang them up in the sun or near a fire.
parents.page_112: To make natural cordage, move your right hand back so that one inch of cord remains visible and apply another twist. Simultaneously, grasp the left-hand cord with the middle and fourth fingers of your right hand. Sweep these fingers upward to pull the left-hand cord under the captive piece. Inch your left thumb and forefinger forward while clamping down on the new twist, then release your right thumb and forefinger to let the lower cord fall. Grip the upper cord one inch from your left fingers with your right thumb and forefinger and repeat these steps along the length of the cord. To splice in an extra length, overlap the new piece two inches along the existing cord and twist them together between your left thumb and forefinger before continuing as before.
parents.page_113: The Arbor knot is a versatile survival knot used to secure one end of a line to a fixed point without needing adjustment or to join two poles together, such as in an A-frame shelter. To tie it, hold the working end in your right hand and the standing line in your left. Pass the cord around the tree, then loop the working end over the standing line and back over the cord around the tree. Keep the standing line taut with your left hand while pulling the working end down with your right to tighten an overhand knot around the standing line. Tie a second overhand knot in the working end as close to the first knot as possible, then pull on the standing line to lock both knots together. Give the standing line a sharp tug to secure it. Next, pass the working end around the standing line and over itself to form an overhand loop. Pass the free end up through this loop to complete the knot, which is now an overhand knot tied around the standing line. The working end is the active part used for tying, while the standing end is the passive part around which the knot is formed.
parents.page_114: The slip knot is a simple knot with many practical uses in survival situations. To tie it, loop the working end of the line and pull to tighten the loop. To loosen it, pull on the loop itself. When tying, hold the working end between your right thumb and forefinger and hold the standing end between your left thumb and forefinger. The double chain fastening knot securely attaches the free end of a line to a fixture, particularly when weight is involved and you do not want the knot to slip or come loose. To tie this knot, loop the working end around the fixture such as a tree or pole. Pass it over and around the standing line. Twist the standing line over the working line to form a loop. Hold this loop between your left thumb and forefinger. Pass the working end back around the fixture and then bring it under the standing line. Make a loop with the working end and pass it into the first loop. Collect the second loop with your left thumb and forefinger and pull it through. Pass the working end over the top and around the standing line again, and once more around the fixture. With your right thumb and forefinger, pull both ends away from the loop in your left hand. Put the loop over whatever you want to secure. Pull the knot tight. Bring the working line around and thread it under itself to complete the knot. Lock the knot into position.
parents.page_115: The Siberian hitch, also known as the Evenk knot, is an effective method for attaching a rope to a fixture, such as securing a ridgeline to a tree for a poncho shelter. To tie the knot, hold the working line in your right hand and the standing line in your left. Pass the cord around the tree, then pinch the working line with your left-hand fingers. Pull the working line up through the loop to tighten the knot on itself, followed by pulling the working end to secure it tightly around the tree. To release the knot, pull the end of the standing line. To adjust or manipulate the knot, bring your left hand under the standing line and turn your palm so that one loop twists over another. Hold the working end between your right thumb and forefinger, then bring the loop around your left fingers over the standing line. Lay the standing line on your left palm and loop the working line one and a half times around your fingers before hooking your thumb into the loop to complete the adjustment.
parents.page_116: You can dismantle a tent or free a boat from its mooring more quickly by finishing off a taut line hitch with a quick-release knot. The taut line hitch is widely used in survival and outdoor activities because it allows you to adjust the tension on a fixed line, such as a guy-line on a tent or a boat mooring in tidal waters. To tie this knot, attach the standing line to an anchor point. Form a loop with the working line and pass the working end through it. Repeat the process by making a loop, known as a bight, in the working line and passing it through the earlier loop rather than just the end. Pass the working end up through that loop again so the two knots sit side by side without tightening them yet. Tighten the knot as usual for a taut line hitch but leave the working end free so you can pull it quickly to release the knot. To secure the knot, pass the working end under the standing line and back through the loop it created, ensuring it goes outside the earlier loops. Overhand the loop around a tree if necessary. Tighten the knot by pulling both lines. Pulling the knot farther from the anchor point will increase the tension.
parents.page_117: Anyone venturing into the wilderness should carry a knife, preferably a bushcraft knife, as it is likely the most important survival tool after knowledge itself. Large working blades are vital for those living primitive lives; examples include parangs and machetes, which feature blades about 18 to 20 inches long and are used mainly for cutting and slashing. The kukri is another similar knife used by the Gurkhas of Nepal. When using these large knives, it is important to wear a wrist lanyard. Ideal knives should have a blade that is 3/16 inch thick, made of stainless or high-carbon steel, with an attachment point for the wrist lanyard. Blades that extend through to the end of the handle have no weak points. For comfort and durability, avoid ribbed rubber handles; instead, choose fitted leather sheaths that hold the knife securely and can accommodate a firesteel for sparking fires. A guard on the blade reduces the chance of hands slipping onto the edge. Additionally, sharp blades that end near the handle put less strain on the wrist when working close to the handle. An experienced person can accomplish most survival skills, such as making feather sticks, with a bushcraft knife because it possesses features that help in every situation.
parents.page_118: A bushcraft knife is designed for carving, cutting, and splitting small logs; use a machete or saw for chopping large logs. The blade should not exceed 8 to 9 inches in length. When passing a knife within a group, ensure only one good knife is available and pass it handle first with the blade facing upward to maintain safety. To cut close to your body while maintaining wrist control, use the chest lever grip by holding the knife in a backhand position and utilizing arm and chest muscles for power. For tasks requiring precise visibility and control, especially when follow-through might cause injury, adopt the backhand grip where the blade faces upward. The forehand grip is the most natural method, allowing your back and arm to provide strength while your wrist controls the action; ensure the blade faces down and place one or both thumbs on the back of the blade for enhanced control. Generally, grip the handle at the end nearest the blade to utilize the section of the blade closest to the handle, avoiding serrated edges as they hinder this specific cutting technique. Always clean and dry the blade when not in use before returning it to its sheath. Wear the sheath securely on your belt or around your neck using a lanyard.
parents.page_121: Water is essential for survival and requires a regular intake of 2–3 quarts daily to maintain balance and prevent dehydration, though needs increase with high temperatures, physical exertion, injury, or age. Soldiers in extreme environments like deserts or jungles may require up to 14 liters per day. Always plan your movements around water availability and ensure you can replace lost fluids. Filter and purify any water before drinking it using available hydration systems or small trail methods. While stomach bugs from contaminated water can impair performance, dehydration is far more deadly; therefore, if no clean water is available, drink contaminated water rather than nothing so that medical treatment remains an option. Never consume urine or salt water as these accelerate dehydration. In short-term survival situations, food is not a primary priority since starvation does not occur within a few days, but hunger and energy loss can affect coordination after several days. If food is found, eat small amounts frequently while ensuring sufficient water is available for digestion. In long-term survival scenarios, the need for food becomes critical, so regularly reassess your situation and adjust plans accordingly to avoid reaching a state where you lack the physical capacity to obtain food when it becomes necessary. Obtaining wild food requires knowledge, effort, skill, and luck.
parents.page_122: Your body converts food into fuel, providing heat and energy while aiding recovery from work, injury, or sickness. A healthy body can survive for weeks without food by using reserves stored in its tissues. You expend approximately 70 calories per hour just breathing and up to 5,500 calories per day if working hard. When gathering food in the wild, always ensure that the energy gained from the food exceeds the energy expended in procuring it; otherwise, the effort is wasteful. Food that is easy to find, gather, and prepare should be your first priority. Plants are easy to collect and should be your first choice for food as long as they are readily available in your environment, but you must be absolutely sure they are edible because mistakenly eating the wrong leaf or berry could cause vomiting and diarrhea, worsening your situation. Avoid mushrooms unless you are 100% sure they are edible, as they can be fatal. Fishing requires little effort once lines or traps are set, and they will work around the clock; fish is high in protein and relatively simple to prepare and cook. Insects, reptiles, and amphibians may also be available, but remember that many are poisonous, although they can be used as bait for fish and mammals. Birds and mammals have their own survival mechanisms and are wary of humans, especially in remote areas where contact with humans is limited. Even if caught, a bird or mammal will need to be killed, plucked or skinned, and cooked before consumption.
parents.page_123: Water is essential for every physical and chemical process in the human body, serving as a delivery service to transport oxygen and nutrients, a waste remover to flush toxins via urine, a coolant to regulate body temperature, a breathing aid to moisten inhaled air, a sensory aid to conduct nervous impulses, and a shock absorber to protect vital organs and lubricate joints. Dehydration occurs when water loss exceeds intake due to factors like extreme temperatures, humidity, physical exertion, clothing, body size, fitness levels, or injury. The effects of water loss progress from mild symptoms such as thirst, stiffness in joints, darkened urine, dry mouth, headache, nausea, and dizziness to severe conditions including impaired vision, deafness, shrunken skin, loss of appetite, impatience, drowsiness, lethargy, inability to swallow, slurred speech, swollen tongue, tingling limbs, difficulty walking, breathing problems, blueness of extremities, delirium, unconsciousness, and ultimately death. Survival requires maintaining a steady balance between water intake and loss to prevent the fatal progression of dehydration.
parents.page_124: Water constitutes approximately 80 percent of brain tissue, protecting organs from shock, and makes up about 22 percent of bones. It moistens soft tissues in the nose, mouth, and eyes while helping dissolve minerals and nutrients for body access. Water flushes waste products through the kidneys and liver, provides cushioning for joints and soft tissues, aids digestion to convert food into energy, moistens lungs to assist breathing, regulates blood pressure, helps remove fatty acids from the body to reduce heart attack risk, hydrates skin, and flushes toxins through the bladder. Additionally, the evaporation of sweat from the skin helps regulate body temperature.
parents.page_125: Water-borne diseases result from ingesting water contaminated by human or animal feces and urine, which contain protozoa, viruses, bacteria, or intestinal parasites. These illnesses cause approximately 10 million deaths annually worldwide. Common symptoms include loss of appetite, nausea, abdominal pain, profuse foul-smelling watery diarrhea, vomiting, lethargy, fever, blood in the urine, abdominal cramps, dark-colored urine, jaundice, aching muscles, and rapid dehydration. Specific conditions such as infectious hepatitis, bacillary dysentery, giardiasis, cryptosporidium, amebic dysentery, cholera, E. coli infections, leptospirosis, salmonella, bilharzia, and hookworms present with varying combinations of these symptoms, including blood or pus in stools, cold clammy skin, increased heart rate, coughing up larvae, and severe fatigue. Some diseases can be fatal if not diagnosed early, particularly affecting vulnerable groups like the very young or the elderly.
parents.page_126: The amount of water needed to survive depends on your physical condition, environment, and exertion level. Even while resting in the shade, an average person loses more than one quart of water daily through breathing and urination, a loss that increases significantly with sweating. A minimum of three quarts per day is required to stay healthy in a survival situation, with higher needs for extreme heat or heavy workloads. In the first 24 hours, you must address protection and location, which are demanding tasks that increase thirst. Drinking too much water can cause hyponatremia, a condition where excess water dilutes sodium levels in the blood, leading to cell swelling, neurological issues, coma, and death. Prevention involves controlling water and salt intake; if you lack salt or tablets, filter saltwater through fabric to remove salt content. If your water supply is limited, ration it efficiently until rescue, but ensure adequate hydration early on rather than going without water for the first 24 hours unless specific circumstances dictate otherwise. Factors such as stress from the incident, seasickness, injury, or harsh environments like deserts may make water rationing unviable. Drinking nothing for the first day with a limited supply can lead to severe dehydration where remaining water provides no benefit.
parents.page_127: To find water in temperate climates, locate the most accessible source near your camp and position a catchment device there to maximize exposure to rainfall. Secure a tarpaulin to four stakes using string, ensuring one end is higher than the other to create a natural run-off channel. Place a heavy stone in the middle of the tarp, about two-thirds of the way toward the lower end, to direct rainwater into this channel. Position a container, such as a mess can, below the end of the channel to collect the flowing water. Any non-porous material can serve as a catchment device for collecting rainwater, which is the safest method for procuring drinking water without prior treatment, provided the collection device itself remains uncontaminated.
parents.page_128: In temperate climates, always treat water from natural sources before drinking, with the sole exception of rainwater. Most natural water sources are used by animals for drinking, bathing, urinating, and defecating, so you must filter and purify collected water to avoid contamination. Streams and rivers provide permanent water in low-lying areas; green vegetation often indicates their presence. Water near mountaintops is typically clearer and flows faster, so collect it from the surface while checking upstream for contaminants or following the flow downstream. Rock holes found in high ground act as natural rainwater collectors; if water appears trapped deep within, surgical tubing can be used to retrieve it. Wells may be covered and marked by locals in remote areas, so learn local markers before use. Collect water at the point where rivers, streams, or run-offs enter lakes or ponds, avoiding stagnant water within the body. Soaks are found near rivers in low-lying areas below the existing water table and are often indicated by vegetation but may be polluted by animal activity. Seepage occurs at the base of cliffs or rocky outcrops through slow-running drainage channels. In arid areas, rivers and streams usually only flow during floods and contain higher levels of pollutants. Be aware that dry riverbeds can experience flash floods during the rainy season that move faster than you can run. Observe animal behavior as a warning sign; for instance, if small gazelles suddenly disappear from a water source, investigate the cause before collecting water.
parents.page_129: To collect water from dew in temperate climates, tie absorbent material such as rags or a T-shirt tightly around each ankle and walk through dew-covered grass before sunrise or late in the evening. The material will absorb moisture from the air and surfaces as you move; wring out the fabric to extract the water into a container and repeat the process until you have an ample supply or the dew has evaporated. Dew forms on exposed non-porous surfaces like car roofs or tarpaulins when surface temperatures are low enough to cause condensation of moisture from warmer air. You can also harvest dew by walking through fields with absorbent material tied around your ankles. Alternatively, construct a dew trap by digging a hole approximately 18 inches (45 cm) deep, lining it with a plastic sheet, and filling it with smooth, clean stones. Water will condense on the stones overnight; line the bottom of the hole to collect the water and weigh down the lining with a stone to keep it in place. Harvest the collected water as early as possible the next morning to prevent evaporation.
parents.page_130: To find water in temperate climates, create a transpiration bag by placing a smooth rock in the lower corner of a plastic bag and securing it over tree branch leaves. As water evaporates from the leaves, it condenses and collects at the lowest point of the bag, which can be retrieved using surgical tubing. Alternatively, cut green vegetation into a plastic bag, add a smooth rock to the bottom corner, tie the bag shut, and secure it in direct sunlight to encourage evaporation and condensation. To delay dehydration, conserve existing water supplies and use them efficiently. Work only during the coolest parts of the day to avoid sweating. If the sun is shining, seek shade and keep covered. Suck on a small, smooth button or pebble to stimulate saliva production and reduce the sensation of thirst. Avoid consuming protein-rich foods because they require more water to digest than other food groups.
parents.page_131: To construct a solar still, locate or dig a hole in the ground that is at least two feet wide and two feet deep. Place an empty container in the center of the hole. Fill the surrounding area with vegetation, a receptacle containing undrinkable water such as saltwater or urine, or fabric soaked in undrinkable water. Cover the entire hole with a plastic sheet. Position a stone in the center of the plastic sheet to create a low point that directs condensed water into the container below. The sun's heat will cause water from the vegetation or undrinkable sources to evaporate and distill into pure vapor. This vapor will condense on the underside of the plastic sheet and drip into the central container. Essential tools and materials include digging implements, two containers, vegetation or undrinkable water, fabric or clothing, a plastic sheet, tarpaulin, or space blanket, stones, and optionally surgical tubing to retrieve the collected water.
parents.page_132: To locate hidden water sources in temperate climates, observe the landscape for patches of green vegetation, noting that plants may draw moisture from deep roots rather than surface water. Water is more likely to be found downhill or in low-lying areas such as valleys and at the base of cliffs or rock formations. On coastlines, look for wetlands where water has seeped inland, which may contain tolerable salt levels or can be distilled using a solar still. Watch animal behavior closely; listen for birds twittering at dawn and dusk and follow their flight paths to find drinking spots. Flocks of birds circling over one spot usually indicate a water source below, except for meat-eating birds like vultures. All finches and grain-eaters require regular water supplies, so observe their flight patterns to locate sources. Animal tracks often lead to water, particularly where sets of tracks converge. The presence of flies, bees, and mosquitoes almost certainly means water is nearby. Additionally, look for herd animals such as elephants and wildebeest, as they depend on accessible water.
parents.page_133: In hot-humid climates like jungles and rainforests, your body loses significant water through perspiration to regulate temperature, requiring you to drink up to 3.5 gallons (14 liters) daily to avoid dehydration. While water sources are plentiful in these environments, rivers and streams may be unavailable during certain seasons or at high elevations, making alternative procurement methods essential. The most effective method is catching rainwater using passive collection devices that require no energy once set up. You can construct a sloped bamboo roof with a gutter to channel rain into containers, or create a drainpipe by tying half-cut bamboo along the path of water running down a tree trunk. Alternatively, wrap absorbent materials like rags or T-shirts around leaning trees to catch dripping water; shape the material to form a low point where water drips and place a container underneath. Wide-leafed plants can also be used to build a roof by overlapping leaves from bottom to top to channel water into a bamboo gutter placed at the base. Regardless of the method used, all collected rainwater must be filtered and purified before consumption.
parents.page_134: In hot-humid climates, you can source water from vegetation such as pitcher plants that collect rainfall or dew in hollow parts. Some trees store rainwater in natural receptacles like cracks or hollows, and life-saving liquid can be obtained from a tree's roots or sap. To access water trapped in green bamboo, carefully cut into the bamboo with a machete or knife. You can also collect small unripe coconuts to drink their fluid or create a spigot to tap water directly from a tree. Water vines found in tropical jungles and rainforests are identifiable by their size and shape and provide fresh water, though not all contain drinkable liquid as some hold poisonous sap. Most water-bearing vines are about 2 inches in diameter. When found, make a small cut with a machete to check the sap's color; if the fluid is milky, do not drink it, but if it is clear, the water is safe. Cut off the vine at a point lower than the first cut so the liquid, which has a neutral fruity taste, can flow. Prevent the vine from touching your mouth because the bark may contain irritants. If the pores in the upper end of the vine close and stop water flow, simply cut the top of the vine again with your machete to rectify this.
parents.page_135: Anyone entering hot-dry climates should carry sufficient water for their needs plus an emergency supply. Green vegetation usually indicates the presence of water or moisture, and techniques used in temperate climates may work in some desert conditions. If no surface water is available and other means are unavailable, a water-yielding plant may be your only option. While clear sap, fruit, or trapped rainwater from plants can quench immediate thirst, do not rely on these sources for long-term survival. Unusual clusters of green plants may indicate a minor presence of water, while an abundance of greenery suggests a more substantial source. Water is rarely found deeper than 6 feet below the surface, so you will need to dig for it during the coolest part of the day. Be aware that finding water inside the bend of a dry wadi in a real survival situation has slim chances. Prickly pear cacti, found in low-spreading clumps up to 3 feet tall in dry sandy soils, have edible fruit that can provide life-saving fluid. If you must procure water in the desert, you are likely already in a desperate situation, so combine knowledge of temperate environment techniques with the methods described here to increase your chances of survival. Always remove the prickly outer layer of the prickly pear fruit before consumption.
parents.page_136: Always start your journey hydrated and carry enough water to meet your needs plus an emergency supply sufficient to get you out of danger. Cache water ahead if necessary and check your map for probable water sources, confirming their reliability with locals and asking about unmarked wells. Barrel-shaped cacti contain a safe-to-drink milky fluid; carefully remove the top with a machete, mash the flesh into pulp with a stick, and extract juice by sucking through a hollow grass stem or soak up the fluid with cloth and wring it out, though rewards from both methods are minimal. Agaves native to Mexico and the southern and western United States have thick fleshy leaves containing drinkable fluid; cut the huge flower stalk with a knife or machete to collect the juice. Monitor your progress against water usage, reevaluating goals if you use more than expected, and remember it is better to turn back than push on into unnecessary danger. Mark your map or GPS waypoints for any water sources sighted during travel so you can return to known sources rather than relying on hope. Keep water bottles in the shade or in windy locations to maintain cool temperatures.
parents.page_137: Build a fire to generate heat for melting ice or snow. To collect meltwater from ice, construct a platform using a large flat stone supported by logs or smaller stones on either side of the fire. Angle the platform to create natural runoff channels for the melting water so it flows into a collection container. If you already possess some water, pour it into a container and heat it over the fire; add small pieces of broken ice to accelerate melting while keeping the water hot but not boiling to prevent evaporation. Alternatively, if no water is available, fashion a gently sloping platform from flat stone or wood positioned above a fire to slowly melt ice or snow, collecting the resulting water as it flows off. When choosing between ice and snow for melting, prioritize ice because it melts faster and is denser; if ice cannot be found, use compact snow instead.
parents.page_138: Prioritize water usage in cold weather with the same urgency as in desert environments. Always locate a water source near your fire-building and maintenance area. Before attempting to melt snow or ice, search for alternative natural water sources, as collecting meltwater is more time- and fuel-efficient. Your ability to procure water in freezing conditions depends directly on your capacity to start and maintain a fire. To melt ice safely, build a small fire on a platform positioned on a gentle slope to allow melted water to run off, supported by two logs with the block of ice placed in the center. Never try to melt ice or snow in your mouth, as this can cause freezing injuries to your lips and mouth; additionally, melting ice inside your body expends vital heat that could lead to hypothermia. Ensure you have sufficient fuel to maintain a fire for a considerable period. Regulate your body heat to minimize overheating and sweating. Keep drinking water close to your body to prevent freezing, but avoid placing containers directly against your skin; instead, store them between layers of clothing to utilize the warmer trapped air, which helps raise the water's temperature. Do not use recently frozen seawater because it contains high levels of salt.
parents.page_139: To find water in cold climates, cut a solid piece of dense snow, often called a marshmallow, and skewer it with a stick. Secure the stick in the ground close enough to a fire so that it receives heat, and position a suitable container underneath to collect the water as it melts. If you already have water, follow techniques for melting ice by heating a little water and adding bits of snow. Do not pack the snow too tightly; if an air pocket forms, the heat from the fire will be absorbed by the metal container rather than by the snow, which could result in the fire burning a hole through the metal container before it melts the snow. Snow is a great insulator; even if the temperature dips to -40°F (-40°C), water in a bottle will remain largely unfrozen if placed under at least 1 foot of snow. Make sure you store the bottles upside-down so that if some of the water does freeze, it freezes at the bottom of the bottle and not at the top. Always look for the whitest, purest-looking snow, and ice that is bluish.
parents.page_140: To find water in cold climates using a melting sack, create an improvised sack from any porous material like a T-shirt or sock and suspend it near a fire using a stick. Position the sack close enough to the fire so that heat melts the snow inside, which can then be collected in a container. If you lack natural fuel for melting ice or snow but have your first- or second-line equipment, you can use your survival fire can as a container. Set up the fire can on the ground and shelter it from wind using your body or pack. Place small quantities of ice or snow into the can, light the fire can, and position it over the flame. Add more ice or snow as it begins to melt. With these tools, you can procure life-saving water even without access to natural fuel sources.
parents.page_141: The sea presents one of the most challenging environments for survival due to its lack of natural protection against extreme temperatures, wind, rain, and rough conditions, as well as offering little help with location. You should avoid abandoning your vessel unless it is absolutely necessary. If you are adrift in a liferaft with no immediate hope of rescue, obtaining drinking water becomes a major priority. Even without specialized equipment like solar stills or reverse-osmosis pumps, there are other methods to procure fresh water. These include channeling collected rainwater into a receptacle and spreading a tarpaulin over a large surface area to catch as much rainwater as possible. It is critical never to drink saltwater because its salt concentration is three times higher than that of blood; ingesting it will cause dehydration, and prolonged use can lead to kidney failure and death.
parents.page_142: To find water at sea, utilize the built-in rainwater catchment systems found in most modern liferafts which channel rain and dew into internal pockets. You can also construct a similar system using a tarpaulin or any waterproof material by spreading it in a bowl shape to maximize collection during showers; always deploy this before nightfall to capture overnight rainfall. To harvest dew, secure the tarpaulin like a sunshade at night and turn up its edges to catch moisture, or use a sponge or cloth to collect dew from the sides of the raft. Before abandoning ship, retrieve as much fresh water and signaling devices as possible. Conserve your water rations by fixing a daily ration based on your total supply, the output of solar stills or desalting kits, and the size and condition of your party. Prevent contamination by keeping fresh water supplies shaded from both direct sunlight and glare off the sea's surface. In hot conditions, you may dampen clothes with saltwater to lower body temperature, but avoid overdoing it to prevent saltwater boils and rashes. Relax and sleep whenever possible to reduce water loss. Use every available container, even simple garbage bags, to collect rainwater, ensuring they remain sealed and attached to the raft. If you have no water, do not eat, as protein consumption will hasten dehydration.
parents.page_143: Reverse-osmosis pumps are hand-powered devices that force seawater through a membrane to filter out salt, capable of producing approximately 23 quarts or liters of fresh water daily. These units require significant physical effort, so users should share the task to avoid excessive perspiration and water loss. Desalting kits utilize ion exchange to convert seawater into fresh water but produce only small amounts over several hours; they should be reserved for situations where solar stills cannot be used. Solar stills offer a simple method of distilling water using sunlight by placing saltwater at the bottom of a container lined with black fabric to absorb heat. The sun evaporates the water, causing pure vapor to condense on the inner surface of clear plastic and drip down the sides for collection via a tube. Most solar stills found on liferafts are inflatable. While these products are standard issue on most liferafts, it is advisable to carry at least one desalting device when entering a marine environment if rainwater or dew cannot be collected.
parents.page_144: To construct a solar still for finding water at sea, gather two containers of different sizes, a plastic sheet, string, and a weight. Place the smaller container inside the larger one and cover the opening with the plastic sheet. Secure the sheet to the rim using string and place a rock or other weight in the center of the sheet to create a low point where condensed water will drip into the inner container. If surgical tubing is available, tie it to the setup to retrieve fresh water without dismantling the still. In dire emergencies, you may collect liquid from sea ice found in Arctic waters; look for old sea ice that appears bluish, has rounded corners, and splinters easily, though approaching icebergs is dangerous. You can also extract drinkable fluid from fish by cutting them open to access the aqueous fluid along the spine and in the eyes, but avoid drinking other fluids as they are rich in protein and fat which will cause dehydration. Sea turtle blood contains a salt concentration similar to humans and can be collected by slitting the throat, but this should only be done in an absolute emergency given that sea turtles are endangered. If your water supply is not salty or poisonous but too foul to drink orally, you can rehydrate by absorbing up to a pint of water daily through the large intestine using an improvised tubing device.
parents.page_145: All water obtained in a survival situation must be treated before drinking to eliminate harmful pathogens and microorganisms, with the exception of rainwater. If you lack a device that both filters and disinfects water, you must complete these tasks in two separate stages. First, filter the water to remove debris; this can be done using a Millbank bag or by constructing an improvised filter. To build a tripod filter, gather three sticks of equal length or a bent sapling to form a stable frame. Place materials inside the tripod frame in distinct layers, starting with the coarsest material at the top and progressing downward to increasingly finer materials such as parachute silk. Pour water into the top layer; it will pass through the layers and become filtered as it moves through the progressively finer materials. Even after filtration, the water must be disinfected before consumption.
parents.page_146: To create an improvised bottle filter, cut the bottom off a plastic container or make a hole in it, and hang the vessel upside down from a branch. Fill the container with layers of filtering materials arranged from coarse to fine as you move downward; start with coarse gravel at the top, followed by fine gravel, coarse sand, charcoal fragments, and finally fine porous material such as cloth at the bottom. Pour water into the top opening so it filters through the layers and emerges from the bottom. This method is effective for producing large quantities of filtered water in a compact form, though the process is slow, taking approximately five minutes to filter one quart or liter. Note that a sock can also be used as an effective filter material. Regardless of the filtration method used, the emerging water must still be purified before drinking.
parents.page_147: Mini portable water purifiers are specially designed units that filter and purify contaminated water by pumping it through micro-filters, chemicals, or both. These devices range from small emergency pumps capable of purifying up to 13 gallons (50 liters) to larger units. To use them, lower the input tube into the water source, suck water through the straw with the filter end placed in any accessible non-saline water, and drinkable water will flow out from the output tube. The filter system is often housed inside a water bottle. Survival straws are compact devices that utilize carbon or iodine resin systems to eliminate diseases and harmful chemicals. To use these straws, you must draw water into your mouth and decant it into a container so you can reach it with the straw. Disinfecting water is vital because drinking untreated water may cause water-borne diseases. The most effective method for treating water is boiling, but if you cannot make a fire, several devices are available to filter and purify water. In a survival situation, the availability of water and your ability to treat, carry, and store it could be the difference between survival and death. Gravity or pressure filters are incorporated within drinking bottles where water flows naturally via gravity or is squeezed through by the operator. These bottles typically employ a filter to remove sediment and organic contaminants, a micron filter to remove protozoa, and a chemical that kills water-borne bacteria and viruses.
parents.page_148: Microorganisms and virtually all intestinal pathogens are killed at temperatures well below the boiling point, so bringing water to a boil is sufficient for disinfection; continuing to boil it wastes fuel, time, and water. If boiling is not possible or you lack a purification device, rely on non-mechanical techniques where concentration and contact time depend on water quality and temperature. Iodine effectively destroys bacteria, viruses, and cysts at a concentration of 8mg per quart at 68°F if left for 10 minutes, though it is less effective against viruses and cysts without phosphoric acid. Potassium permanganate granules should be mixed with water until it turns light pink and left for at least 30 minutes before drinking. Adding unscented household bleach containing 5 percent sodium hypochlorite is a cheap way to add chlorine; use one drop per quart or two if the water is cloudy, then wait at least 30 minutes before consumption. Ultraviolet light disrupts the DNA of harmful microorganisms, rendering them harmless, with exposure needs varying by water quality. Passive UV methods involve filling plastic bottles with water, replacing lids, and placing them in direct sunlight on a dark surface. Active UV devices like Steripen are small purifiers placed in pre-filtered water that can treat one quart in 48 seconds.
parents.page_149: Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location. If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger. Prepare to abandon ship by steering closer to land or known shipping lanes, delegating tasks among survivors, and ensuring lifejackets and liferafts are ready. If no one knows your status, notify people of your plight using any available means of communication. If you have a cell or satellite phone, contact someone immediately; if the situation warrants emergency rescue and you possess a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), activate it. Abandon ship in a controlled manner, deploy all liferafts, and attempt to enter them while they are still dry. Use the rescue line to reach people in distress and follow the immediate action instructions printed inside the liferaft. Address the core principles of survival: protection from the elements, location signaling, water procurement, and food acquisition. If you must move due to extreme survival conditions at sea, find ways to locate water and food. In case of a sinking vessel, secure water, use location aids, and protect yourself from drowning and environmental exposure. Avoid splashing in the water to prevent attracting sharks if animals are nearby. If injured, stabilize your condition and apply first aid before moving or abandoning ship.
parents.page_150: Do not neglect taking anti-seasickness tablets, as vomiting causes dehydration and reduces morale. Ensure everyone wears survival equipment and knows how to operate it. Do not second-guess rescuers when they arrive; follow their instructions exactly. Keep rescue services informed of your situation and update them as conditions change. Prepare to abandon ship by packing lifejackets and suitable clothing. Know how to use your survival equipment and carry basic location aids like a flashlight, whistle, and plastic water bottle on your person. Prepare the vessel for rescue by clearing the deck of loose objects and be ready to drop the sail if a helicopter rescue is attempted. Make an inventory and ration all supplies. Do not cut the painter to the vessel until you are certain it will sink, as the vessel itself is what rescuers will search for. Never drink seawater under any circumstances, as it increases dehydration. Eat only if you have sufficient water to digest the food; note that fish is high in protein and requires plenty of water. If you cannot survive in place but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything possible to attract rescue. If your situation changes so that you must move to find help or locate a suitable place to stay, consult alternative survival guidelines. Deploy a drogue to lessen drifting. Inventory all food, water, and equipment, and begin rationing immediately. Prepare water procurement devices such as solar stills and reverse osmosis pumps. Protect yourself from the sun, wind, and salt-water spray. If you have no liferaft, huddle together in a group with children in the center; if alone, draw your knees up to your chest to lessen heat loss. Improvise flotation aids from anything that can float or hold trapped air, such as plastic bags, bottles, or knotted clothing.
parents.page_153: You are only considered a survivor once you have been rescued, which means you must either achieve self-rescue or be saved by others. Self-rescue may not always be possible due to being lost, adverse conditions like flooding or bad weather, or injuries that prevent movement. When self-rescue is impossible, your priority is to attract attention and make contact with rescuers using available location aids or improvised tools. Never hesitate to seek rescue due to embarrassment; survival is more important than avoiding shame. Always inform others of your plans and expected return time so someone knows to look for you if you do not arrive. Carry the best location aids for your environment and know how to use them effectively to increase your chances of being found. In many situations, a critical decision involves whether to stay put or move to a safer location. Generally, it is advisable to remain where you are because rash attempts to walk out can lead to greater danger.
parents.page_154: A location aid can make the difference between life and death. The Skystreme is a multitasking inflatable silver foil kite that weighs just 11/2 ounces and packs down to a small size. It can perform four tasks in a survival situation: it acts as a location aid by being orally inflated to lift off from the ground unaided in winds of 4 mph, allowing its attached 165 feet of line to raise it above tree levels where it reflects sunlight and is visible to the naked eye at a distance of two miles. At night, you can hang a small flashlight or Cyalume underneath the kite to aid visibility. The metallic surface also reflects radar and can be detected by aircraft at a distance of 10 miles. Additionally, the device functions as an emergency first aid splint when inflated around a broken or sprained limb, serves as a thermal vest when inflated and placed inside clothing to act as a body warmer, and provides water storage when used to carry and store water instead of being inflated with air.
parents.page_155: Once you are out of immediate danger, assess your situation carefully and plan accordingly, as clear thinking at this initial stage can determine the difference between life and death. In most cases, remaining where you are is the preferred option, but every situation is unique; factors such as circumstances, environment, conditions, and your individual state significantly impact what can be achieved. Avoid making rash decisions to walk out of a situation, as this may place you in even greater danger. The rule of threes helps focus decision-making, especially if you are injured, at risk of further injury, or facing immediate environmental threats: three seconds is the psychological reaction time for making a decision, three minutes is the limit your brain can survive without oxygen before suffering irreparable damage, three hours is the critical survival time in extreme climates without protection, three days is the approximate time you can live without water, and three weeks is the approximate time you can live without food. The four priorities of survival are protection, location, water, and food, with the specific priority depending on your situation. If you are no longer in danger from injury or the elements, focus your efforts on establishing a safe location and getting rescued. Use the SURVIVAL acronym framework to remember what you need to do to remain alive and get rescued.
parents.page_156: In a survival situation, seven factors known as the enemies of survival can work against you if you are not mentally prepared: pain, thirst, temperature, fear, fatigue, hunger, and boredom and loneliness. You can memorize these using the mnemonic "Be Prepared To Face These Hostile Factors." When boredom sets in, you become inactive and lose the ability to deal with your situation effectively, so you need to keep busy. Loneliness can add to the magnitude of your tasks, leading to a feeling of helplessness. If you are injured, do not ignore the pain; attend to a minor injury as it could grow into a major problem. A positive attitude coupled with keeping busy helps to distract the mind from pain. Thirst is not a good indicator of the body's need for water, so stay ahead of dehydration and prioritize your need for water according to your environment. You should dress to suit the environment you are in and be aware of the signs and symptoms of temperature-related conditions, such as hypothermia and heat stroke. Fear is one of our body's greatest survival tools since it can stimulate you so that you are ready to act, however, it can also debilitate. Fear is good as long as you have control over it, and the key to controlling fear in a survival situation is knowledge. Tiredness leads to mistakes that, at best, cause frustration, and at worst, may result in injury or death. Never underestimate the importance of quality rest to your physical and mental well-being.
