In a short-term survival situation lasting one to five days, procuring food is not a high priority as long as you drink water and pace yourself to work within your limits. However, take every opportunity to procure food without expending energy.
parents.page_157: Assess the particulars of your surroundings, physical condition, and equipment. If you are in a group, share tasks and responsibilities. The true skill of a survivor is to understand what is required and improvise solutions to particular problems. Determine if you have the skills to be proactive in your own rescue. In any survival situation, it always helps to know where you are so that you can make the best decisions about what to do and where to go next. Observe how indigenous people and local wildlife have developed ways of adapting to their environment to survive. Most people react to a true survival situation through either training or instinct. Listen to your subconscious survival senses and gut feelings. Some people without training and equipment have survived the most horrendous situations simply because they had the will to live. Fear and panic can be formidable enemies, so it is imperative that you have the knowledge and training to counteract them and prevent them from making your situation worse. Learning basic skills increases your chances of survival; without training, your prospects are down to luck, which is never the best place to start.
parents.page_158: Look at how local people dress and act; in hot countries, they leave manual work until the coolest parts of the day and work slowly to reduce sweating and conserve water. In a jungle, pay attention when animals become quiet or quickly leave an area because danger is usually nearby. You may start with all the right equipment, but it may get lost, broken, or wear out, so your ability to improvise will help you survive in relative comfort. Think laterally, like a climber stranded on a mountain who used his camera flash to signal a rescue helicopter. Knowing your precise location clarifies whether rescuers are likely to find you or if you must rescue yourself, and it gives you an idea of the obstacles you may face. Channel your efforts into making sure that when rescuers are looking for you, your aids to location are in place. Whatever has happened, approach your predicament in a calm and rational manner because the situation requires careful thought and planning. If you act in haste, you may overlook important factors, lose vital equipment, or simply make matters worse, as the saying "Undue haste makes waste" is especially true in a survival situation. The stories of prisoners of war often reveal what kept them alive: religious beliefs, thoughts of family and friends, or a determination not to let the enemy win. If the will to live is not there, then just having the knowledge and equipment may not be enough. If uncontrolled, fear and panic can destroy your ability to make rational decisions, causing you to react to your feelings and imagination rather than to your actual situation. If you are in a group, your responses can directly affect others; positive responses are productive and can motivate. Prior preparation is the key to survival: discover what you need to know about the environment you are going to, familiarize yourself with your equipment, and practice your basic skills until they become second nature. This thorough preparation will help you combat the fear of the unknown and increase your self-confidence. Every environment has its own particular characteristics such as hot, dry, cold, or wet, so determine what you need to do to adapt to that environment. Assess how your equipment can be used best and remove yourself and others from danger.
parents.page_159: There are three principles for attracting attention: attract, hold, and direct. To attract, pick a place that maximizes your chances of being seen, such as open or high ground. To hold, maintain the signal until rescuers indicate they have seen you and try to send critical information via a May Day or Help message. To direct, do all you can to guide rescuers to your present location. If you cannot rescue yourself due to injury, being lost, or bad weather, you must use location aids to attract attention. These aids can save your life, so ensure you know how to use them effectively. A helicopter on a search pattern may make only one pass over an area before moving on, so you must act decisively within a few minutes. Location aids include satellites in geostationary earth orbit and low-altitude earth orbit, as well as Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacons used at sea and Emergency Locator Transmitters mostly for use in aircraft.
parents.page_160: Choose a cell phone equipped with a GPS unit to fix and track your position, along with a camera to send pictures of your location and any injuries. Alternatively, purchase a satellite phone connected to the Iridium Satellite Phone System. When activated, a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) transmits a radio distress signal to two complementary satellite systems known as LEOSAR and GEOSAR, which together form the COSPAS-SARSAT system. This signal is relayed to the rescue coordination center closest to the beacon's location. Marine VHF radios are handheld units that transmit and receive on frequencies between 156 to 174 MHz, typically using Channel 16 for international calling and distress. Your marine radio device should be waterproof, able to float, and kept on charge. Always take a cell or satellite phone with you when traveling, and bring a marine VHF radio on sea trips. Personal Locator Beacons are designed for personal use and should always be used responsibly; they are for emergency use only.
parents.page_161: To create a dome signal fire that generates enough smoke to be seen from afar, construct a large fire on a raised platform beneath a dome-shaped structure made from bent saplings. If saplings are unavailable, use poles to form a teepee shape instead. Bend two long saplings at 90 degrees to each other to create the dome frame. Prop two parallel long poles up over a log or rock at one end and secure them with four stakes at the other end to steady the platform. Lash additional poles to each side of the dome for stability. Drive pegs into the ground to anchor the structure further. Lay lengths of green wood side by side on the supporting poles. Prepare tinder, kindling, fuel, and vegetation; fold small dead branches from lower tree trunks into bundles held together by a thin branch or vine. Stab a branch or vine into the bundle, wrap it around, and tuck it under itself to secure the kindling. Place the prepared fire on the platform and load it with green vegetation. Position the vegetation close enough to the fuel below to catch easily when lit, but ensure it is not smothered. Prepare the initial flame using a lighter or matches to ignite the kindling and turn it into a sustained fire. Required tools include a knife, saw, poles, long saplings, green vegetation, tinder, kindling, fuel, and a light source such as a lighter or matches.
parents.page_162: Build three fires in a recognized formation, either as a triangle or a straight line, ensuring they are at least 65 feet apart. Site the firebase off the ground to prevent dampness and allow sufficient airflow for ignition. Keep dry tinder in a waterproof container nearby along with fuel sources such as hexamine tablets, stove fuel, gasoline, paper, or birch bark. Prepare a witch's broom by stuffing a cleft stick with kindling or bark to quickly light and transfer fire to the signal site. Leave an access point for lighting the fire but keep it closed to maintain dryness of tinder, kindling, and fuel. Layer green vegetation on top of the dome to form a roof over the fire platform and keep additional green vegetation nearby to add smoke. Light the witch's broom from your camp fire, take it to the dome, and use it to ignite the signal fire. Use kindling to help start the fire. When smoke billows upward, light a preprepared signal flare taped or tied to a long pole and position the flare as high into the smoke column as possible. Cover the top of the firebase with green vegetation and other smoky materials to keep the base dry while increasing visibility.
parents.page_163: In addition to signal fires, other visual devices can attract rescuers. Whatever device you use, persist with your signal to hold the rescuers' attention. To create a highly visible illuminated windmill effect, attach a chemical lightstick containing cyalume to a cord about three feet long and whirl it in front of you. Depending on local conditions, this signal can be seen up to two miles away or even by an aircraft crew. While whirling the lightstick, blow your whistle using the International Distress Signal of six blasts over one minute followed by one minute of silence; a reply consists of three short blasts. For daylight hours only, you can use a tinsel tree when staying in one place for a while. Cut a silver survival blanket or any reflective material into strips and attach them to a tree so they move in the breeze and catch sunlight, glinting like mirrors.
parents.page_164: In an emergency, proceed in the direction indicated by arrows on signs that require medical assistance or other rescue signals. Signal flares come with one end producing orange smoke for daytime visibility and another end emitting a bright flare for nighttime use; however, the flare end can also be used during the day. Always follow the manufacturer's instructions printed on the outside of the device and wear gloves to protect your hands from heat. Keep the flare away from a liferaft when lighting it to prevent damage, and ensure you do not discard any flares until both ends have been used. Ground-to-air markers can be improvised using items that contrast sharply with the ground, such as orange lifejackets, ensuring the message is large enough to be seen from all directions. You may use internationally recognized emergency codes or write SOS or HELP in large letters to attract attention. Always carry a whistle when venturing into the wilderness because sound travels great distances in areas with little noise pollution; tie the whistle around your neck for easy access. The most recognized signs of distress are a red star shooting up into the sky or a billowing cloud of orange smoke produced by signal flares and rockets. These devices range from simple handheld units to specialized kits capable of penetrating jungle canopy, and you should hold the flare as high as possible to maximize visibility.
parents.page_165: Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location. If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger. You must decide between staying put or moving based on specific conditions: if no one knows you are missing, you must notify people of your plight using any available means, as a rescue party will likely be dispatched if you are missed. If you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons to remain, you must move to a location offering better survival chances or rescue opportunities. Get yourself out of cold, wind, and wet conditions to prevent hypothermia; avoid confrontation with animals by moving away from danger; stabilize injuries and apply first aid. Use cell or satellite phones to inform others of your predicament, and consider using a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) if the situation warrants emergency rescue. Address the core principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food.
parents.page_166: Use your body heat to melt snow, as this process lowers your body temperature and can induce hypothermia. Breathe air onto cold hands because the moisture in your breath will cool down and conduct heat away from them. Avoid sitting or lying directly on the cold ground; instead, use available materials to improvise a sitting or sleeping platform. Select a shelter site away from dangers like avalanches, ensuring it is large enough for you and your equipment. Incorporate a cold sink and keep your sleeping platform higher than the sink. Keep cutting and digging tools in the shelter in case heavy snowfall or an avalanche requires you to dig your way out. Mark the entrance to the shelter so you can find it easily. Maintain a fire once established, as it can be used to melt snow and provide warmth. Prepare all location aids for immediate use. Do not underestimate the need for water in cold environments, as dehydration is just as likely there as in hot ones. Post-holing through virgin snow is exhausting; instead, improvise a pair of snow shoes. Ventilate your shelter and check for natural dangers such as avalanches. Make an informed decision on the best location to move to if you must relocate. Keep hydrated by wrapping snow in a wet item of clothing and sucking it as it melts, but only while walking or working and generating heat. Improvise a walking stick that can be used to check the depth and quality of snow and detect unseen drop-offs. Check regularly for signs of frost nip, frostbite, and hypothermia. Have aids to location accessible while moving and distributed while static. Protect all extremities from the elements by tying gloves to cord threaded through your jacket so they do not get lost. If you cannot survive where you are but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything possible to attract rescue. If your situation changes, such as moving to find help and finding a suitable location to stay, consult the alternative survival guidelines for that new context.
parents.page_167: Before setting off on any journey, verify that you possess the necessary medical equipment, with special attention to required medications. Maintaining personal hygiene is essential as it keeps your body efficient and lowers health risks; note that your physical condition directly influences your psychological state. It is highly recommended to attend a first aid training course and carry a comprehensive manual. Keep your first aid kit dry and easily accessible at all times. Ensure the seals on sterile dressings remain intact and immediately replace any items you use. Always protect yourself from danger, as you cannot assist others if you become a casualty yourself. If an area is unsafe, summon help and monitor the casualty's condition from a safe distance.
parents.page_168: Assess emergency situations quickly and methodically by prioritizing casualties with life-threatening conditions such as unconsciousness or severe bleeding. Determine if a casualty is conscious by checking for responses to voice or gently shaking their shoulders if unsure. Verify the airway is open; if the person can talk, it is clear, but if they cannot speak, check breathing and clear any obstructions. If the casualty is unconscious and not breathing, call for help immediately and begin CPR. Once immediate life threats are managed, conduct a detailed assessment and investigate how the incident occurred to identify likely injuries. Ensure your first aid kit is suited to the specific environment you will visit by including personal medications like painkillers, asthma inhalers, antihistamines, and oral rehydration salts. Add environment-specific items such as malaria tablets, mosquito repellent, sunblock, tick removers, and anti-poison-ivy cream. Stock your basic kit with alcohol-free antiseptic wipes, latex-free gloves, antibiotic eye ointment, various adhesive dressings including fabric, waterproof, and hypoallergenic types, gel blister bandages, sterile pads, roller bandages for joints, triangular bandages, micropore or zinc-oxide tape, scissors, tweezers, safety pins, and disposable syringes.
parents.page_169: Use a combined sterile dressing, which consists of a pad attached to a bandage and is sealed for easy application; it can also serve as a sling and should be taped to your backpack strap for quick access during an emergency. Always wear latex-free disposable gloves, such as nitrile gloves, to prevent cross-infection between yourself and the casualty, as contact with latex can trigger allergic reactions. Clean wounds using alcohol-free antiseptic wipes to avoid irritation while effectively disinfecting the area.
parents.page_170: In an emergency, immobilize and support hand, arm, or shoulder injuries in a raised position. If a triangular bandage is unavailable, use strong cloth approximately three feet square folded in half to create a triangle, or utilize jacket or backpack straps. Have the casualty support their injured arm with their other hand while you secure the sling. To support an injured forearm or hand, fold a jacket over the arm and pin it in place. For button-up jackets, undo a button and slide the injured arm into the opening for support. Support upper arm injuries by looping a belt into a figure-eight pattern to hold the arm raised. Pin your sleeve to your jacket or backpack strap for additional support. Rest a shoulder sprain by tucking your hand into your backpack strap. Most injuries can be dealt with or stabilized using basic first aid and common sense.
parents.page_172: Fatigue and fear are identified as major enemies of survival that must be managed. In any survival situation, basic principles dictate that you should prioritize water, shelter, fire, and food. Water purification is essential; if filters are unavailable, rely on boiling or other purification methods. Fire can be used for warmth, cooking, signaling, protection from insects, and melting ice or snow. Various methods exist to create fire, including using matches, a bow drill, a firesteel, a magnifying glass, or char cloth as tinder. Specific fire types include the Dakota hole, snake hole, teepee, and star-shaped fires, which are particularly useful in desert situations. For lighting, a flashlight is recommended, and petroleum jelly applied to tampons can help waterproof matches. First-aid kits should be carried, containing items like antiseptic wipes. Improvised flotation aids are necessary if you are in the sea or water conditions are poor. Hunger is another enemy of survival; while humans can survive for a significant time without food, emergency rations and mini-fishing kits are valuable tools. Knowledge of your environment, whether it is cold, desert, mountainous, rainforest, temperate, or sea-based, is crucial for planning the worst-case scenario. Flow charts provide guidance for specific conditions such as cold, desert, jungle, mountain, sea, and general wilderness survival. When hiking, follow Naismith's rule for distance calculation and ensure you have proper gear, including hiking boots and a walking stick. Prioritize your gear choice to sustain your load effectively. Heat exhaustion is a risk in hot climates, requiring specific attention to hydration in both hot-dry and hot-humid environments.
parents.page_173: The index provided contains references to various survival topics but does not include specific survival instructions or factual content. It lists subjects such as hypothermia, fire starting, shelter building, navigation, and emergency situations alongside page numbers where these topics are discussed in the full book. Without the actual text from those referenced pages, no actionable survival facts or step-by-step instructions can be extracted. Therefore, this section contains no useful survival information for reconstruction. EMPTY
parents.page_174: Use the natural environment for navigation by observing the night sky, sun, and stars to determine direction and time. Practice setting bearings without a compass and utilize a watch as a navigation aid. Apply specific techniques for off-road driving, including traversing scree, crossing rivers, and navigating sand in desert areas. Master snow navigation methods such as using pulks or toboggans, pulling loads, and adapting to different types of snow and ice. When walking in snow conditions, use improvised snowshoes and be aware of the risks associated with night walking. Manage dehydration carefully when dealing with salt water and ensure proper hydration strategies for both desert and cold environments. Carry extra water specifically for desert areas and understand how temperature acts as an enemy to survival in various climates. Utilize a space blanket for thermal regulation and employ tools found in a survival kit, including needles for repairs and waterproof bandages. Practice coping strategies through team sessions and realistic training with equipment to prepare for survival situations. Understand the importance of ventilation in shelters and learn to construct different types of shelters like A-frames, lean-tos, caves, and trenches suitable for forests, jungles, deserts, and snowy environments. Be aware of carbon monoxide poisoning risks when using certain shelter types or equipment. Use emergency signals such as flares, ground-to-air markers, light windmills, tinsel tree signals, and whistles to attract rescue. Carry a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) and consider using inflatable kites for signaling. Treat stings with credit cards to extract venom and keep a first-aid kit accessible. Follow survival flow charts and adhere to fundamental survival principles while managing sustainment loads effectively.
parents.page_175: Water is essential for survival, with the human body able to survive only a limited time without it. Thirst acts as a critical enemy that must be managed carefully. To obtain water, you can collect rainwater using bamboo, wide-leaf roofs, or drainpipes. Natural sources include dew, cactus plants, fish, melting snow and ice, and underground water found via vines. Plants also serve as significant sources of moisture through transpiration bags. In hot-dry climates, specific techniques like collecting dew from cacti are vital. You can catch rainwater in various ways to ensure a steady supply. For those stranded at sea, blood from sea turtles can be used as an emergency water source. Melting sacks and solar stills are effective methods for extracting water from ice or creating it from humidity. Distillation processes and reverse-osmosis pumps can also purify available water sources. Once collected, water must be purified to avoid water-borne diseases. Boiling is a primary method for disinfection, alongside the use of chlorine tablets, iodine liquid, and potassium permanganate. Bleach can also be used for purification purposes. Bottle filters, gravity or pressure filters, tripod filters, and Millbank bag filters provide mechanical filtration options. Solar stills combine distillation with natural heat to purify water. UV light is another modern method for disinfecting water supplies. Desalination techniques allow you to convert saltwater into fresh water when necessary. Always avoid drinking saltwater directly as it accelerates dehydration. When planning survival, consider the quantity of water needed and implement rationing strategies to stretch supplies. In emergency situations, having a sustainment kit with stored water is crucial. Flash floods can contaminate water sources, so be aware of wildlife dangers that may also affect water availability. Proper camp craft in adverse weather conditions helps protect your water collection systems. Always prioritize finding safe water sources before addressing other survival needs like food or shelter.
parents.page_177: Treat the wilderness with respect by carrying in only what you are prepared to carry out, leaving only footprints, and taking only pictures.

children.search_text: Four essential elements must be present for a survival situation to have a chance of a positive outcome: knowledge, ability, the will to survive, and luck.
While knowledge and ability can be learned, the will to survive is hard-wired into our survival mechanism and may not be recognized until tested.
People who are fully trained and well-equipped have sometimes given up hope in survivable conditions, whereas others who were less prepared have survived against all odds by refusing to give up.

Anyone venturing into the wilderness should understand basic survival principles to carry out correct beforehand preparation, choose the right equipment, learn how to use it, and practice necessary skills.
Knowing how to survive in a particular situation allows you to assess your situation, prioritize your needs, and improvise items of gear that you do not have.
For example, while you may be able to start a fire using a lighter, you must know what to do if it stops working.
Similarly, anyone can spend a comfortable night inside a one-man bivi shelter, but you must know what to do if you lose your pack.
Survival knowledge and skills must be learned and practiced under realistic conditions because starting a fire with dry materials on a sunny day teaches very little.
The real survival skill is in understanding why a fire won't start and working out a solution.
The more you practice, the more you learn, as finding solutions and overcoming problems continually adds to your knowledge and helps you deal with similar problems should they occur again.
Always apply the principle of expending the least amount of energy for the maximum amount of gain..
Research the environment you are preparing for to understand both its attractions and its dangers, as there is a thin line between awe and being at nature's mercy.
Fully understanding an environment allows you to select appropriate equipment and know how to use it effectively when needed.
Never underestimate the power of nature regardless of your equipment quality or skill level.
If plans fail, stop immediately to reassess your situation and priorities.
Do not hesitate to turn back and try again later, as the challenge will remain available tomorrow.
The most effective survival strategy is to avoid entering a survival situation in the first place..
Never assume that the amount of prior preparation required is directly related to the length of a trip or its perceived danger.
Most survival situations arise either from being thrust into an uncontrollable scenario or developing from a sequence of events that could have been avoided by recognizing danger signs early.
Unfortunately, many such situations occur due to ignorance, arrogance, or underestimating the forces of nature.

Whether preparing for an overnight camping trip or a year-long journey, the more prepared you are for the specific environment, the more likely you will be able to cope physically and mentally if confronted with a survival situation.
It is equally important to choose the correct equipment and clothing.
When selecting gear, consider what you would need to survive if the worst happens.
This constitutes your "first-line" equipment, which you should carry at all times.

It must enable you to address the basic principles of survival relevant to your environment and consists of the clothing you wear and your basic survival equipment, such as a survival tin and belt order.
Before heading off, double-check that your gear works and ensure you know how to use it properly.
The more you understand how and why a piece of equipment works, the better able you will be to improvise if it gets damaged or lost..
A walking stick is one of the simplest yet most important survival aids you will ever need and should be the first piece of equipment to improvise if you find yourself in a survival situation.
Known as the survivor's third leg, it increases your ability to support yourself by allowing you to have two points of contact with the ground at any one time.
This reduces the chance of slipping, which is crucial because your ability to walk may be your main means of rescue; reducing your mobility seriously reduces your ability to survive.

Your walking stick can be employed in many different survival situations including supporting you as you walk, protecting your face when walking through thickets or gorse, checking for adequate support when crossing marshy ground, testing the ground ahead for obstructions, checking the depth of water when crossing streams and rivers, protecting you against wild animals, forming a ridgepole for your shelter, helping with pace counting, spearing fish or catching game, and digging up roots or plants..
The pre-impact period involves recognizing threats and warnings where individuals may respond with denial or over-activity.

The impact period is life-threatening, during which about ten to twenty percent of people remain calm and aware, up to seventy-five percent become stunned and unable to react rationally, and ten to twenty-five percent exhibit extreme behaviors like screaming.
If the recoil period fails to restore normal reasoning and emotional expression within three hours to three days, individuals may develop psychiatric disorders.

The post-trauma period follows directly after impact, often lasting between three hours and three days, characterized by a gradual return to normal abilities but also marked by guilt, depression, anxiety, and aimlessness known as Post-Traumatic Stress Disorders.

Understanding these psychological reactions is crucial because mental breakdown compromises survival chances; contrary to popular belief, panic does not normally occur unless contagious, though psychogenic shock from moving into unknown situations requires mental preparation to manage stress effectively..
Panic arises from the fear of potential outcomes and often occurs when individuals are trapped or face a time limit for escape.
Depressed victims may sit amidst chaos, gaze vacantly, fail to respond to questions, remain unaware of their situation, and be unable to help themselves, thereby risking further injury.
Hyperactive victims are easily distracted, talk excessively, generate many ideas, and frequently offer unhelpful suggestions.
Aggression, anger, and hostility are common reactions that can be irrational and may even be directed at rescuers or medical staff.
Some survivors feel guilty for having survived while others did not, and some irrationally blame themselves for causing the incident.
There have been cases where disaster victims committed suicide immediately after being rescued, so all rescued individuals must be monitored closely.
Most people who die from psychological trauma do so within the first three days following the event.
Individual reactions to survival situations vary, but common emotional responses include panic, depression, hyperactivity, anger, and guilt, which may occur during or after any survival experience..

Mental preparation is essential for survival, as reactions to disaster stem from direct psychological shocks or aggravating factors that must be identified and avoided to maximize chances of overcoming problems.
Hunger initially poses little threat but leads to significant psychological changes after a long-term lack of food, resulting in apathy, irritability, depression, and an inability to concentrate.
Thirst is a more acute problem than hunger, particularly for survivors at sea or in the desert, often causing agitation, irrational behavior, delusions, and visual hallucinations.

Seasickness can create an overwhelming desire to give up, which can become fatal; to combat it, keep a fixed point like the horizon in sight and take small sips of fresh water if supplies allow, though these must be rationed in life-or-death situations.
Both hunger and thirst produce physical and psychological effects that lead to loss of concentration, memory impairment, motor dysfunction, faulty decision-making, and irrational behavior.

Physical exhaustion may exist from the start or develop due to sleep deprivation and prolonged hardship; survivors often find themselves unable to relax when they need sleep, causing fatigue to overwhelm them and deteriorate both mental and physical performance..
Mental preparation is a critical component of survival, involving the development of coping strategies and psychological readiness.
Prior knowledge serves as power, enabling individuals to handle survival situations more effectively than those who are unprepared.
The will to survive is defined as refusing to accept death and involves overcoming extreme emotional and physical discomfort while setting and working toward specific goals.
Maintaining hope is vital despite negative information or perceptions, as positive thinking helps ward off psychological trauma.

Before acting, monitor your own condition to ensure you are capable of the task and observe others closely to identify those who are genuinely disturbed; offer simple words of comfort to help numb individuals become more responsive, but avoid administering sedatives.
Acceptance of a situation does not mean giving in to it; instead, knowing when to be active or passive can improve survival chances.
A powerful motivating force is the desire to reunite with principal figures of attachment, such as spouses, partners, children, grandchildren, and close friends..

Plan your journey carefully regardless of whether it is a short day trip or a long expedition, and maintain a basic planning outline for regular trips that you can expand with supplementary details as needed.
Minimize regret by anticipating potential problems and risks, planning to avoid them, and equipping yourself with the necessary knowledge and tools to handle them.
Prioritize preparation to prevent poor performance, as understanding survival principles and knowing how to improvise can determine your fate in a crisis.
Start organizational planning with essential show-stoppers such as money, passports, visas, vaccinations, tickets, and insurance.
If traveling with a group, consider team dynamics by practicing on shorter trips beforehand to refine equipment usage and group organization.
Research the social customs of every country you visit to avoid penalties, punishment, or imprisonment.

When your team includes both men and women, plan specific arrangements for sleeping, washing, carrying loads, and responsibilities rather than assuming traditional gender roles like women cooking and men building shelters..
Train yourself mentally and physically before departing on any journey to ensure you can operate within your capabilities, which allows you to fully enjoy the experience.
Practice with all equipment under realistic conditions that mimic the actual trip environment to identify strengths, weaknesses, and personal limitations.
Before setting off, assess the specific skills required and confirm you are capable of meeting those demands.
When testing new gear, simulate real-world scenarios such as operating a GPS while wearing gloves in cold weather or pitching a tent in darkness and rain to verify functionality.
Replicate your intended trip conditions during training by duplicating the environment, distance, weight load, and language barriers you will face.
Gradually increase the weight you carry until it matches what you plan to take on the trip, as this conditions your body and helps you determine essential items.
Research weather extremes for your destination, noting that areas like deserts can be hot during the day but drop below freezing at night.
Train for the specific daily distance you intend to cover to assess if it is achievable and sustainable.
If traveling to a country where your native language is not widely spoken, learn useful phrases beforehand or bring a phrase book and electronic translator..
Even meticulously planned trips can encounter unexpected difficulties such as strong winds or injuries, so it is essential to prepare an emergency plan of action.
Rescue services operate more effectively when they possess all relevant information about your situation.
You should keep people informed of your intended whereabouts and itinerary dates so they can raise the alarm if you deviate from your plans.

Write down your trip details including pertinent places and dates, create a standardized emergency plan of action that considers your worst-case scenario, give a copy to your next of kin and group members, keep a copy for yourself, and inform local services like park ranger stations of your plans.

Your emergency plan should contain up-to-date information about you, such as a recent photograph, passport details, the equipment you are carrying, and the languages you speak, especially if traveling in another country.
If you go missing, this information allows rescue groups to understand your intent and plan effectively, enabling them to find and help you quicker..

Before departing, ensure you have a complete emergency plan of action including your full name as it appears on your passport, current contact details such as phone numbers and email addresses, and the names and locations of next of kin.
List all necessary medications, such as anti-malaria tablets, and note any severe allergies like reactions to amoxicillin.
Record specific trip details including designated campsites with their grid coordinates, vehicle descriptions with license plate numbers, and the names of group members.

Establish a clear communications plan that outlines when you will contact family or friends, acknowledging potential limitations in cell reception on the trail, and provide alternative contact numbers for yourself and your companions.
Define a daily itinerary specifying which campsite to visit each day and whether to park vehicles at specific locations.
Identify foreseeable problems or intentions for each day of the trek and designate emergency rendezvous points such as ranger stations with their grid coordinates and telephone numbers.

Include personal identification details like passport number, expiration date, distinguishing marks including scars and tattoos, date of birth, driver's license information, languages spoken, height, weight, hair color, and swimming ability.
Document your outdoor skills and experience, noting any military survival training or bushcraft courses attended to ensure readiness for living outdoors in the field..
Before heading into the wilderness, fully prepare by researching how native inhabitants dress, work, and eat to understand their adaptations.

This knowledge helps you select the best gear, adopt effective techniques, and learn correct survival skills, which is crucial since many survival situations result from avoidable sequences of events.
Always prioritize water over food because your body needs water to digest food..
The four basic principles of survival are protection, location, water, and food, which should generally be prioritized in that order.
Water is critical; while you can survive a few days without it, your mental and physical performance will decline significantly within 24 hours.
If you are injured, exposed to hot weather, or have heavy workloads, you may only survive a few hours without water.
Your location determines your chances of survival and rescue.
You typically have two options: stay put or move.
Staying is preferred if you can mark your location to aid rescuers.
If staying is unsafe due to imminent danger, move to a location that offers better survival odds or rescue opportunities.
Choose a spot that maximizes your ability to attract attention.
Food becomes crucial as the duration of the survival situation increases.
You can survive five to seven days without food even with moderate workload, though you will feel hungry, become tired, experience slowed movements, and lose the body's ability to repair itself.
Unless you are already malnourished, starvation is unlikely within a week.
Protection involves staying in a condition that allows you to remain proactive for continued survival and rescue.
Physically, protect yourself from injury, environmental elements, and wildlife.
Mentally, guard against fear, guilt, and depression that can diminish your will to live.
Lighting and maintaining a fire is the best way to achieve protection, as it offers physical safety while providing a sense of security and familiarity that helps normalize dire situations..
Surviving in temperate areas is generally favorable due to mild climates and abundant natural resources, though rivers, lakes, and swampy wetlands are common where drainage is poor.
The greatest threat in these regions is hypothermia, particularly during winter and at night.
In rainforest environments, while resources are plentiful, heat, humidity, dangerous animals, and dense vegetation create challenging conditions.
Mosquitoes pose a significant fatality risk, making the identification of edible plants crucial to avoid poisonous species.
Navigation is difficult due to dense undergrowth, increasing the danger of getting lost.

Essential survival practices in rainforests include making noise to scare away most animals who wish to avoid you, boiling or treating all water to prevent illness, keeping covered and washing frequently to combat infections caused by high humidity, always building shelters and sleeping off the ground, keeping any found dry tinder protected from moisture, and following rivers downhill as they typically lead toward civilization and the coast..
Climate and terrain can vary widely, so prepare for a range of eventualities.
Weather can change quickly, so check the local forecast before you set off and carry an AM/FM radio to listen to local weather reports.
Plan a realistic route and prepare an EPA, being ready to reassess your route during the trip.
Take clothing suitable for all possible conditions.
Carry a survival can, knife, emergency equipment, cell phone, and first-aid kit, and learn how to use them.
Carry adequate water and the equipment needed to purify more if necessary.
Carry some form of basic shelter even if only going out for the day.
Take a map and compass, and consider using a GPS as an aid..
In cold environments, natural resources are often scarce, making survival dependent on your equipment and supplies.
While shelters can be dug from snow, fire essentials are limited in polar and tundra areas.
The greatest dangers include hypothermia and polar bears in northern regions.
Survival is more feasible in the taiga where wood, fresh water, and edible flora and fauna are available.
To prevent hypothermia and exposure, dress in loose-fitting layers of clothing to avoid overheating while ensuring your clothes stay dry and clean.
If your hands become cold, do not warm them with your breath as this will make them wet; instead, tuck them under your armpits.
Avoid sitting directly on the ground, snow, or ice; sit on your pack or create a sleeping platform using boughs to conserve body heat.
Regularly check your face, toes, hands, and ears for frost nip, which is the first stage of frostbite.
Take shelter from the wind whenever possible, especially during survival situations.
Ensure your shelter is ventilated by keeping vent holes clear and checking them frequently, particularly during heavy snowfalls.
If fire is your primary source of warmth, triple the amount of firewood you think you need to ensure it lasts the night.
Survival in the desert requires full preparation due to hostile temperatures and scarce natural resources.
Always prepare an Emergency Plan of Action (EPA) to notify someone of your plans before entering a desert area.
Carry extra water and bring equipment designed to maximize your chances of procuring more water.
If your vehicle breaks down, leave it only if staying there is no longer safe or feasible.
When venturing into remote areas, augment your map and compass with a GPS, and consider taking a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) or satellite phone.
The greatest dangers in the desert are dehydration and heat exhaustion.
While African savanna areas may contain dangerous mammals, desert regions are also home to a range of venomous snakes..
Surviving at sea requires relying on your supplies since natural resources in the open ocean are virtually nonexistent.
Improvising shelter or location aids is limited to what you carry.
Seasickness, particularly in a small liferaft, can impair your ability to stay hydrated and motivated.
Your only drinking water supply comes from rain or mechanical desalination devices.
Reaching shipping lanes greatly increases your chances of rescue.
To increase survival chances at sea, pack sea-survival equipment as if your life depends on it.
Take emergency immersion-survival suits to protect yourself and aid flotation and location.
Always carry a Personal Locator Beacon.
Bring several means of obtaining water.
Do not abandon your vessel until absolutely necessary.
Carry anti-seasickness tablets.
In the mountains, survival prospects are good at lower elevations where trees, rivers, and edible plants and animals are likely present.
At higher elevations, resources are fewer, and the risk of avalanches, crevasses, and cold-related injuries poses the greatest threat..
High altitude and lower oxygen levels place higher-than-normal demands on your body and equipment.
Respect nature and err on the side of caution, as rescue is unlikely to be quick or easy on a mountain.
Plan an achievable route and prepare an emergency plan.
Dress in layers; start a walk lightly dressed for cold conditions and add or remove layers as necessary.
Wear a hat and gloves.
Take a flashlight because weather changes and unforeseen problems may mean you are on the mountain in darkness.
Carry an avalanche transceiver when traversing avalanche-prone slopes..
Before departing on a trip, weigh your specific gear requirements against the limitations of your chosen mode of transportation.
Forward planning is essential when deciding what equipment to take; assess your personal needs, the likely weather and terrain, and the amount of gear you can transport.
The environment of the region will dictate the type of equipment needed, but your proposed mode of transportation is the main constraint on the quantity of gear.
Additional weight makes traveling uncomfortable, consumes valuable energy, slows progress, and limits the distance you can travel each day.
Organize and prioritize your gear so that any items needed in a survival scenario are always close at hand.
Carry essential items such as a compass and flashlight around your neck for easy access.
Attach a neck cord to sunglasses for security.
Wear a hat for protection from the sun.
Always keep a map on hand and store a waterproof copy in a survival tin.
Keep the survival tin in a secure pocket.
Strap a water bottle outside your day pack for easy access.
Keep a cell phone in a secure pocket or in a waterproof bag attached to a neck cord..
Before venturing out, you must categorize your equipment into three distinct lines based on their criticality to survival.
First-line gear constitutes your basic survival equipment and includes essential outdoor clothing, navigation tools, and safety items.

This category specifically comprises your bushcraft knife, firesteel, and belt pouch, which should be worn on your person so that if you must abandon the rest of your load, these are the only items available to help you survive.
You must constantly risk-assess your situation and adapt your gear priorities as conditions change.
Second-line gear is required to stay safe under normal conditions and should be carried at all times.
Examples include a spare set of clothes, a bivouac sack, cordage, emergency rations, a first-aid kit, a hexamine stove with items for making hot drinks, a matchless fireset, and a metal cup.
Third-line gear, also known as your sustainment load, is equipment needed to keep yourself alive and function for longer periods.
The amount of third-line gear you carry depends on your mode of transportation and carrying capacity.

This category includes a form of shelter like a tent or tarpaulin, cooking utensils such as a stove or pot, a backpack, food stores, a sleeping bag and mat, large water containers or hydration packs, and wash kits with sanitary items.
Regardless of the total amount of gear you take, organize every item into these three categories according to their importance to ensure essential items are always accessible.
When packing your gear, follow the rule of reverse order: pack the things you will need first as the last items in your bag..
Do not be tempted to overdress, as sweat can soak you just like rain and sudden temperature changes can cause hypothermia.
Instead, wear layers to control your body temperature.
Modern outdoor clothing is lightweight, hard-wearing, and versatile; choose fabrics suited to your specific environment.
Layering traps air between layers to help you stay warm in any condition.
Wearing the correct layers in the right order is essential.
Use wicking fabrics like polypropylene for both hot and cold weather.
Several light layers are better than one heavy layer because multiple layers give you the flexibility to fine-tune your temperature by adding or removing them.
Good insulators include wool, fleece, microfleece, and down.

The layering system consists of three parts: the outer layer repels rain while allowing sweat to escape, the mid-layers provide warmth with materials like fleece that retain heat even when wet, and the base layer wicks moisture away from your skin.
Wear lightweight, full-length pants to shade your skin from the sun.
Choose the right boots and wear a hat to prevent excessive loss of body heat through your head..
Footwear selection should prioritize personal needs such as foot shape and required support, while also considering the distance, terrain, and cost.
Before purchasing boots, wear them around the house and go on short hikes to ensure they are properly broken in.
A good boot sole features a treaded outsole for traction, cushioning under the heel and toe for comfort, and shock-absorbing properties.
Lightweight boots combine fabric and leather hybrids to offer the support and traction of heavy shoes with the flexibility of sports shoes.
Hiking boots are typically made of durable, water-resistant leather that balances weight, durability, and protection.

Jungle-trekking boots utilize rot-proof leather and canvas with a directly molded sole; they often include holes in the instep for ventilation and moisture drainage, along with high uppers to keep mud off pants.
When packing a backpack, place items needed first at the bottom of the pack and heavy items close to your back to prevent the pack from pulling away from your shoulders.
Store daily essentials in outside pockets, use a cloth bag for personal items, keep rain gear at the top of the pack, and carry an upright, accessible water bottle.
The lower compartment should hold sleeping bags and mats.

Additional features to look for include flexible breathable uppers that dry quickly, cushioning pads to absorb shock, insoles that keep feet in position, padded ankles for comfort and support, wide deep studs for grip, and full-length shank plates for rigidity..
If you are in a group, try to help any others who are in danger.
If no one knows you are missing or where you are, you must notify people of your plight by any means available.
If you are missed, a rescue party will almost certainly be despatched to find you.
If you have a cell or satellite phone, let someone know your predicament immediately.
If your situation calls for an emergency rescue and you have a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), consider using it.
You must address the principles of survival: Protection, Location, Water, and Food.

Evaluate whether you can survive where you are; if no one knows your location and you cannot survive there without physical reasons to stay, move to a location that offers a better chance of survival or rescue.
If you face extreme elements, find or improvise shelter immediately.
Avoid confrontation with animals.
Stabilize any injuries and apply first aid..
Do not leave food at your campsite as it attracts predators.
Avoid sheltering too deeply in the woods; your location must remain visible for rescue.
Do not eat unidentified food to prevent illness, especially since food is not a priority in short-term survival situations.
Before building a shelter, inspect the site for hazards such as insects, flooding, rock falls, wild animals, and deadfalls.
Inventory and ration all your supplies.
Keep a fire going to purify water, maintain warmth, and signal for rescue.
Fill plastic bags or spare clothing with dry foliage to use as an insulated mattress or pillow against cold or damp ground.
If in a group, assign tasks to everyone to keep them occupied and reduce worry.
Do not ignore your fire; instead, constantly look for dry tinder, kindling, and fuel.
When moving, walk faster than the slowest person in your group but be extremely careful walking downhill to avoid fatal twisted ankles.
Regulate clothing to avoid overheating while moving and hypothermia when stationary.
Use or improvise a walking stick to reduce trips and falls.
Improvise shelter when not moving.
Plan your route around potential or known water sources and filter or purify all water where possible.
Keep location aids accessible while moving and when deployed statically.
If you cannot survive in place but also cannot move due to injury, do everything possible to attract rescue.
If your situation changes from staying put to moving to find help, consult the alternative survival guidelines..
A basic survival kit is an essential item for any outdoor expedition and should be compact enough to carry at all times.
Its contents must address the key principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food.
You can buy ready-made kits, but you should always adapt the contents to your specific needs and the environment you will be in.
Items in your kit should be high-quality and multi-purpose.
Choose a tin with a waterproof seal and locking clasps for storage.
Your kit should contain blister medical pads and bandages, including a range of both waterproof and fabric options in various sizes; waterproof bandages can also mend holes in tents and tarpaulins.
Include antiseptic wipes for treating wounds and cleaning bites, noting that they can be ignited by a spark to start a fire.
Carry water purification tablets, choosing between puritabs or iodine, but avoid iodine if you are allergic to it or to shellfish; remember that water must be filtered before being purified.
Include petroleum jelly to apply to chapped lips, rashes, and sores, and store it in a small resealable plastic bag as it can also be smeared onto tampons to make them burn longer.
Pack waterproof notepaper for drawing maps or leaving messages.
Include a photograph of loved ones as a psychological incentive in a survival scenario.
Carry a credit card which helps extract insect stings, and include money wrapped in cellophane..
Space blankets or aluminum foil serve multiple purposes including signaling, providing shelter, carrying water, and cooking.
Many are dual-sided with one silver side and another green for camouflage or orange to stand out.
Plastic bags can function as water carriers or transpiration bags.
Medicines such as painkillers and antibiotics provide essential basics if separated from main gear.
A small candle offers a reliable flame to build fires around.
Nylon tights provide warmth or act as improvised water filters, mosquito nets, or fishing nets.
Small AM/FM radios, whether battery or solar-powered, are useful for communication.
Surgical tubing can be used to procure water.
Fire cans or matchless fire sets offer self-contained methods for starting fires.
Sailmaker's needles are multi-purpose tools that can serve as arrow points or mend tents and tarpaulins; wide eyes are preferred.
Safety pins secure clothing or mend sleeping bags and tents.
Mini glowsticks provide emergency lighting and location aid.
Non-lubricated condoms can carry water or act as waterproof covers for smaller items like cell phones.
Mini fishing kits are useful near water, with fishing line adaptable for other tasks including hooks, flies, swivels, and split-shots.
Waterproof matches and tinder balls start fires and should be stored in small resealable plastic bags.
A pencil sharpened at both ends is versatile.
Potassium permanganate dissolves in water to sterilize it, clean wounds, and make fire.
Flashlights, specifically small white and red Photon lights taped in the off position, provide illumination.
Mini multi-tools include useful features like compasses and saws.
High-viz cards and signal mirrors act as location aids.
Compasses serve as emergency back-ups.
Flint, fire steel, and tampons are used for starting fires.
Survival saws or pocket chainsaws can be wrapped around the inside of a tin or cut in half if space is limited.
Single-edged razors function as multi-purpose tools.
Needle and thread sets using strong waxed cotton pre-threaded through the needle are essential for repairs..
Before setting off on an expedition, ensure you have a basic understanding of how to read a map and use a compass.
Assessing a map of your intended area allows you to make informed decisions while preparing for your trip.
Understanding the local area and terrain reduces your chances of getting lost and enables you to continually evaluate your progress, allowing you to alter your plans as necessary.
You will be able to plan the safest and most appropriate route, locate water and shelter, and identify areas where you can use location aids properly.
Proficiency with a map and compass eliminates worry about getting lost or straying off-track, freeing you to enjoy your outdoor experience.

In a survival situation, effective navigation—whether using a map and compass or natural features—plays a major role in decision-making regarding whether to stay put for rescue or move to a safer area.
While a Global Positioning System (GPS) is an excellent aid, it relies on batteries and technology that can fail, so do not rely on it exclusively.
Before venturing into an unfamiliar environment, carefully research the type of terrain you will encounter and investigate the best methods for traveling safely and efficiently across it.
Checking the weather forecast before you go allows you to evaluate conditions and make informed decisions prior to travel.
The ability to assess your situation and modify your plans means that you will be able to avoid many potential survival situations..

To test your ability to navigate using bearings and pace counting, select an open area where you can walk at least 330 feet or 100 meters in any direction without cheating by heading directly toward a marker.
Begin by placing a marker on the ground as your starting point.
Dial a specific bearing onto your compass, such as 110 degrees, and walk along this line while counting your paces until you estimate you have traveled the full distance, then stop.
Next, add 120 degrees to your original bearing to calculate a new direction, dial this updated bearing onto your compass, and pace out another 330 feet or 100 meters before stopping again.
Repeat this process one final time by adding another 120 degrees to the previous bearing, dialing the resulting angle, such as 350 degrees, and walking the same distance.
If you have maintained your bearings accurately and counted your paces correctly, you should arrive back at your original starting point..
Maps serve as two-dimensional representations of three-dimensional terrain, allowing you to determine distance and elevation while helping you visualize an area for easier navigation.
Every topographic map includes a legend or key that explains the symbols used; this is essential for interpreting what features are shown.

The scale indicates the ratio between the map size and actual ground distance, with a 1:25,000 scale offering the most useful level of detail for hikers, where 4 centimeters on the map equals 1 kilometer on the ground.
A smaller scale like 1:50,000 provides only a basic overview.

Contour lines depict height above sea level, colors indicate ground type, and specific symbols represent natural features such as water, mud, vegetation, vertical faces or cliffs, boulders, outcrops, coniferous trees, non-coniferous trees, bracken, heath, and rough grassland.
Additionally, hiking paths are clearly marked on these maps to aid in route finding..
Maps display height above sea level in meters using contour lines, which connect points of equal elevation to show detailed topography.
The vertical distance between these lines, known as the contour interval, is typically 5 meters on scale 1:25,000 maps but may be 10 meters on mountain maps.

To estimate distances accurately, use the map's scale; for curved routes around obstacles, curve a piece of string along the path and transfer it to the scale line, or use solder wire for greater accuracy as it maintains its shape while remaining flexible.
Alternatively, lay a piece of paper between two points, mark the route, and measure it against the scale.
On 1:25,000 maps, each grid square represents 1 kilometer, with diagonal travel covering approximately 1.5 kilometers.
To plan energy-efficient routes, follow contour lines to bypass hills rather than walking up and down steep slopes.
Grid references are determined by reading easting numbers first, followed by northing numbers; for example, a shaded area might have a four-figure reference of 2046.
For greater precision, divide the grid square into tenths mentally to create a six-figure reference, such as 185445.
Vertical grid lines are called eastings because their values increase toward the east, while horizontal lines are called northings..
To navigate effectively, you must be able to orient your map to the land using a compass.
A compass works by using a magnetized needle that rotates freely to align with the North and South magnetic poles.
The four cardinal points are North at 0° or 360°, East at 90°, South at 180°, and West at 270°.
Always hold the compass level to allow the needle to rotate easily.
Never use a compass near metal objects, other magnetized items, electric currents like overhead power lines, or inside buildings and vehicles as these cause interference..
Setting your map allows you to read the terrain and predict features as you move, helping you realize quickly if you are heading off course.
Rotate the compass dial so that "N" sits under the index line.
Lay your map flat on the ground and ensure nothing nearby affects your compass.
Place the compass on the map so its edge runs parallel with a vertical grid line, known as an easting.
Keep the orienting lines aligned with the grid lines on the map and rotate the entire map until the north magnetic needle sits inside the orienting arrow.
The map is now set to magnetic north and should align with features around you.
If the magnetic variation in your area exceeds 5 degrees, compensate accordingly.
Map legends refer to north in three ways: true north, grid north, and magnetic north.
True north is the direction of a meridian of longitude converging on the North Pole.
Grid north runs parallel to vertical grid lines on a map and differs from true north because maps are flat.
Magnetic north is the direction indicated by a magnetic compass.
The angle between magnetic north and grid north is called magnetic variation, which is given in the map legend.
When converting a magnetic bearing to a grid bearing or vice versa, you must adjust for magnetic variation.
If the variation is west, use the phrases "Mag to grid—get rid" or "Grid to mag—add." If the variation is east, the opposite applies.
Check your map's legend to establish the magnetic variation, which depends on your location and whether it is east or west of grid north.
If the variation is 0 degrees, make no adjustments.
When converting a magnetic bearing to a grid bearing with a 12 degree west variation, subtract 12 degrees.
With an east variation, add it on.
When converting a grid bearing to a magnetic bearing with a 12 degree west variation, add 12 degrees.
With an east variation, take it off..
Always give your full attention to any compass work, as rushing can lead to navigational errors that may cause you to get lost.
To navigate using a map and compass, lay your map on a flat surface and ensure nothing nearby interferes with the compass reading.
Use your compass as a protractor to calculate your bearing by laying the edge of the compass between your starting point and your destination.
Ensure the direction of travel arrow on the compass points toward your destination on the map.

You can also find your position by taking a bearing from a known feature back to yourself; this is done by taking a normal bearing and either adding or subtracting 180 degrees, or by reading the bearing exactly opposite to the index line.
Never underestimate the combined power of a map, compass, and the skills and knowledge required to use them well, as your life may depend on it..
To take a bearing using a map, first align the orienting lines on your compass with the vertical grid lines on the map.
Read the bearing between your two points from the index line on the compass.
Convert this grid bearing to a magnetic bearing by consulting the magnetic variation information in the map legend; add or subtract the appropriate figure and adjust your compass accordingly.
Hold the compass level at chest height so you can look squarely down at it.
Turn your body until the north end of the compass needle sits inside the orienting arrow.
The direction of travel arrow will now point exactly in the direction you need to walk..
You may need to take a bearing to a specific point to navigate toward it, even if you can see the feature initially but might lose sight of it later due to terrain.
You can also plot bearings on a map to determine your own position.
To transfer a compass bearing from a feature onto a map, first adjust for magnetic variation.
For example, if the magnetic variation is 12 degrees west and your magnetic bearing is 45 degrees, subtract the variation to get a grid bearing of 33 degrees.
Dial this revised bearing into your compass.

When taking a bearing on a feature, point your compass at the feature, hold the base plate steady, and rotate the housing until the orienting arrow sits directly under the north needle; this gives you the magnetic bearing.

To transfer the bearing onto a map, place the top left corner of the compass base plate over the feature on the map, ensure both the map and compass are level, then rotate the entire compass until the orienting lines are parallel with the vertical grid lines on the map.
Finally, draw a line from the feature along the left side of the base plate to mark your bearing on the map..

If you are unsure of your location but can recognize features on the ground that appear on your map, use a compass to take bearings on these features and transfer them onto the map to determine your position accurately; this method is known as triangulation or resection in military terminology.
If you are not at a known feature, identify at least two recognizable features on the ground that are at least 0.6 mile (1 km) apart and separated by an angle of at least 40 degrees.

Take compass bearings to these features, convert them into back bearings, and draw lines from the corresponding points on your map extending them until they cross; the intersection point indicates your exact location.
For greater accuracy, repeat this process using a third feature.

If you are already located on or near a known feature such as a river, road, or track, take a bearing on another recognizable feature like a church and mark it on the map; the point where this line crosses your known feature reveals your specific position..
There are several methods for calculating distance while hiking, and experienced hikers typically use at least two simultaneously.
Pace counting involves knowing the number of paces required to cover a specific distance and then counting them during travel.

You can mark progress by cutting notches in a walking stick or moving a rubber band down a notch every 100 meters, or by placing pebbles in a pocket for every 100 meters traveled; most people take about 60 paces or 120 steps per 100 meters.
Alternatively, use your map to identify key features along your route and calculate the distance between them.
As you pass these features, mentally check them off or mark your progress on the map to track total distance traveled.
To estimate hike duration using Naismith's rule, allow one hour for every three miles or five kilometers of travel.
Add 30 minutes for every 1,000 feet or 300 meters of climbing.
Subtract 10 minutes for every 1,000 feet or 300 meters of descending, but add 10 minutes instead if the slopes are very steep.
Break your route down into small sections for focused navigation and ensure each section includes a water source and a safety point..
If a straight-line path is blocked by an obstacle, use a compass to calculate four ninety-degree turns to walk around it.
Count your paces during the first and third detours so you can return to your original route.
When aiming for a specific destination like a footbridge, deliberately aim off to one side rather than directly at it; this ensures you know which direction to turn once you reach the target area.
To conserve energy while crossing hilly terrain, walk along contours by maintaining the same height around features instead of climbing up and down repeatedly.
Follow long linear features such as rivers or roads that run in your general direction of travel, as these simplify navigation.
When navigating to a difficult-to-locate point, aim for a nearby prominent feature first, calculate the distance and bearing from it, and use pacing to reach the exact destination..
If you lack a compass, you can improvise one using magnetized ferrous metal such as a needle, razor blade, straightened paperclip, small nail, or staple; thinner materials are easier to magnetize.
To magnetize the item, stroke it repeatedly along its length in one direction with a magnet, ensuring the end that attracts to the south pole of the magnet will point north.
Once magnetized, the needle must be able to turn freely without interference from wind or other elements.
You can suspend the magnetized blade by attaching it to a cotton thread and placing it inside a plastic bottle, removing the bottle base if the neck is too narrow for the blade.
Alternatively, allow the needle to float freely in water while protecting it from external disturbances.
Always carry a magnet with you but keep it away from your actual compass to prevent accuracy issues.
Essential tools for this process include a knife, a magnet, or a battery with wire and tape..
To magnetize a needle using electricity, wrap it in a small piece of paper to insulate it from electrical current.
Wrap insulated wire tightly around the full length of the needle and attach it to a battery with at least 1.5 volts until the battery becomes warm, indicating the process is complete.
Ensure no loose wire touches the needle and do not touch the wire once it is connected to the battery.
Alternatively, you can use brass snare wire insulated with non-conductive material like paper.

For the floating method, place the magnetized needle on a small dry leaf, piece of paper, bark, grass blade, or inside a shortened straw in a sheltered body of water such as a puddle or a non-magnetic container.
The needle will rotate and align itself; allow it to float freely to determine which end points north using natural aids like the sun.
Mark the north end with a pen or a small scratch.
To verify sufficient magnetization, hold the needle against another metal object; if it is attracted and can hold itself against the metal, it is strong enough.
Generally, the longer you work on magnetizing the needle, the stronger and more long-lasting the magnetization will be..
Using the sun as a natural signpost, you can determine the four cardinal directions and approximate time.
The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
At noon, the sun is due south in the northern hemisphere and due north in the southern hemisphere.
To find direction using a shadow stick, drive a stick upright into level ground.
Place a stone at the tip of the shadow.
Wait three hours and place a second stone at the new position of the shadow's tip.
Draw a line between the two stones to establish an east-west line; the first stone marks due west and the second marks due east.
Mark a line perpendicular to the east-west line to find the north-south direction.
In the northern hemisphere, the shadow falls north of the east-west line, while in the southern hemisphere, it falls south.
The shortest shadow indicates noon.
This method works between the Arctic Circle at 66.5 degrees North and the Antarctic Circle at 66.5 degrees South.
A stick approximately three feet high is recommended for this technique..
Once you have established the east-west and north-south lines, place a stick at their intersection to create a sundial.
Tie a cord to this central stick and attach a smaller stick to the other end of the cord.
Use the smaller stick to draw a 180-degree arc between two marker stones on the ground.
Divide this arc into 12 equal sections and mark each division with a notch, where each notch represents one hour from 6am to 6pm.

The central notch indicates noon, while the position of the shadow cast by the stick shows the approximate time; for example, if the shadow aligns with a specific notch, it indicates that time, such as roughly 5pm..

To determine direction using an analog watch in the Southern Hemisphere, point the 12 o'clock marker at the Sun and bisect the angle between that marker and the hour hand; this line indicates due north.
In the Northern Hemisphere, point the hour hand at the Sun and bisect the angle between it and the 12 o'clock marker to find due south.
Ensure your watch is set to the correct local time and accounts for daylight savings time before using this method.
If you lack a physical watch but know the time, draw a watch face on paper with marked hour and minute hands to simulate the tool.
Note that this technique becomes less effective as you approach the equator.
You can also study natural signs for orientation if you know the predominant wind direction of an area.
Windswept trees typically point away from the wind, while their growth is fullest on sides facing the Sun.
Moss and lichen grow in shaded areas.
Some plants, such as barrel cacti, twist toward the Sun as they grow.
In very windy areas, small animals and birds tend to nest or burrow on the lee-side of hills, away from the wind.
Spiders spin their webs out of the wind.
In powder-snow conditions, snow dunes often form parallel to the prevailing wind, and frost erosion is most severe on slopes facing the Sun..
To navigate using stars in the northern hemisphere, locate the Big Dipper and draw a line from the two front stars at the top of the dipper.
Extend this line outward until you reach Polaris, which is situated approximately four times the distance between those two stars away from the Big Dipper; Polaris marks due north on the horizon.
In the southern hemisphere, find the Southern Cross and project a line along its longer axis toward a dark area of sky.
Then, draw a second line at a 90-degree angle from the midpoint between the two bright stars in the Centaurus constellation.
The point where these two lines intersect indicates due south, with the horizon below that intersection marking your southern direction.

Alternatively, you can orient yourself by noting that the crescent moon provides directional clues: drawing a line between its two horns points roughly south in the northern hemisphere and roughly north in the southern hemisphere.
Since the moon reflects sunlight, it rises in the east and sets in the west, making it useful for orientation on clear nights, particularly during a full moon when using a shadow stick method..
Hiking is an effective method for exploring wilderness terrain, but it requires a decent level of fitness and appropriate clothing and equipment.
To ensure safety and efficiency, you must master basic hiking skills.
When ascending hills, lean forward slightly while maintaining momentum, shorten your stride, keep your feet flat on the ground as you push upward, and move your legs forward from the hips.
Swing your arms to aid in balance and generate momentum.
Always carry a walking stick for support during climbs.
Ensure your backpack fits properly for comfort and that its deep tread grips the ground to reduce slipping.
Aim for a slow, even pace that all group members can maintain throughout the hike by developing a consistent hiking rhythm.
Take regular rest breaks and ensure everyone in the group knows the route.
Additionally, break in new boots before undertaking long hikes to prevent blisters..
Negotiating difficult ground such as scree requires specific techniques to advance confidently and safely.
Scree is a mass of small rocks that slides underfoot, making travel uphill and downhill challenging with a slippery surface that slows progress.
When traversing scree, choose a zigzag route and look for paths containing similar-sized rocks.
Walk sideways across the slope, taking small steps and testing rocks for stability before placing your full weight on them.
Walking downhill places significant strain on your thighs, knees, and ankles, especially when carrying a heavy backpack.
To avoid losing control or gathering too much speed, use your arms for balance and maintain a steady rhythm.
Unless you are in the desert where it is cooler to move in darkness, avoid walking at night due to nocturnal predators and navigational difficulties.
If walking at night is unavoidable, use a flashlight or improvise a torch by setting alight birch bark or similar material.
If lighting is not possible and time permits, close your eyes for 20 minutes to allow your eyes to adjust to night vision.
Use a walking stick to feel for obstacles, tripping hazards, or sudden drops.
Keep your pace slow and deliberate and check your compass regularly..
River crossings are dangerous and should only be attempted when absolutely necessary, as getting cold and wet can lead to hypothermia, making it difficult to recover warmth and dryness.
Always consult your map to find routes around the river and select the safest available crossing point.
Trees on the opposite bank can provide shelter from wind once you exit the water.
Avoid crossing downstream of debris or fallen trees.
Lean on a pole while moving your feet for stability.
Unusual variations in water flow may indicate submerged rocks, and exposed rocks can be slippery.
Do not cross on the outside of river bends where water flows faster; instead, look for shallow banks to make entering and exiting easier.
Walking upstream generally leads to shallower water, but remember that even shallow water can have strong currents, as can water that appears calm on the surface.
Always check for bridges farther up or downstream.
Before entering the water, ensure you have a change of clothes or a way to get dry on the other side.
In cold conditions, collect everything needed to start a fire, such as tinder, kindling, and dry wood, and keep these items dry while crossing.
Wear footwear when wading to protect your feet from rocks and other hazards.
Remove pants to keep them dry and decrease resistance in the water.
Use a walking stick for added support..
To cross a river in a huddle, position the strongest person upstream to bear the main force of the current while others provide stability and support.
Link your arms tightly together and take short, deliberate steps across the water.
When crossing in a line, maintain balance by moving perpendicular to the current, proceeding slowly and placing each step carefully to avoid being swept away.
