Wading with a group is safer than crossing alone; link arms to create a stable structure that offers backup if someone falls.
Loosen backpack straps and pass only one arm through them so you can release the pack quickly in an emergency.
Arrange the group so the person bearing the main force of the current takes the lead, the lightest person crosses in the middle, and the strongest person handles the water's force.
Place arms on each other's shoulders for added stability.
Never cross white water or flooded rivers, as it is safer to avoid crossing entirely than to risk injury.
Use a walking stick to assess changes in the depth of the riverbed before proceeding..
Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location.
If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger.
You must decide between staying put or moving based on specific conditions: move only if you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons to remain; otherwise, stay.
If you have a cell or satellite phone, contact someone immediately to report your predicament.
If an emergency rescue is required and you possess a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), activate it as an option.
Address the core principles of survival in this order: protection, location, water, and food.

In mountain environments, find or improvise shelter immediately to protect yourself from elements, avoid confrontation with animals, stabilize any injuries by applying first aid, and get out of dangerous situations quickly..
Do not allow your extremities to get too cold as these areas are most susceptible to frostbite.
Overlook the dangers of carbon monoxide poisoning in cramped shelter; do not let candles, cookers, or fires burn all night.
Breathe on your hands to warm them because you are exhaling warm air that you will need to replace with cold air.
Select a shelter site that offers protection from the elements.
Fill plastic bags or spare clothing with dry foliage and use them as a mattress or pillow for insulation from the cold, damp ground.
Deploy all your aids to location and prepare for immediate use.
Check upstream for the quality of your water source.
Light a fire and take turns tending it if in a group to keep it going all night.
Continually reassess your situation and adapt your actions accordingly.
Be alert for signs of rescue.
Descend hills carefully by zigzagging across them to put less strain on leg muscles.
Travel at a fast pace because high altitude means less air and places greater demands on even a fit person.
Do not sweat too much because the moisture will chill you further.
Ignore opportunities to collect dry tinder, kindling, and fuel only if necessary.
Keep all clothing dry and clean.
Collect drinking water from fast-moving streams and purify it if you have the means.
Watch out for signs of cold-related injuries such as frost nip, frostbite, and hypothermia.
Regulate your clothing to avoid overheating when moving and hypothermia when static.
Use a walking stick to aid safe movement.
Watch the weather closely and be prepared to change your plans quickly because mountain climates are highly unpredictable.
If you cannot survive where you are but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything you can to attract rescue.
If your situation changes, consult the alternative instructions for moving versus staying..
When hiking over frozen terrain, physical fitness is essential because the conditions result in slow and exhausting progress.
You must have the right equipment and know how to use it effectively.
Wearing snow shoes or skis along with breathable, layered clothing is mandatory.
Ski poles can be used to test the snow ahead of you.
Snow shoes prevent sinking into the snow, while waterproof pants or gaiters keep your legs dry.
The ability of a shoe to pivot reduces drag and improves maneuverability.
Wear a backpack to keep both arms free for balance and movement.
A ski mask protects your eyes from glare.
Snow shoes and skis work by spreading your body weight over a larger surface area, allowing you to walk on the surface of the snow rather than sinking into it.
Always set off when it is cold because you will warm up quickly; add or remove layers as required.

Walking over snow without snow shoes is dangerous because sinking into the snow leaves you exhausted and wet, which in cold conditions can quickly lead to hypothermia, particularly when you stop and are no longer generating heat..
If you lack pre-manufactured snow shoes, you can construct simple ones using a knife, green wood, and cordage to move more effectively over snow.
Begin by cutting five lengths of green wood that are as thick as your thumb and as long as the distance from your foot to your armpit.
Next, cut three shorter pieces to serve as cross-pieces.
Lash the ends of the five longer pieces together securely with cordage.
Determine where the ball of your foot will sit on the shoe and lash the first cross-piece across the five lengths at that position, ensuring the shoe balances.
Securely lash the five loose lengths of wood together at the back of the shoe.
Fix the second cross-piece roughly two inches behind the first one.
Lash the third cross-piece where your heel will rest.
Repeat these steps to create a second shoe before moving on to assembly.
Place your foot on the snow shoe so that the ball of your foot sits directly over the front cross-piece and your heel rests on the back cross-piece.
Tie your boot to the snow shoe using available cordage, but ensure the heel remains free to pivot for proper movement..
To move over snow without gaiters, tie plastic bags around the bottom of your legs to keep them dry.
For short distances or to reach a road, attach branches to your feet using cordage to spread your weight and lift you out of deep snow.
Select a tree like pine with strong, close branches.
Choose saplings that have some flexibility; heating them over a fire makes them easier to bend.
Gently bend the longest branch into a teardrop shape and lash the two ends together.
You can also add cross-pieces and twigs or cordage to create a base for your feet.
Tie the front of your boot to a bough as an alternative method.
Ensure the back of your foot can lift slightly when walking, and use cross-pieces to strengthen your shoe..
Using a pulk is an efficient method for carrying equipment or a person over snow by pulling it behind you with human power.
Pulks are small, low-slung toboggans typically made of lightweight plastic and come in various sizes.
To use one effectively, attach it to your body using a harness.
Wear breathable clothing to prevent overheating and ensure you know how to release your harness quickly in an emergency.
When traveling downhill in a group, designate one person to be harnessed to the rear of the pulk to act as a brakeman.
Pulks pick up speed easily, so exercise caution when descending slopes.
When ascending slopes, kick firmly into the slope and test your weight before moving upward.
For descending, you can ski without skis, known as boot skiing.
Always test the snow ahead with a walking stick.
If you encounter melted depressions called sun cups, cross on the rims to avoid sinking.
In deep snow, walk in single file within a group and take turns leading, as the front position is the most strenuous because it creates the path.
Avoid rocks in spring conditions since they absorb heat and make the snow above unstable.
To navigate ice safely, use a walking stick to test the surface, especially over rivers and lakes.
If traveling in a group on ice, rope yourselves together for safety.
Wear crampons for extra grip and use an ice ax to cut steps on steep slopes or to halt a fall.
Never cross glaciers without a guide, as they require specialized skills..

Four-wheel-drive vehicles allow you to manually switch between two-wheel-drive for paved roads and four-wheel-drive for low-traction conditions, with the key difference being the number of powered wheels.
When driving over rough terrain, avoid hooking your thumbs around the steering wheel because hitting a rut can jerk the wheel and cause injury.
Lower your tire pressure slightly before tackling difficult terrain.
In four-wheel-drive systems, all four wheels are powered by the engine simultaneously; gasoline engines typically offer more power while diesel engines provide better durability at low speeds.
The primary purpose of four-wheel-drive is to reduce the likelihood of getting stuck rather than to extend travel distance until you become stuck.
If unsure about conditions ahead, walk the route first to identify potential problems and options for self-recovery or escape, then mark the path with visible markers to follow while driving.
Use four-wheel-drive only on rough terrain and avoid it on hard roads to prevent damaging tires and gears, as this mode consumes significant fuel.
On soft sand, tires tend to push sand from the front to the rear of the tire; if movement stops completely, the wheel can dig itself into a hole.
To prevent this, steer continuously from side to side so the tire exits its own ruts and avoid rapid changes in speed.
Driving off-road over rough terrain is mentally and physically demanding, so take regular breaks and share driving duties if traveling in a group.
Always have at least two people in the vehicle: a driver and a spotter responsible for navigation.
Ensure the vehicle is fit for the journey and carry spare water, wheels, fuel, survival equipment, sand ladders, a 12-volt heavy-duty tire inflator, winching straps, and a first aid kit.

On soft surfaces, lowering tire pressure improves traction; to find the correct pressure, place a brick half an inch from the side of a rear tire on flat ground, deflate the tire until it touches the brick, measure that pressure, and apply it to all four tires while ensuring you can reinflate them afterward..
Driving on deep mud requires concentration and adaptability; use wide tires or lower tire pressure in deep mud, but avoid driving over hard surfaces beneath the mud as this worsens traction.
Steering outside existing track ruts usually provides the best grip.
When crossing water, always walk your route first to assess depth and current speed.
Do not cross if the water is too deep or the current is too fast.
Maintain a correct driving speed: going too fast splashes water everywhere, while driving too slowly may flood the engine bay.
Driving on snow and ice demands very smooth methods; apply gradual pressure to the accelerator and brakes to prevent wheel spin.
Use low gears, particularly when descending hills.
Practice fitting snow chains before they are needed in emergency situations.
Ensure vehicle drain holes are fitted if applicable and keep the water level below the top of the wheels..
If you get stuck, avoid aggressive attempts to free your vehicle as this may dig you into a deeper rut.
Instead, calmly evaluate options such as reversing, pushing, digging, using traction aids, or winching.
Ideally, travel with at least two vehicles so one can assist with winching, handling breakdowns, or driving for help.
If four-wheel drive fails to free the vehicle, alternate between reversing and driving forward in first gear.
If this does not work, have passengers exit the vehicle while you drive forward as they push.
If pushing and reversing are ineffective, dig down in front of each tire to create an upward slope that allows gentle acceleration out of the rut; avoid revving the engine during this process.
When the vehicle still will not move, place branches, wooden planks, sand ladders, or blankets in front of the wheels to increase traction.
Gently ease the vehicle forward onto these materials at a slow, steady speed without excessive engine revving.

Essential recovery equipment includes a winching mechanism with strap, cable or tow rope, an inflated spare tire, shovel and saw, sleeping bag or blanket, branches or stakes, and sand ladders or mats..
To winch a vehicle out of a hole using a stake, attach the cable fairly low down on the main stake and push supporting stakes into the ground at a slight angle, lashed together for added strength.
You can use a natural hollow to save energy when digging a hole for an anchor.
Place a large log or rock to provide an angle for the winch.
Cover the winch cable with a blanket to reduce whiplash if it breaks and secure the anchor further by refilling the hole.
When winching to another vehicle in a convoy, be aware that both vehicles risk getting stuck.
Use natural anchor points such as trees, large rocks, roots, or deadfalls whenever possible.
If using a tree, place the strap or cable near the ground and use a winch strap to prevent damaging the tree; if one tree cannot provide enough support, tie it to others nearby.
For buried anchor points, dig a hole at least 3 feet deep, attach your winch cable to an object like a log or spare wheel, bury the anchor in the hole, and refill the hole to secure it further.
If using a tire as an anchor, use the lever behind the spare wheel as an attachment point for the cable.
Never stand within range of the winch cable while operating it, as a breaking cable can cut a tree or a person in half.
Always place a blanket or sleeping bag over the cable to mitigate injury from snapping cables..
To create a survival poncho float, gather a large waterproof material such as a poncho or any other suitable sheet.
If you have two ponchos, place the first one face down on the ground and lay your equipment kit in the center.
Fold the four corners of the poncho inward over the pile to form a rectangular package that prevents water from entering.
Secure the edges tightly using ropes, bootlaces, vines, or any available cordage.
For added buoyancy, fill the float with brushwood before sealing it completely.
If you have a second poncho, repeat the process by placing the first sealed float face down on the second sheet and securing it again.
Before entering the water, push your poncho hood to the inside and tighten the neck drawstrings or cordage to ensure the package remains watertight.
Gently lower yourself into the water with the float attached and pull it along as you cross the river obstacle.
This improvised raft keeps your belongings dry and provides essential flotation when a major water barrier lies between you and rescue..
To build an effective raft, use naturally buoyant materials such as logs, bamboo, or discarded oil drums.
Logs should be dry standing deadwood for better flotation and must be of the same diameter.
Bamboo rafts are lighter due to their hollow, compartmentalized sections.
Oil drums are ideal but require caution as they may contain toxins.
Most improvised rafts will float half-submerged, so construct an extra raised platform.
For a raft made of logs, cut notches into them so cross-pieces can sit snugly above the water level.
To create a paddle, select a suitable length of green wood that is wide but comfortable to grip.
Make a split at one end of the handle and insert smaller branches into it, lashing each branch until you have a sufficient paddle area.
Lash the ends of the handle together to make the paddle more rigid.
The length of the split determines the size of the paddle face.
Trim the edges with a saw to make them even.
If there is no river current, you will need to improvise a paddle for propulsion; otherwise, you can use the current and a walking stick to maneuver the raft..
Whether you plan to wait for rescue in place, move to a safer location, or walk out of the situation yourself, selecting an appropriate site for your shelter is essential.
This decision applies whether you need accommodation for a single night or a longer-term stay.
A solid understanding of good location principles ensures survival actions are safe and effective.
Organizing your campsite creates a sense of purpose and order while providing a secure environment for both yourself and your equipment.
Designating specific areas for storing tools and equipment prevents loss of vital items and reduces the risk of injury to you or your team members.

Fire is an integral component of any campsite, serving multiple critical functions including warmth, water purification, cooking, signaling, protection from wild animals, and providing light after dark.
It also offers a profound sense of security; the psychological impact of successfully starting a fire should never be underestimated, nor should the negative effects of failing to do so.
Even in a survival scenario, establishing a simple camp can restore a sense of normality and home.
Continuously learning wilderness skills is an ongoing process.
Mastering the correct method for using a knife improves safety and conserves valuable energy by ensuring tasks are completed efficiently.
Similarly, knowing how to tie simple knots allows you to utilize available cordage in the most effective manner possible.
The more knowledge you possess about how things work, the greater your ability to improvise solutions when resources are scarce, which can be the difference between continued survival and despair..
On any expedition, carry equipment such as a basic bivouac shelter or tarpaulin to form protection from the elements if needed.
A space blanket is an essential piece of gear that should be packed and carried at all times even if nothing else is included.
Space blankets pack down very small and typically feature one silver side to reflect heat and one bright orange side to aid location.
They provide immediate protection from the elements in any environment and can be converted into a basic shelter.
To produce a spark with a firesteel, use a rod made from ferrocerium or magnesium alloy paired with a sharp striker device such as a knife blade or short piece of hacksaw blade.
When the striker is drawn over the rod, a spark is produced.
To control the spark and direct it accurately to reduce scattering of tinder, place the rod in the center of the tinder.
Position the striker onto the rod and lock your hand in position.
Pull the rod up and away from the tinder while drawing it against the striker.
To direct the sparks, alter the angle at which you pull the rod up and away..
Always choose a campsite based on the four principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food.
Ensure there are no obvious dangers and that you can signal for rescue.
Assess your environment for potential threats such as animals, unstable rocks or trees, and flooding risks.
Look for signs of animals, especially near water sources, and try to pitch your camp against a rock face so it can only be approached from one direction.
Position the entrance to your shelter at an angle to the wind.
Avoid gullies due to the risk of flash floods or avalanches.
Do not camp near standing water where insects like mosquitoes swarm.
Place your latrine downwind of the camp and downstream from any water source.
If predators are present, hang unused food 10 feet (3 meters) off the ground and 3 feet (1 meter) away from tree trunks or branches.
Keep noise-making items on hand to scare off prowling predators.
In case of an encounter with dangerous wild animals, stay calm as most will avoid confrontation unless provoked, cornered, or surprised, particularly when protecting young.
If you see a bear, make yourself look as big as possible by raising your arms and walk slowly backward without running.
If the bear follows, hold your ground; if it attacks, play dead or fight back depending on the situation.
Organize your site by designating specific areas for storing equipment and firewood, cooking, and sleeping.
Establish routines for activities to ensure camp safety and reduce the risk of accidents..
Avoid camping under standing deadwood, as these trees have not yet fallen but may collapse due to heavy wind or the weight of rain and snow; they are excellent sources for kindling and fire fuel.
Steer clear of campsite locations on outside river bends, which are prone to erosion and flooding, especially if a river bursts its banks during heavy downpours.
Be cautious of deadfalls, which are dangerous branches that have broken off trees like beech, ash, and yew without warning.
When camping near rocks, inspect for cracks because fires below them can trigger rockfalls, and in cold conditions, watch out for sudden ice falls.
Pitch your camp near a source of wood and designate an open area to serve as a location aid and landing site for rescue helicopters.
Dig a small runoff trench around your shelter to reduce flooding risks during heavy rain and divert water away downhill.
Build three signal fires on open ground and always locate a nearby water source, checking the upstream area for contamination before use..
Caves offer ready-made shelters that are typically dry and secure, but they present specific dangers including animals, poor air quality, and water risks.
Never venture deeper into a cave than you can clearly see to avoid hidden drops or slippery surfaces, and strictly avoid old, disused mines due to the risk of collapse.

To maintain body heat, cover the floor with bedding made from the driest available materials such as feathers, pine boughs, spruce boughs, dry leaves, moss, bracken, or grasses; collect twice as much bedding as you think necessary and surround it with logs to prevent spreading.
Be aware that caves harbor bears, bats, insects, spiders, and snakes, noting that vampire bats can carry rabies and bat droppings are highly combustible.
When considering coastal caves, watch for signs of flooding during high tide such as lines of seaweed, driftwood, or debris, damp smells, and rock pools.

If you experience light-headedness, nausea, an increased pulse, or rapid breathing, leave the cave immediately; additionally, if a flame dims or turns blue, it indicates a lack of oxygen and requires immediate evacuation.

When building a fire inside a cave, place it at the rear rather than the front to prevent smoke from blowing back into your exit path, ensure adequate airflow, use fuel that produces minimal smoke, and secure side logs with wooden pegs while utilizing rocks to retain heat..
To construct a corded A-frame shelter using a poncho, blanket, sheet, or tarpaulin, locate two trees spaced approximately two feet farther apart than the length of your material.
Attach a cord to one tree three feet off the ground using a double chain fastening knot.
Secure the other end of that cord to the second tree with a taut line hitch, adjusting it until tight.
Peg the middle loop on each side and pull the material taut.
Peg out the corner loops while pulling them tight.
Place long boughs along the sides of the shelter and shorter boughs at each end to retain bedding material.
Attach one-foot loops to the grommets on the longer sides and three-foot loops to the end ones.
Tie a knot around the hood to seal it and prevent water from pooling, then raise the hood for protection..
A natural hollow in the ground can be converted into a trench shelter that protects you from the wind if you lie inside it.
Avoid hollows in low-lying areas that may flood or those on slopes exposed to runoff water.
Trench shelters can be built in varied terrain types including sand, snow, shoreline, and forests.
If the hollow is too shallow to lie in, remove some soil using a digging stick.
Be wary of wet soil as it could be prone to flooding.
If the ground is too hard to dig, build up the sides with logs.
Lay bedding material on the ground inside the shelter.
Use a saw to cut several sturdy poles or branches long enough to place across the hollow to create a supporting roof.
Place a thicker, longer log on top of the cross poles for a pitched roof.
Add shorter branches, sticks, or poles on the log and cross them to complete the pitched roof structure.
Pack all materials tightly together.
Insulate the pitched roof with lots of foliage, starting the final layer at the ground and working upward.
Leave an entrance at one end oriented 90 degrees to the wind.
Useful tools include a sturdy digging stick with a pointed end, strong branches and logs, and a saw such as a pocket chainsaw.
Suitable materials for the roof include tree bark, turf, leaves, forest mulch, moss, bracken, and pine boughs..
To construct a Forest A-frame shelter, select a ridgepole and smooth one side with a knife while leaving the other side bare with branch stubs.
Hammer two poles into the ground to form an "A" shape, resting one end of the ridgepole on top of this frame and the other end on the ground.
Weave alternating rows of saplings through the poles to create a framework for the covering, ensuring you leave an entrance near the front.
Cover the framework with a thick layer of natural materials such as pine boughs, branches, and twigs to form a thatch.
Position additional poles against the stubs on the ridgepole, decreasing in height as you work from the front "A" toward the back ground level.
At each junction, rest the side poles against the stubs or tie them securely to the ridgepole using an arbor knot.
Cover the first layer with leaves, moss, and mulch, starting from the ground and working up to the ridgepole.
Ensure the ridgepole is at least 3 feet longer than you are for proper coverage.
Build a fire reflector at the entrance of the shelter.
Use tools such as a knife, saw or ax, cordage, saplings, poles of various lengths, and covering materials like pine boughs, leaves, and moss to complete the structure..
To build a forest lean-to, select a flat area between two trees or vertical supports firmly secured in the ground.
If constructing for a group, determine the required width by having everyone lie down next to each other and adding six inches per person.
Position the ridgepole against the trees at your desired height and lash it securely using an arbor knot.
Place a support pole beneath the ridgepole and lash it to the trees, then peg a log where you want the foot of the shelter to create space for your feet.
Drive five roof poles evenly along the ridgepole so they slope back toward the foot of the lean-to.
Keep the outside poles between the trees to prevent them from moving outward, lash each roof pole to the ridgepole, and hammer side stakes alongside the outside poles to form walls.
Gather necessary tools including a saw, knife, cordage, saplings, foliage for covering, poles, and stakes.
Ensure the ridgepole is longer than the distance between the trees, use pegs to secure the log position in the ground, and make sure roof poles are at least as long as the shelter needs to be..
To construct a forest lean-to shelter, weave saplings in and out through the roof poles starting from the ridgepole, alternating the weave pattern between horizontal and diagonal for each row.
Form walls by weaving smaller saplings through side stakes.
If everyone is inside the same lean-to, only one fire is needed.
Layer the interior floor with dry material to create bedding and place a pole across the shelter to keep the bedding in position.
Bank the final covering against the walls to improve insulation.
Before beginning construction, gather all necessary materials while there is still daylight.
Ensure your location aids, such as signal fires or flares, are visible or can be activated quickly.
Wear gloves when clearing leaves and debris to protect against spiders and snakes.
Layer down clothing during work to prevent overheating.
Make the shelter as waterproof as possible by applying large materials first to the roof and walls.
Ensure the bedding raises you at least four inches off the ground to stay dry.
Establish a watch system to maintain and control the fire..
An A-frame shelter is relatively easy to construct using a modified poncho, tarpaulin, shelter sheet, or groundsheet as the roof and sleeping surface.
First, modify your chosen sheet into a bed by following specific instructions for creating a poncho bed.
Next, cut seven long poles capable of supporting your weight.
Tie two of these poles together using an arbor knot to form one side of an "A" shape, then secure this joint to a tree or tree branch.
Repeat this process to create a second "A" frame at the opposite end, ensuring both frames are aligned in a straight line.
The distance between the two "A" frames must be at least two feet longer than your height.
Place a ridgepole across the top of the two "A" frames and tie it securely to both junction points for added stability.
Position the poles of your poncho bed on the outside of the A-frame structure, then lower them until the sheet is taut.
Finally, wedge the legs of the frame into the ground and secure all junctions with cordage and pegs..
Place a tarp or sheet over the ridgepole and secure it by tying a guyline to each corner, running the line to a peg or tree, and fastening it with a taut line hitch.
Tighten the shelter by adjusting these hitches.
Gather a cutting tool such as a parang, machete, ax, saw, or bushcraft knife.
Clear the ground around your shelter using a makeshift brush rather than bare hands to avoid bites from snakes or spiders.
Construct your shelter high enough off the ground to deter insects and animals, especially those active at night.
Prevent dehydration and heat-related injuries like heat stress or heat stroke by drinking water frequently, taking regular breaks in the shade, and working at a moderate pace.
Inside the shelter, use a full mosquito net along with a head-net.
Build a fire to keep insects and animals away.
Shelters in the rainforest should be erected quickly, sized just for your needs, and built safely away from animals.
To create a modified poncho bed, fold the poncho or tarp in half and secure each grommet to its opposing grommet to form a tube.
Place a pole down each side of this tube to form a bed..
Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or your location, whether you are in danger, and if you have any means of communication.
If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger.
You must survive until you are located or find help; if no one knows your status, notify people of your plight using any available method.
If you are missed, a rescue party will almost certainly be dispatched to find you.
If you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons to remain, move to a location that offers a better chance of survival or rescue.
To get yourself out of immediate danger, slow down your pace regarding elements, find or improvise immediate shelter, avoid confrontation with animals, stabilize any injuries, and apply first aid.
If you have a cell or satellite phone, let someone know your predicament.
If your situation is serious enough for emergency rescue and you have a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), consider using it.
Address the core principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food..
Do not let the oppressive nature of the jungle overwhelm you; instead, slow your pace to match the environment.
Store dry tinder and split or quarter wood to access the dry inner core, avoiding damp firewood.
Never eat anything you cannot positively identify as edible, as illness can render you unable to function.
Select a shelter site where you can sleep off the ground and where rescue signals will be most effective.
Use a mosquito net, place damp foliage on your fire to repel insects, or cover exposed skin with mud.
Deploy all location aids immediately and prepare them for instant use while remaining constantly alert for signs of rescue.
Keep yourself covered despite the heat because high humidity encourages infections; wash at every opportunity.
Maintain a fire to aid in signaling, ward off insects, and keep your location visible.
Use your hands to clear undergrowth, though a machete or walking stick is better suited for this task.
Never drink untreated water.
Do not leave it too late in the day to stop and make camp; aim to establish camp at least three hours before sunset.
Make noise as you progress through the jungle to warn animals of your presence.
If you cannot survive where you are but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything possible to attract rescue.
If your situation changes from moving to staying, consult alternative survival guidelines.
When navigating, use line of sight since visibility may be less than 33 feet (10 meters).
Improvise a shelter when not moving, ensuring you sleep off the ground, clear of damp floors and animals.
Look for dry tinder and fuel.
Follow water courses downstream, as transportation in the jungle relies on rivers and settlements are most likely found alongside them.
Step onto logs to see what is on the other side rather than stepping straight over onto an unseen snake..
To improvise immediate desert shelter from the sun, use a shelter sheet, tarpaulin, poncho, or space blanket.
You can either build a scrape in a natural hollow or erect a quick shelter above the ground.
If you have cordage and pegs, dig a scrape and use the cord to secure the sheet above it; otherwise, hold the sheet in place using soil, sand, or rocks.
For all layered shelters, maintain tautness and separation between layers.
Use a second sheet as an additional layer to create dead air space that reduces temperature below, or if you only have one cover, double it over with at least 6 inches of separation between layers.
Essential tools include cordage, pegs, a digging tool, a knife, rocks or ballast material, and strong posts.
If the hollow is too shallow to lie in, scrape out the bottom soil to build up the sides.
You can also make a cordless scrape by digging down or building up the sides while anchoring the sheet with rocks.
Spread your cover across the top of the hollow ensuring edges overlap the sides.
Avoid building your shelter during the hottest part of the day and ration your sweat rather than your water; if you start to sweat, take a break.
Avoid low-lying areas, dry riverbeds, and wadis due to the risk of flash floods, and avoid the tops of large hills because of lightning and extreme winds.
Build your shelter on a small rise where it can be warmer at night.
The opening should face north in the Northern Hemisphere and south in the Southern Hemisphere to avoid direct sunlight during the day.
Dig down to create a depression since the ground is cooler below the surface.
Build your shelter for the worst possible conditions as desert weather is unpredictable.
If your shelter sheet has a shiny side, make sure it faces up to reflect heat and act as a location aid.
Weigh layers down with rocks or sand..
To construct a desert shelter, first unpack your essentials before setting up camp.
If you cannot find a natural hollow, erect a poncho shelter in a location that remains cool during the day, such as under trees or bushes, or at the top of a slight rise to catch a breeze.
For daytime use, keep the sides and bottom open for airflow while supporting the roof with a stable pile of rocks; a deeper hole will help keep the interior cooler.
At night, de-rig the outer shelter and secure it with rocks, shingles, or sand to retain heat.
Sleep on bedding if available.
To find a suitable site, locate one beside a tree or secure a sturdy post next to where you want the opening.
Rig a ridgeline to the tree or post and peg your poncho over it to form the primary shelter.
Repeat this process with a second poncho, shelter sheet, or space blanket to create a separate insulating layer.
Alternatively, if you have cordage, tie your sheet to four posts; otherwise, use your backpack or a pile of stones as anchors.
Prepare the hollow as for a cordless scrape and tie the sheet to the posts while leaving a gap for air circulation.
Create a second layer above the first, placing foliage between the layers to maintain separation, ensuring the second layer is at least six inches (15 centimeters) above the first..
To build a quinzhee, find a relatively flat area covered with snow and mark out a circle for the shelter with walls about 10 inches thick, stamping down the snow to compact it.
Use your backpack and boughs or leaves covered with a tarpaulin to form the core of the shelter.
Position the doorway at 90 degrees to the wind.
Gather soft snow using a mess can, pan, snow shoe, or similar item.
Pile snow on the core to form a dome of the required height, building up layers until you have a covering of at least 10 inches while smoothing out each layer.
Smooth the entire surface of the snow dome and leave it to sinter or harden for one to three hours.

To maintain the right temperature inside, remove layers of clothing as you work, keeping only base layers with a waterproof layer on top, and ensure all clothing stays dry so it can be worn when you stop working.
Keep the shelter at a constant temperature because if snow melts and refreezes, it loses its insulating properties.
Avoid heating your shelter too much..
To build a quinzhee, mark guide-sticks at 12 to 18 inches before inserting them into the snow.
Push sticks of equal length through the snow toward the center of the dome to ensure an even thickness in the roof and walls.
Construct a small compact mound in front of the dome, then burrow into it to remove your bag and tarp while excavating snow from the core.
Use the guide-sticks to maintain wall thickness of at least 10 inches.
Smooth the interior snow surface to prevent drips.
Build a raised sleeping platform to keep cold air away from your sleeping area.
Create a ventilation hole one foot up from the ground and another at the top of the dome.
For general snow shelter construction, remember that fresh uncompacted snow is an excellent insulator because it traps 90 to 95 percent air.
Always check your site for hazards like snowdrifts, freezing winds, avalanches, cornice collapse, and large animals.
Keep tools inside the shelter in case you need to dig out, brush snow off equipment and clothing before entering, and tie all vital gear to yourself to prevent loss in deep snow.
Prevent carbon monoxide poisoning by creating holes 3 to 4 inches in diameter at both the base and top of your shelter to ensure free airflow.
Carbon monoxide is an odorless gas produced when insufficient oxygen allows fuel to burn improperly; it can be fatal in well-insulated, non-ventilated environments.
Signs of poisoning include fatigue, faintness, flu-like symptoms, severe headaches, nausea, and decreased mental coordination, which worsen over time.
If poisoning occurs, get into fresh air immediately and breathe it for at least four hours to reduce carbon monoxide levels in your system by half..
The ability to make and maintain a fire is a significant psychological factor in survival; those who can do so are more likely to succeed than those who give up.
Always carry some form of firelighter and practice your skills under different conditions with various materials.
Collect tinder as soon as you enter the forest, and if it is wet, dry it in your pockets before use.
Gather more than enough quantities of all necessary materials to ensure success.
When selecting a location, clear the ground in advance and never light a fire directly on bare earth.
Use a green wood platform to protect the fire from ground moisture and limit heat dissipation into the soil.
Position wet tinder, kindling, and larger pieces of wood close to the main fire to help them dry out.
Build the fire away from your shelter to prevent sparks from blowing in and igniting it.
Contain the fire using green wood or dry, nonporous rocks that will not explode when heated.
Use a container of water, sand, or soil to fully extinguish the fire before leaving the site.
You can hang a pot from a hook for cooking and use a fire reflector to increase heat efficiency..
Heat is required to ignite fuel and can be generated through sparks, chemical reactions, or friction.
Once a fire starts, you must add fuel by beginning with small, dry pieces that generate enough heat to burn increasingly larger pieces.
To build a fire reflector for a shelter, check that airflow crosses the reflector properly.
Hammer two poles into the ground spaced one pole-width apart and add a second set of poles.
Tie the tops of the upright poles together to form a wall as long as the shelter's entrance to retain heat and block wind and rain.
Construct the reflector so you can build a fire about three feet from the shelter entrance, using an L-shaped end to retain more heat.
Stack poles to reach the required height.
A matchless fireset can offers a one-stop solution for starting fires in any weather; it contains a sparking device, tinder, kindling, and fuel.
Hexamine is scraped onto cotton, which is then ignited by a flint and steel spark long enough for fuel blocks to catch fire.
Keep a plastic Ziploc bag with electrical tape on the can to keep it waterproof and use the taped can as kindling.
Store dry tinder like cotton and tampons in a separate plastic bag.
Use a quarter of a hexamine fuel block to light the fire.
Oxygen, heat, and fuel are crucial for a strong fire, and achieving the best balance between them is key.
If you smother the fire with too much wood, oxygen may not reach the flame.
If the fire is dying, fan it with paper or your hand to create a draft that feeds oxygen to it.
Use green wood so heat does not set the reflector alight..
To build a fire, you need three dry material elements: tinder, kindling, and fuel, collected in sufficient quantities.
Tinder is the first element used to start a fire and can come from your equipment or natural and man-made sources.
Experiment with available materials before they are needed and ensure all tinder is dry by leaving damp items in the sun.
Natural tinder sources include feather sticks, bark, bamboo shavings, plant and animal down, fine wood dust, pine pitch, dead moss, and dry grass.
Man-made sources include cotton balls, lint, tampons, tissue paper, camera film, rubber, and candle wick.
To prepare tinder for a spark or coal, create a tinder bundle by vigorously teasing, rubbing, and pulling the fibers until they form a grapefruit-sized ball.
Push the finest combustible material into the interior of the bundle and mix it thoroughly.
Carry items like cotton balls or tampons in a 35mm film canister or Ziploc bag, using some for each fire.
Cotton balls smeared with petroleum jelly help make flames last longer.
Kindling is added to burning tinder once it is dry and ranges from the thickness of a match to a finger.
If kindling is damp, remove the outer bark and break it into six-inch sticks.
Soft-wood twigs are highly combustible, while wood with flammable resin burns hot and for a long duration.
Some tinder types like bark, palm leaves, pine needles, and grass can serve as kindling but require larger quantities.
Fuel should be added once the fire sustains itself for five minutes after initial tending to create a bed of hot coals known as a good heart.
Fuel pieces should be as thick as your wrist or forearm.
Hard woods from deciduous trees like oak and maple produce good coals, while soft woods from conifers like pine and fir are easier to light but generate less heat.
Additional fuel options include peat, charcoal, and dried animal droppings..
To make a feather stick, select a piece of standing dead wood with an even grain and no knots.
Ideally, use wood from a tree that has died but not yet fallen, as it lacks green foliage and sheds bark easily; avoid the bottom few inches where moisture wicks up from the ground.
If using small branches snapped off a tree, remove the bark first.
Lay your knife blade flat on the stick and run it down ten times without cutting to feel for the grain.
Then, run the blade down to cut a shaving, stopping before the end so the shaving remains attached to the stick.
Turn the stick slightly and repeat the process along the edge created by the first cut, working around the entire circumference until the rest of the stick is feathered with curled shavings.
Keep the stick firmly on the ground to steady your action, angle the blade toward the wood to ensure shavings curl as they are made, and collect any shavings that fall off.
A finished feather stick consists of a thin piece of wood with attached curled shavings, providing tinder, kindling, and fuel in one unit.
Four to six such sticks provide sufficient combustible material to start a fire..
To make char cloth, turn the lid of a small can with a tight-fitting seal upside down and hammer a nail into the center to create a small hole; smaller holes are preferable.
Cut pieces of 100 percent cotton cloth that fit inside the can without folding and place them securely within the container.
Position the can over a fire to burn off all the oxygen inside, allowing pyrolysis to occur in the absence of oxygen.
Once the smoke stops rising from the hole, the process is complete.
Allow the can to cool down before removing the cloth.
The finished char cloth should be black, semi-rigid, soft, and not brittle.
Test a piece by striking it with a spark or flame; if it creates an ember, it is ready for use as lightweight tinder that requires minimal space and ignites easily from weak sparks.
Ensure the char cloth remains dry to function properly..
To make a fire can, cut out a long, thin piece of cardboard that is one-quarter inch wider than the depth of your can.
Roll this cardboard tightly along its length until the roll fits snugly inside the can.
Light a candle and allow the melting wax to drip into the can, soaking the cardboard and filling it up.
Stop when the can is nearly full and let the wax harden.
Once cooled, hold the can at an angle and light the top of the cardboard with the candle.
Allow the flame to spread across the top surface.

A fire can burns for hours with a concentrated, controllable flame that produces no smoke, making it useful for starting stubborn fires in poor conditions, boiling water, basic cooking, or warming hands.
Essential tools include a can with a tight-fitting lid such as a small shoe-polish can, ribbed or plain cardboard, a candle with matches or a lighter, and a knife or scissors.
Position the candle so the flame melts the wax rather than burning into the air.
Remember that an extra quarter-inch of cardboard will burn down slightly and act as a wick.
The can becomes hot quickly, so do not touch it when lit.
When the flame starts to fail, either add more wax or replace the cardboard and start again..
Once you have collected dry tinder, kindling, and fuel, you can begin building your fire.
Select a fire type based on your specific needs such as warmth, cooking, signaling, drying clothing, or waste disposal.
Always estimate the amount of fuel required and double it to ensure sufficiency.
Choose a fire design that requires the least effort for maximum gain.
It is more efficient to build a small fire and sit close to it rather than building a large fire and sitting far away.
If your wood is wet, remove the bark and split the wood because the center will usually be dry.
Once the fire is established, place damp tinder, kindling, and larger pieces of wood near the flames to dry them out.
To light the fire, place a tinder ball on a green wood platform and ignite it.
Gently lay kindling by the tinder ball and arrange it in a teepee shape to allow air circulation where the heat is strongest.
As the kindling catches fire and flames grow, slowly add larger pieces of kindling until you can introduce split logs.
Start with small amounts of kindling to ensure the fire establishes itself before adding more fuel..
To build a teepee fire, surround the tinder ball with kindling arranged in a teepee shape and place small, medium, and large fuel logs in a square at its foot.
For a hole-in-the-ground fire, create a hole in the side of a bank with a chimney extending up through the ground; light it inside using any available fuel.
Alternatively, line a hole three feet deep with non-porous stones, place tinder and kindling inside, and rest large logs against the sides so they drop down as they burn.
A simple fire can be made from tinder, kindling, and any type of fuel by placing two long logs on either side in a V-shape.
Another method involves arranging large non-porous stones in a circle with a tinder ball in the middle and kindling around it, adding fuel logs once the fire is established.
To build a log cabin style fire, arrange four logs so they meet in the middle and push additional logs in as they burn.
For a long-lasting fire, place tinder, kindling, and fuel logs in a depression six feet long and lay two long fuel logs on top of the burning fire.
A snake hole fire requires digging a large hole for the fire and a slightly smaller hole for the chimney with a tunnel linking the two, using small logs as fuel.

This configuration allows cooking at ground level, is quick to light, can burn wet wood because the inner fire dries it, creates a draft for high temperatures, provides shelter from the elements, becomes self-feeding once lit, uses hardwood logs or stones to shelter the fire from wind, produces great heat, reduces environmental impact by using existing fire rings, lasts long enough to stay alight all night, emits concentrated heat, and keeps flames below ground so the fire remains hidden..
Lighting tinder is the first stage of making a fire when matches or lighters are unavailable.
You can create sparks using various ignition devices such as flint and steel, a magnifying glass, a battery, or chemical reactions involving potassium permanganate.
To use a magnifying glass, focus bright sunlight onto dry tinder to create a hot spot and hold the glass steady until the tinder catches fire; reading glasses with lenses can also serve this purpose.
For matches, strike them away from you on the box and cup them in your hands to light them commando style; waterproof matches are typically standard matches coated with wax and varnish.
Always carry a lighter around your neck on a piece of cord for safety.
You can generate sparks using a battery by laying wire across the terminals or by removing the bulb from a flashlight, placing wire wool over the terminal, and switching the flashlight on.
Thinner wire works better for this method, especially with lower voltage batteries like 1.5v, but use it only for short periods to avoid draining the battery.
Potassium permanganate is a strong oxidizer that can create an explosive mixture when mixed with certain chemicals and may stain skin and clothes..
To strike a match, hold the box in one hand and position the match between your thumb and first two fingers of the other.
Strike the match firmly away from yourself.
When it lights, cup your hands to protect the flame and allow it to burn slightly along the stem before touching the tinder.
For reliable ignition in all conditions known as commando style, press your middle finger on the match head with the flame pointing down.
A firesteel one-handed striker is used by holding the device next to the tinder while placing the firesteel directly underneath the striker.
Draw the firesteel back across the striker to direct sparks onto the tinder, ensuring the firesteel moves rather than the striker.
Potassium permanganate devices last for about 12,000 strikes and reach temperatures of 5,400°F (3,000°C), functioning in all weathers and at any altitude.
A one-handed version exists with a safety feature to prevent accidental use, designed specifically for fighter pilots who may have injured an arm or hand after ejecting.
To create sparks manually, mix potassium permanganate and sugar in equal amounts on a hard surface, then press down on the mixture with your knife and drag the blade along to generate a spark.

Alternatively, mix a small amount of potassium permanganate with glycol or antifreeze, quickly wrap it in paper, and place it on the ground while standing clear due to the risk of sudden and dangerous combustion..
Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location.
Ask yourself if you are in danger, if you have any means of communication, and if you are part of a group.
If you are in a group, try to help others who are in danger.
You must decide whether to stay put or move based on specific conditions: if no one knows you are missing, you must notify people of your plight by any available means.
If you are missed, a rescue party will almost certainly be dispatched to find you.
If you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons requiring you to remain, you must move to a location that offers a better chance of survival or rescue.

Get yourself out of immediate danger by addressing specific threats: find or improvise shelter immediately for sun and heat exposure; avoid confrontation with animals and move away from them; stabilize any injuries and apply first aid.
If you have a cell or satellite phone, let someone know your predicament.
If your situation is serious enough for emergency rescue and you have a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), consider using it.
Address the core principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food..
Do not leave a broken-down vehicle unless absolutely necessary, as it is easier for rescuers to spot.
Conserve energy by remaining idle unless tasks must be completed.
Use any available combustible materials like wood or vehicle tires to generate heat and smoke.
Seek shade or improvise shelter, noting that digging even six inches below ground level significantly lowers temperatures.
Ration sweat rather than water; in temperatures above 100°F (38°C), you require about one quart per hour, or half that amount if it is cooler.
Continually reassess your water situation and options for augmenting supplies.
Leave items outside overnight to collect dew for drinking.
Prepare all location aids for immediate use and remain constantly alert for signs of rescue.
Sit directly on hot ground when stopping.
Travel during the hottest part of the day only if you must move, but avoid taking risks such as running down sand dunes which could cause fatal injuries.
Force the pace by traveling at the speed of the slowest person in your group.
Shelter in dry riverbeds due to the risk of flash floods.
If you cannot survive where you are and cannot move due to injury, do everything possible to attract rescue.
If your situation changes from moving to staying, consult alternative survival guidelines.
When moving, make an informed decision on the best location and leave clear indications of your intent if abandoning a vehicle.
Keep location aids accessible while moving and distributed when static.
Protect yourself against glare from the sun and windburn.
Seek or improvise shelter when not moving and seize all opportunities to collect fuel for a fire, as deserts get cold at night.
Be on constant lookout for signs of water or civilization such as green vegetation, converging animal or human tracks, or circling birds..
To construct a bow drill, cut a stick as long as your arm and create a notch near each end.
Secure one notch with an arbor knot and the other with a double chain fastening knot, leaving enough slack to wrap around your finger.
For the drill itself, select a straight piece of wood free of knots, ideally measuring about 1 inch in diameter and 8 inches long.
Carve one end to fit into the bearing block with a dull point to minimize friction, and carve the opposite end to fit into the hearth board with bluntness that maximizes friction.
This method is highly efficient for generating fire by friction but requires specific knowledge, skill, practice, appropriate wood selection, time, and effort.
Essential tools include a knife, dry wood, and cordage such as paracord, fishing line, shoelaces, or bailing twine..
The bow drill set functions best when the drill and hearth board are crafted from the same dry wood, ensuring both parts wear down evenly.
Suitable woods include sycamore, hazel, ivy, lime, willow, and sotol, though sotol is not found in Europe.
Slight ridges or bumps on the drill help prevent the cord from slipping.
Construct the bearing block from hard wood approximately 3 to 4 inches across and 4 to 5 inches long; split it in two and create a notch.
Make the hearth board from flat wood about 1 inch thick.
Hold the drill so it sits about 3/8 inch from the edge of the board.
Notch a small hole to help start the drill, then cut a V into the center of that hole through the hearth board; the bottom of this V is where char collects and the ember forms in the ember pan.

Use a knife point to make the notch, insert the drill's pointed end into the hole, twist the cord once around the drill with the cord on the side of the bow, and ensure the V is about one-eighth of the hole's circumference.
Set up the bow, drill, and bearing block, then bow the drill into the hole in the hearth board until the hole is as wide as the drill, but do not go all the way through..
Before starting, ensure your dry tinder bundle, kindling, and fuel are within reach.
Be aware that this process requires significant energy, so take precautions against overheating and dehydration.
It is advisable to practice making tinder bundles and lighting them with a small coal from an existing campfire before attempting the bow drill.
To begin, hold the bow horizontal to the ground and parallel to your body.
Lean forward and back to apply and reduce downward pressure on the drill.
Start by bowing slowly and evenly using the full length of the bow while breathing steadily.
Gradually increase speed and pressure until smoke appears.
Once smoke is visible, vigorously bow with maximum effort for about ten more times to produce a coal in the ember pan.
Place the ember pan on a flat, dry surface aligned with the V on the hearth board.
Loop the bow cord once around the drill from the inside.
Insert the drill into the hole on the hearth board and slot the block on top, ensuring a green leaf is placed in the notch for lubrication.
Lock your wrist into your shin to use it as support while steadying the hearth board with your foot.
Use your arm to bow and bear down on the block with your body weight.
If the drill slips, move your grip farther along the bow to increase tension in the string.
Keep your head still above the bow and maintain the drill upright.
Friction heats the wood as the drill turns, which is essential for creating a coal..
Gently remove the drill and lift the hearth board away from the coal.
If the coal sticks to the board, tap it with your knife to loosen it.
Lift the ember pan off the ground and hold it in the air to check if the coal is glowing.
If the coal is not glowing but just smoking, gently fan it with your hand until you see it glow.
Transfer the glowing coal into your tinder bundle.
The coal must touch the tinder to transfer heat, so squeeze the tinder around the coal without crushing it or starving it of oxygen.
Gently blow into the bundle to help fan the coal and encourage it to catch.
You can swing the bundle down and back up in between breaths to let dry air fan the coal.
Continue as the amount of smoke increases until the bundle bursts into flames.
Do not throw the burning bundle on the ground; calmly place it where you intend to have your fire.
Start building the fire around the bundle once kindling is alight.
Use green wood as a base and hold the tinder bundle up, away from your face, with your back to the wind to help combustion..
Man-made cordage is a vital survival item used for building shelters, repairing gear and clothing, creating traps and nets, and generating fire with a bow drill.
Carry ample supplies in both your backpack and on your person to ensure availability if separated from your pack.
When using a survival canister, verify that the needle has an eye large enough to pass the inner strands of the cordage.
Paracord is a lightweight nylon kernmantle rope consisting of 32 braided strands; each strand contains smaller threads useful for secondary tasks.
It is highly recommended due to its strength, small pack size, and widespread availability.
To prevent unraveling after cutting, coil paracord into palm-sized hanks or cut it into 30-foot lengths for storage in pockets.
Before crafting cordage from natural materials, check existing supplies like shoelaces, belts, clothing strips, hat brims, guylines, draw cords, towropes, and dental floss.
For shoelaces, wrap twice the required length around your boots before tying them on.
Adopt a scavenger mindset to collect useful items such as plastic bottles and bailing twine.
Always use cordage efficiently by tying simple, strong knots that can be released without cutting, preserving the material for future tasks.
Red cordage is ideal in survival situations because its color stands out..
If you lack man-made cordage, you can create a natural substitute using roots, stems, bark, or sinews found in your immediate environment to erect shelters.
To prepare nettle fibers, sit astride a log and lay a nettle stem in front of you.
Roll a smooth, rounded stick back and forth on the stem while pressing down hard until the stem is crushed.
Use your thumbnail to tease open the crushed tissue along the entire length of the stem to expose the spongy pith inside.
Bend the stem in the middle; the inner pith will break away from the outer skin, allowing you to carefully peel them apart.
The long, fibrous strips of skin can then be used for short bindings or natural cordage.
When selecting plants, old stinging nettles or wood nettles with long stems offer the most fibers.
Wear gloves or cover your hands while stripping leaves by grasping the stem at the base and pulling your clenched fist up the full length of the stem.

Ensure the plant material meets specific requirements: the fibers must be long enough to avoid weakening the line if spliced together, rougher fibers are preferable for braiding as they bite together better than shiny or smooth ones which tend to unravel, and the fibers must be strong and pliable enough to withstand bending and tying without breaking..
To make natural cordage, first prepare a sufficient quantity of fibers and let them dry before use, though they can be utilized immediately in survival situations.
The most effective method involves twisting the fibers.
Grip the first length of fiber a third of the way along its length between the forefinger and thumb of your left hand.
Grasp the fiber with your right thumb and forefinger one inch from your left hand and twist it until a tight loop forms.
While maintaining tension on the cord, move your left thumb and forefinger forward to clamp down on the loop.
Start your first length of cordage closer to one end rather than in the middle to offset subsequent splices, as splices are potential weak points.
If the twisted fibers are thin enough, they can be braided to create a thicker, stronger cord.
To dry the fibers, hang them up in the sun or near a fire..
To make natural cordage, move your right hand back so that one inch of cord remains visible and apply another twist.
Simultaneously, grasp the left-hand cord with the middle and fourth fingers of your right hand.
Sweep these fingers upward to pull the left-hand cord under the captive piece.
Inch your left thumb and forefinger forward while clamping down on the new twist, then release your right thumb and forefinger to let the lower cord fall.
Grip the upper cord one inch from your left fingers with your right thumb and forefinger and repeat these steps along the length of the cord.
To splice in an extra length, overlap the new piece two inches along the existing cord and twist them together between your left thumb and forefinger before continuing as before..
The Arbor knot is a versatile survival knot used to secure one end of a line to a fixed point without needing adjustment or to join two poles together, such as in an A-frame shelter.
To tie it, hold the working end in your right hand and the standing line in your left.
Pass the cord around the tree, then loop the working end over the standing line and back over the cord around the tree.
Keep the standing line taut with your left hand while pulling the working end down with your right to tighten an overhand knot around the standing line.
Tie a second overhand knot in the working end as close to the first knot as possible, then pull on the standing line to lock both knots together.
Give the standing line a sharp tug to secure it.
Next, pass the working end around the standing line and over itself to form an overhand loop.
Pass the free end up through this loop to complete the knot, which is now an overhand knot tied around the standing line.
The working end is the active part used for tying, while the standing end is the passive part around which the knot is formed..
The slip knot is a simple knot with many practical uses in survival situations.
To tie it, loop the working end of the line and pull to tighten the loop.
To loosen it, pull on the loop itself.
When tying, hold the working end between your right thumb and forefinger and hold the standing end between your left thumb and forefinger.
The double chain fastening knot securely attaches the free end of a line to a fixture, particularly when weight is involved and you do not want the knot to slip or come loose.
To tie this knot, loop the working end around the fixture such as a tree or pole.
Pass it over and around the standing line.
Twist the standing line over the working line to form a loop.
Hold this loop between your left thumb and forefinger.
Pass the working end back around the fixture and then bring it under the standing line.
Make a loop with the working end and pass it into the first loop.
Collect the second loop with your left thumb and forefinger and pull it through.
Pass the working end over the top and around the standing line again, and once more around the fixture.
With your right thumb and forefinger, pull both ends away from the loop in your left hand.
Put the loop over whatever you want to secure.
Pull the knot tight.
Bring the working line around and thread it under itself to complete the knot.
Lock the knot into position..
The Siberian hitch, also known as the Evenk knot, is an effective method for attaching a rope to a fixture, such as securing a ridgeline to a tree for a poncho shelter.
To tie the knot, hold the working line in your right hand and the standing line in your left.
Pass the cord around the tree, then pinch the working line with your left-hand fingers.
Pull the working line up through the loop to tighten the knot on itself, followed by pulling the working end to secure it tightly around the tree.
To release the knot, pull the end of the standing line.
To adjust or manipulate the knot, bring your left hand under the standing line and turn your palm so that one loop twists over another.
Hold the working end between your right thumb and forefinger, then bring the loop around your left fingers over the standing line.
Lay the standing line on your left palm and loop the working line one and a half times around your fingers before hooking your thumb into the loop to complete the adjustment..
You can dismantle a tent or free a boat from its mooring more quickly by finishing off a taut line hitch with a quick-release knot.
The taut line hitch is widely used in survival and outdoor activities because it allows you to adjust the tension on a fixed line, such as a guy-line on a tent or a boat mooring in tidal waters.
To tie this knot, attach the standing line to an anchor point.
Form a loop with the working line and pass the working end through it.
Repeat the process by making a loop, known as a bight, in the working line and passing it through the earlier loop rather than just the end.
Pass the working end up through that loop again so the two knots sit side by side without tightening them yet.
Tighten the knot as usual for a taut line hitch but leave the working end free so you can pull it quickly to release the knot.
To secure the knot, pass the working end under the standing line and back through the loop it created, ensuring it goes outside the earlier loops.
Overhand the loop around a tree if necessary.
Tighten the knot by pulling both lines.
Pulling the knot farther from the anchor point will increase the tension..
Anyone venturing into the wilderness should carry a knife, preferably a bushcraft knife, as it is likely the most important survival tool after knowledge itself.
Large working blades are vital for those living primitive lives; examples include parangs and machetes, which feature blades about 18 to 20 inches long and are used mainly for cutting and slashing.
The kukri is another similar knife used by the Gurkhas of Nepal.
When using these large knives, it is important to wear a wrist lanyard.
Ideal knives should have a blade that is 3/16 inch thick, made of stainless or high-carbon steel, with an attachment point for the wrist lanyard.
Blades that extend through to the end of the handle have no weak points.
For comfort and durability, avoid ribbed rubber handles; instead, choose fitted leather sheaths that hold the knife securely and can accommodate a firesteel for sparking fires.
A guard on the blade reduces the chance of hands slipping onto the edge.
Additionally, sharp blades that end near the handle put less strain on the wrist when working close to the handle.
An experienced person can accomplish most survival skills, such as making feather sticks, with a bushcraft knife because it possesses features that help in every situation..
A bushcraft knife is designed for carving, cutting, and splitting small logs; use a machete or saw for chopping large logs.
The blade should not exceed 8 to 9 inches in length.
When passing a knife within a group, ensure only one good knife is available and pass it handle first with the blade facing upward to maintain safety.
To cut close to your body while maintaining wrist control, use the chest lever grip by holding the knife in a backhand position and utilizing arm and chest muscles for power.
For tasks requiring precise visibility and control, especially when follow-through might cause injury, adopt the backhand grip where the blade faces upward.

The forehand grip is the most natural method, allowing your back and arm to provide strength while your wrist controls the action; ensure the blade faces down and place one or both thumbs on the back of the blade for enhanced control.
Generally, grip the handle at the end nearest the blade to utilize the section of the blade closest to the handle, avoiding serrated edges as they hinder this specific cutting technique.
Always clean and dry the blade when not in use before returning it to its sheath.
Wear the sheath securely on your belt or around your neck using a lanyard..

Water is essential for survival and requires a regular intake of 2–3 quarts daily to maintain balance and prevent dehydration, though needs increase with high temperatures, physical exertion, injury, or age.
Soldiers in extreme environments like deserts or jungles may require up to 14 liters per day.
Always plan your movements around water availability and ensure you can replace lost fluids.
Filter and purify any water before drinking it using available hydration systems or small trail methods.

While stomach bugs from contaminated water can impair performance, dehydration is far more deadly; therefore, if no clean water is available, drink contaminated water rather than nothing so that medical treatment remains an option.
Never consume urine or salt water as these accelerate dehydration.
In short-term survival situations, food is not a primary priority since starvation does not occur within a few days, but hunger and energy loss can affect coordination after several days.
If food is found, eat small amounts frequently while ensuring sufficient water is available for digestion.

In long-term survival scenarios, the need for food becomes critical, so regularly reassess your situation and adjust plans accordingly to avoid reaching a state where you lack the physical capacity to obtain food when it becomes necessary.
Obtaining wild food requires knowledge, effort, skill, and luck..
Your body converts food into fuel, providing heat and energy while aiding recovery from work, injury, or sickness.
A healthy body can survive for weeks without food by using reserves stored in its tissues.
You expend approximately 70 calories per hour just breathing and up to 5,500 calories per day if working hard.
When gathering food in the wild, always ensure that the energy gained from the food exceeds the energy expended in procuring it; otherwise, the effort is wasteful.
Food that is easy to find, gather, and prepare should be your first priority.

Plants are easy to collect and should be your first choice for food as long as they are readily available in your environment, but you must be absolutely sure they are edible because mistakenly eating the wrong leaf or berry could cause vomiting and diarrhea, worsening your situation.
Avoid mushrooms unless you are 100% sure they are edible, as they can be fatal.
Fishing requires little effort once lines or traps are set, and they will work around the clock; fish is high in protein and relatively simple to prepare and cook.
Insects, reptiles, and amphibians may also be available, but remember that many are poisonous, although they can be used as bait for fish and mammals.
Birds and mammals have their own survival mechanisms and are wary of humans, especially in remote areas where contact with humans is limited.
Even if caught, a bird or mammal will need to be killed, plucked or skinned, and cooked before consumption..

Water is essential for every physical and chemical process in the human body, serving as a delivery service to transport oxygen and nutrients, a waste remover to flush toxins via urine, a coolant to regulate body temperature, a breathing aid to moisten inhaled air, a sensory aid to conduct nervous impulses, and a shock absorber to protect vital organs and lubricate joints.
Dehydration occurs when water loss exceeds intake due to factors like extreme temperatures, humidity, physical exertion, clothing, body size, fitness levels, or injury.

The effects of water loss progress from mild symptoms such as thirst, stiffness in joints, darkened urine, dry mouth, headache, nausea, and dizziness to severe conditions including impaired vision, deafness, shrunken skin, loss of appetite, impatience, drowsiness, lethargy, inability to swallow, slurred speech, swollen tongue, tingling limbs, difficulty walking, breathing problems, blueness of extremities, delirium, unconsciousness, and ultimately death.
Survival requires maintaining a steady balance between water intake and loss to prevent the fatal progression of dehydration..
Water constitutes approximately 80 percent of brain tissue, protecting organs from shock, and makes up about 22 percent of bones.
It moistens soft tissues in the nose, mouth, and eyes while helping dissolve minerals and nutrients for body access.

Water flushes waste products through the kidneys and liver, provides cushioning for joints and soft tissues, aids digestion to convert food into energy, moistens lungs to assist breathing, regulates blood pressure, helps remove fatty acids from the body to reduce heart attack risk, hydrates skin, and flushes toxins through the bladder.
Additionally, the evaporation of sweat from the skin helps regulate body temperature..
Water-borne diseases result from ingesting water contaminated by human or animal feces and urine, which contain protozoa, viruses, bacteria, or intestinal parasites.
These illnesses cause approximately 10 million deaths annually worldwide.

Common symptoms include loss of appetite, nausea, abdominal pain, profuse foul-smelling watery diarrhea, vomiting, lethargy, fever, blood in the urine, abdominal cramps, dark-colored urine, jaundice, aching muscles, and rapid dehydration.
Specific conditions such as infectious hepatitis, bacillary dysentery, giardiasis, cryptosporidium, amebic dysentery, cholera, E.

coli infections, leptospirosis, salmonella, bilharzia, and hookworms present with varying combinations of these symptoms, including blood or pus in stools, cold clammy skin, increased heart rate, coughing up larvae, and severe fatigue.
Some diseases can be fatal if not diagnosed early, particularly affecting vulnerable groups like the very young or the elderly..
The amount of water needed to survive depends on your physical condition, environment, and exertion level.
Even while resting in the shade, an average person loses more than one quart of water daily through breathing and urination, a loss that increases significantly with sweating.
A minimum of three quarts per day is required to stay healthy in a survival situation, with higher needs for extreme heat or heavy workloads.
In the first 24 hours, you must address protection and location, which are demanding tasks that increase thirst.
Drinking too much water can cause hyponatremia, a condition where excess water dilutes sodium levels in the blood, leading to cell swelling, neurological issues, coma, and death.
Prevention involves controlling water and salt intake; if you lack salt or tablets, filter saltwater through fabric to remove salt content.

If your water supply is limited, ration it efficiently until rescue, but ensure adequate hydration early on rather than going without water for the first 24 hours unless specific circumstances dictate otherwise.
Factors such as stress from the incident, seasickness, injury, or harsh environments like deserts may make water rationing unviable.
Drinking nothing for the first day with a limited supply can lead to severe dehydration where remaining water provides no benefit..
To find water in temperate climates, locate the most accessible source near your camp and position a catchment device there to maximize exposure to rainfall.
Secure a tarpaulin to four stakes using string, ensuring one end is higher than the other to create a natural run-off channel.
Place a heavy stone in the middle of the tarp, about two-thirds of the way toward the lower end, to direct rainwater into this channel.
Position a container, such as a mess can, below the end of the channel to collect the flowing water.

Any non-porous material can serve as a catchment device for collecting rainwater, which is the safest method for procuring drinking water without prior treatment, provided the collection device itself remains uncontaminated..
In temperate climates, always treat water from natural sources before drinking, with the sole exception of rainwater.
Most natural water sources are used by animals for drinking, bathing, urinating, and defecating, so you must filter and purify collected water to avoid contamination.
Streams and rivers provide permanent water in low-lying areas; green vegetation often indicates their presence.
Water near mountaintops is typically clearer and flows faster, so collect it from the surface while checking upstream for contaminants or following the flow downstream.
Rock holes found in high ground act as natural rainwater collectors; if water appears trapped deep within, surgical tubing can be used to retrieve it.
Wells may be covered and marked by locals in remote areas, so learn local markers before use.
Collect water at the point where rivers, streams, or run-offs enter lakes or ponds, avoiding stagnant water within the body.
Soaks are found near rivers in low-lying areas below the existing water table and are often indicated by vegetation but may be polluted by animal activity.
Seepage occurs at the base of cliffs or rocky outcrops through slow-running drainage channels.
In arid areas, rivers and streams usually only flow during floods and contain higher levels of pollutants.
