Be aware that dry riverbeds can experience flash floods during the rainy season that move faster than you can run.
Observe animal behavior as a warning sign; for instance, if small gazelles suddenly disappear from a water source, investigate the cause before collecting water..
To collect water from dew in temperate climates, tie absorbent material such as rags or a T-shirt tightly around each ankle and walk through dew-covered grass before sunrise or late in the evening.

The material will absorb moisture from the air and surfaces as you move; wring out the fabric to extract the water into a container and repeat the process until you have an ample supply or the dew has evaporated.
Dew forms on exposed non-porous surfaces like car roofs or tarpaulins when surface temperatures are low enough to cause condensation of moisture from warmer air.
You can also harvest dew by walking through fields with absorbent material tied around your ankles.
Alternatively, construct a dew trap by digging a hole approximately 18 inches (45 cm) deep, lining it with a plastic sheet, and filling it with smooth, clean stones.
Water will condense on the stones overnight; line the bottom of the hole to collect the water and weigh down the lining with a stone to keep it in place.
Harvest the collected water as early as possible the next morning to prevent evaporation..
To find water in temperate climates, create a transpiration bag by placing a smooth rock in the lower corner of a plastic bag and securing it over tree branch leaves.
As water evaporates from the leaves, it condenses and collects at the lowest point of the bag, which can be retrieved using surgical tubing.
Alternatively, cut green vegetation into a plastic bag, add a smooth rock to the bottom corner, tie the bag shut, and secure it in direct sunlight to encourage evaporation and condensation.
To delay dehydration, conserve existing water supplies and use them efficiently.
Work only during the coolest parts of the day to avoid sweating.
If the sun is shining, seek shade and keep covered.
Suck on a small, smooth button or pebble to stimulate saliva production and reduce the sensation of thirst.
Avoid consuming protein-rich foods because they require more water to digest than other food groups..
To construct a solar still, locate or dig a hole in the ground that is at least two feet wide and two feet deep.
Place an empty container in the center of the hole.
Fill the surrounding area with vegetation, a receptacle containing undrinkable water such as saltwater or urine, or fabric soaked in undrinkable water.
Cover the entire hole with a plastic sheet.
Position a stone in the center of the plastic sheet to create a low point that directs condensed water into the container below.
The sun's heat will cause water from the vegetation or undrinkable sources to evaporate and distill into pure vapor.
This vapor will condense on the underside of the plastic sheet and drip into the central container.

Essential tools and materials include digging implements, two containers, vegetation or undrinkable water, fabric or clothing, a plastic sheet, tarpaulin, or space blanket, stones, and optionally surgical tubing to retrieve the collected water..
To locate hidden water sources in temperate climates, observe the landscape for patches of green vegetation, noting that plants may draw moisture from deep roots rather than surface water.
Water is more likely to be found downhill or in low-lying areas such as valleys and at the base of cliffs or rock formations.
On coastlines, look for wetlands where water has seeped inland, which may contain tolerable salt levels or can be distilled using a solar still.
Watch animal behavior closely; listen for birds twittering at dawn and dusk and follow their flight paths to find drinking spots.
Flocks of birds circling over one spot usually indicate a water source below, except for meat-eating birds like vultures.
All finches and grain-eaters require regular water supplies, so observe their flight patterns to locate sources.
Animal tracks often lead to water, particularly where sets of tracks converge.
The presence of flies, bees, and mosquitoes almost certainly means water is nearby.
Additionally, look for herd animals such as elephants and wildebeest, as they depend on accessible water..

In hot-humid climates like jungles and rainforests, your body loses significant water through perspiration to regulate temperature, requiring you to drink up to 3.5 gallons (14 liters) daily to avoid dehydration.
While water sources are plentiful in these environments, rivers and streams may be unavailable during certain seasons or at high elevations, making alternative procurement methods essential.
The most effective method is catching rainwater using passive collection devices that require no energy once set up.
You can construct a sloped bamboo roof with a gutter to channel rain into containers, or create a drainpipe by tying half-cut bamboo along the path of water running down a tree trunk.
Alternatively, wrap absorbent materials like rags or T-shirts around leaning trees to catch dripping water; shape the material to form a low point where water drips and place a container underneath.
Wide-leafed plants can also be used to build a roof by overlapping leaves from bottom to top to channel water into a bamboo gutter placed at the base.
Regardless of the method used, all collected rainwater must be filtered and purified before consumption..
In hot-humid climates, you can source water from vegetation such as pitcher plants that collect rainfall or dew in hollow parts.
Some trees store rainwater in natural receptacles like cracks or hollows, and life-saving liquid can be obtained from a tree's roots or sap.
To access water trapped in green bamboo, carefully cut into the bamboo with a machete or knife.
You can also collect small unripe coconuts to drink their fluid or create a spigot to tap water directly from a tree.
Water vines found in tropical jungles and rainforests are identifiable by their size and shape and provide fresh water, though not all contain drinkable liquid as some hold poisonous sap.
Most water-bearing vines are about 2 inches in diameter.
When found, make a small cut with a machete to check the sap's color; if the fluid is milky, do not drink it, but if it is clear, the water is safe.
Cut off the vine at a point lower than the first cut so the liquid, which has a neutral fruity taste, can flow.
Prevent the vine from touching your mouth because the bark may contain irritants.
If the pores in the upper end of the vine close and stop water flow, simply cut the top of the vine again with your machete to rectify this..
Anyone entering hot-dry climates should carry sufficient water for their needs plus an emergency supply.
Green vegetation usually indicates the presence of water or moisture, and techniques used in temperate climates may work in some desert conditions.
If no surface water is available and other means are unavailable, a water-yielding plant may be your only option.
While clear sap, fruit, or trapped rainwater from plants can quench immediate thirst, do not rely on these sources for long-term survival.
Unusual clusters of green plants may indicate a minor presence of water, while an abundance of greenery suggests a more substantial source.
Water is rarely found deeper than 6 feet below the surface, so you will need to dig for it during the coolest part of the day.
Be aware that finding water inside the bend of a dry wadi in a real survival situation has slim chances.
Prickly pear cacti, found in low-spreading clumps up to 3 feet tall in dry sandy soils, have edible fruit that can provide life-saving fluid.

If you must procure water in the desert, you are likely already in a desperate situation, so combine knowledge of temperate environment techniques with the methods described here to increase your chances of survival.
Always remove the prickly outer layer of the prickly pear fruit before consumption..
Always start your journey hydrated and carry enough water to meet your needs plus an emergency supply sufficient to get you out of danger.
Cache water ahead if necessary and check your map for probable water sources, confirming their reliability with locals and asking about unmarked wells.

Barrel-shaped cacti contain a safe-to-drink milky fluid; carefully remove the top with a machete, mash the flesh into pulp with a stick, and extract juice by sucking through a hollow grass stem or soak up the fluid with cloth and wring it out, though rewards from both methods are minimal.
Agaves native to Mexico and the southern and western United States have thick fleshy leaves containing drinkable fluid; cut the huge flower stalk with a knife or machete to collect the juice.
Monitor your progress against water usage, reevaluating goals if you use more than expected, and remember it is better to turn back than push on into unnecessary danger.
Mark your map or GPS waypoints for any water sources sighted during travel so you can return to known sources rather than relying on hope.
Keep water bottles in the shade or in windy locations to maintain cool temperatures..
Build a fire to generate heat for melting ice or snow.
To collect meltwater from ice, construct a platform using a large flat stone supported by logs or smaller stones on either side of the fire.
Angle the platform to create natural runoff channels for the melting water so it flows into a collection container.

If you already possess some water, pour it into a container and heat it over the fire; add small pieces of broken ice to accelerate melting while keeping the water hot but not boiling to prevent evaporation.
Alternatively, if no water is available, fashion a gently sloping platform from flat stone or wood positioned above a fire to slowly melt ice or snow, collecting the resulting water as it flows off.
When choosing between ice and snow for melting, prioritize ice because it melts faster and is denser; if ice cannot be found, use compact snow instead..
Prioritize water usage in cold weather with the same urgency as in desert environments.
Always locate a water source near your fire-building and maintenance area.
Before attempting to melt snow or ice, search for alternative natural water sources, as collecting meltwater is more time- and fuel-efficient.
Your ability to procure water in freezing conditions depends directly on your capacity to start and maintain a fire.
To melt ice safely, build a small fire on a platform positioned on a gentle slope to allow melted water to run off, supported by two logs with the block of ice placed in the center.
Never try to melt ice or snow in your mouth, as this can cause freezing injuries to your lips and mouth; additionally, melting ice inside your body expends vital heat that could lead to hypothermia.
Ensure you have sufficient fuel to maintain a fire for a considerable period.
Regulate your body heat to minimize overheating and sweating.

Keep drinking water close to your body to prevent freezing, but avoid placing containers directly against your skin; instead, store them between layers of clothing to utilize the warmer trapped air, which helps raise the water's temperature.
Do not use recently frozen seawater because it contains high levels of salt..
To find water in cold climates, cut a solid piece of dense snow, often called a marshmallow, and skewer it with a stick.
Secure the stick in the ground close enough to a fire so that it receives heat, and position a suitable container underneath to collect the water as it melts.
If you already have water, follow techniques for melting ice by heating a little water and adding bits of snow.

Do not pack the snow too tightly; if an air pocket forms, the heat from the fire will be absorbed by the metal container rather than by the snow, which could result in the fire burning a hole through the metal container before it melts the snow.
Snow is a great insulator; even if the temperature dips to -40°F (-40°C), water in a bottle will remain largely unfrozen if placed under at least 1 foot of snow.
Make sure you store the bottles upside-down so that if some of the water does freeze, it freezes at the bottom of the bottle and not at the top.
Always look for the whitest, purest-looking snow, and ice that is bluish..
To find water in cold climates using a melting sack, create an improvised sack from any porous material like a T-shirt or sock and suspend it near a fire using a stick.
Position the sack close enough to the fire so that heat melts the snow inside, which can then be collected in a container.
If you lack natural fuel for melting ice or snow but have your first- or second-line equipment, you can use your survival fire can as a container.
Set up the fire can on the ground and shelter it from wind using your body or pack.
Place small quantities of ice or snow into the can, light the fire can, and position it over the flame.
Add more ice or snow as it begins to melt.
With these tools, you can procure life-saving water even without access to natural fuel sources..

The sea presents one of the most challenging environments for survival due to its lack of natural protection against extreme temperatures, wind, rain, and rough conditions, as well as offering little help with location.
You should avoid abandoning your vessel unless it is absolutely necessary.
If you are adrift in a liferaft with no immediate hope of rescue, obtaining drinking water becomes a major priority.
Even without specialized equipment like solar stills or reverse-osmosis pumps, there are other methods to procure fresh water.
These include channeling collected rainwater into a receptacle and spreading a tarpaulin over a large surface area to catch as much rainwater as possible.

It is critical never to drink saltwater because its salt concentration is three times higher than that of blood; ingesting it will cause dehydration, and prolonged use can lead to kidney failure and death..
To find water at sea, utilize the built-in rainwater catchment systems found in most modern liferafts which channel rain and dew into internal pockets.

You can also construct a similar system using a tarpaulin or any waterproof material by spreading it in a bowl shape to maximize collection during showers; always deploy this before nightfall to capture overnight rainfall.
To harvest dew, secure the tarpaulin like a sunshade at night and turn up its edges to catch moisture, or use a sponge or cloth to collect dew from the sides of the raft.
Before abandoning ship, retrieve as much fresh water and signaling devices as possible.
Conserve your water rations by fixing a daily ration based on your total supply, the output of solar stills or desalting kits, and the size and condition of your party.
Prevent contamination by keeping fresh water supplies shaded from both direct sunlight and glare off the sea's surface.
In hot conditions, you may dampen clothes with saltwater to lower body temperature, but avoid overdoing it to prevent saltwater boils and rashes.
Relax and sleep whenever possible to reduce water loss.
Use every available container, even simple garbage bags, to collect rainwater, ensuring they remain sealed and attached to the raft.
If you have no water, do not eat, as protein consumption will hasten dehydration..
Reverse-osmosis pumps are hand-powered devices that force seawater through a membrane to filter out salt, capable of producing approximately 23 quarts or liters of fresh water daily.
These units require significant physical effort, so users should share the task to avoid excessive perspiration and water loss.
Desalting kits utilize ion exchange to convert seawater into fresh water but produce only small amounts over several hours; they should be reserved for situations where solar stills cannot be used.
Solar stills offer a simple method of distilling water using sunlight by placing saltwater at the bottom of a container lined with black fabric to absorb heat.
The sun evaporates the water, causing pure vapor to condense on the inner surface of clear plastic and drip down the sides for collection via a tube.
Most solar stills found on liferafts are inflatable.
While these products are standard issue on most liferafts, it is advisable to carry at least one desalting device when entering a marine environment if rainwater or dew cannot be collected..
To construct a solar still for finding water at sea, gather two containers of different sizes, a plastic sheet, string, and a weight.
Place the smaller container inside the larger one and cover the opening with the plastic sheet.
Secure the sheet to the rim using string and place a rock or other weight in the center of the sheet to create a low point where condensed water will drip into the inner container.
If surgical tubing is available, tie it to the setup to retrieve fresh water without dismantling the still.

In dire emergencies, you may collect liquid from sea ice found in Arctic waters; look for old sea ice that appears bluish, has rounded corners, and splinters easily, though approaching icebergs is dangerous.

You can also extract drinkable fluid from fish by cutting them open to access the aqueous fluid along the spine and in the eyes, but avoid drinking other fluids as they are rich in protein and fat which will cause dehydration.

Sea turtle blood contains a salt concentration similar to humans and can be collected by slitting the throat, but this should only be done in an absolute emergency given that sea turtles are endangered.
If your water supply is not salty or poisonous but too foul to drink orally, you can rehydrate by absorbing up to a pint of water daily through the large intestine using an improvised tubing device..
All water obtained in a survival situation must be treated before drinking to eliminate harmful pathogens and microorganisms, with the exception of rainwater.
If you lack a device that both filters and disinfects water, you must complete these tasks in two separate stages.
First, filter the water to remove debris; this can be done using a Millbank bag or by constructing an improvised filter.
To build a tripod filter, gather three sticks of equal length or a bent sapling to form a stable frame.
Place materials inside the tripod frame in distinct layers, starting with the coarsest material at the top and progressing downward to increasingly finer materials such as parachute silk.
Pour water into the top layer; it will pass through the layers and become filtered as it moves through the progressively finer materials.
Even after filtration, the water must be disinfected before consumption..
To create an improvised bottle filter, cut the bottom off a plastic container or make a hole in it, and hang the vessel upside down from a branch.

Fill the container with layers of filtering materials arranged from coarse to fine as you move downward; start with coarse gravel at the top, followed by fine gravel, coarse sand, charcoal fragments, and finally fine porous material such as cloth at the bottom.
Pour water into the top opening so it filters through the layers and emerges from the bottom.
This method is effective for producing large quantities of filtered water in a compact form, though the process is slow, taking approximately five minutes to filter one quart or liter.
Note that a sock can also be used as an effective filter material.
Regardless of the filtration method used, the emerging water must still be purified before drinking..
Mini portable water purifiers are specially designed units that filter and purify contaminated water by pumping it through micro-filters, chemicals, or both.
These devices range from small emergency pumps capable of purifying up to 13 gallons (50 liters) to larger units.

To use them, lower the input tube into the water source, suck water through the straw with the filter end placed in any accessible non-saline water, and drinkable water will flow out from the output tube.
The filter system is often housed inside a water bottle.
Survival straws are compact devices that utilize carbon or iodine resin systems to eliminate diseases and harmful chemicals.
To use these straws, you must draw water into your mouth and decant it into a container so you can reach it with the straw.
Disinfecting water is vital because drinking untreated water may cause water-borne diseases.
The most effective method for treating water is boiling, but if you cannot make a fire, several devices are available to filter and purify water.
In a survival situation, the availability of water and your ability to treat, carry, and store it could be the difference between survival and death.
Gravity or pressure filters are incorporated within drinking bottles where water flows naturally via gravity or is squeezed through by the operator.
These bottles typically employ a filter to remove sediment and organic contaminants, a micron filter to remove protozoa, and a chemical that kills water-borne bacteria and viruses..

Microorganisms and virtually all intestinal pathogens are killed at temperatures well below the boiling point, so bringing water to a boil is sufficient for disinfection; continuing to boil it wastes fuel, time, and water.
If boiling is not possible or you lack a purification device, rely on non-mechanical techniques where concentration and contact time depend on water quality and temperature.

Iodine effectively destroys bacteria, viruses, and cysts at a concentration of 8mg per quart at 68°F if left for 10 minutes, though it is less effective against viruses and cysts without phosphoric acid.
Potassium permanganate granules should be mixed with water until it turns light pink and left for at least 30 minutes before drinking.

Adding unscented household bleach containing 5 percent sodium hypochlorite is a cheap way to add chlorine; use one drop per quart or two if the water is cloudy, then wait at least 30 minutes before consumption.
Ultraviolet light disrupts the DNA of harmful microorganisms, rendering them harmless, with exposure needs varying by water quality.
Passive UV methods involve filling plastic bottles with water, replacing lids, and placing them in direct sunlight on a dark surface.
Active UV devices like Steripen are small purifiers placed in pre-filtered water that can treat one quart in 48 seconds..
Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location.
If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger.
Prepare to abandon ship by steering closer to land or known shipping lanes, delegating tasks among survivors, and ensuring lifejackets and liferafts are ready.
If no one knows your status, notify people of your plight using any available means of communication.
If you have a cell or satellite phone, contact someone immediately; if the situation warrants emergency rescue and you possess a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB), activate it.
Abandon ship in a controlled manner, deploy all liferafts, and attempt to enter them while they are still dry.
Use the rescue line to reach people in distress and follow the immediate action instructions printed inside the liferaft.
Address the core principles of survival: protection from the elements, location signaling, water procurement, and food acquisition.
If you must move due to extreme survival conditions at sea, find ways to locate water and food.
In case of a sinking vessel, secure water, use location aids, and protect yourself from drowning and environmental exposure.
Avoid splashing in the water to prevent attracting sharks if animals are nearby.
If injured, stabilize your condition and apply first aid before moving or abandoning ship..
Do not neglect taking anti-seasickness tablets, as vomiting causes dehydration and reduces morale.
Ensure everyone wears survival equipment and knows how to operate it.
Do not second-guess rescuers when they arrive; follow their instructions exactly.
Keep rescue services informed of your situation and update them as conditions change.
Prepare to abandon ship by packing lifejackets and suitable clothing.
Know how to use your survival equipment and carry basic location aids like a flashlight, whistle, and plastic water bottle on your person.
Prepare the vessel for rescue by clearing the deck of loose objects and be ready to drop the sail if a helicopter rescue is attempted.
Make an inventory and ration all supplies.
Do not cut the painter to the vessel until you are certain it will sink, as the vessel itself is what rescuers will search for.
Never drink seawater under any circumstances, as it increases dehydration.
Eat only if you have sufficient water to digest the food; note that fish is high in protein and requires plenty of water.
If you cannot survive in place but also cannot move due to injury or other factors, do everything possible to attract rescue.
If your situation changes so that you must move to find help or locate a suitable place to stay, consult alternative survival guidelines.
Deploy a drogue to lessen drifting.
Inventory all food, water, and equipment, and begin rationing immediately.
Prepare water procurement devices such as solar stills and reverse osmosis pumps.
Protect yourself from the sun, wind, and salt-water spray.
If you have no liferaft, huddle together in a group with children in the center; if alone, draw your knees up to your chest to lessen heat loss.
Improvise flotation aids from anything that can float or hold trapped air, such as plastic bags, bottles, or knotted clothing..
You are only considered a survivor once you have been rescued, which means you must either achieve self-rescue or be saved by others.
Self-rescue may not always be possible due to being lost, adverse conditions like flooding or bad weather, or injuries that prevent movement.
When self-rescue is impossible, your priority is to attract attention and make contact with rescuers using available location aids or improvised tools.
Never hesitate to seek rescue due to embarrassment; survival is more important than avoiding shame.
Always inform others of your plans and expected return time so someone knows to look for you if you do not arrive.
Carry the best location aids for your environment and know how to use them effectively to increase your chances of being found.
In many situations, a critical decision involves whether to stay put or move to a safer location.
Generally, it is advisable to remain where you are because rash attempts to walk out can lead to greater danger..
A location aid can make the difference between life and death.
The Skystreme is a multitasking inflatable silver foil kite that weighs just 11/2 ounces and packs down to a small size.

It can perform four tasks in a survival situation: it acts as a location aid by being orally inflated to lift off from the ground unaided in winds of 4 mph, allowing its attached 165 feet of line to raise it above tree levels where it reflects sunlight and is visible to the naked eye at a distance of two miles.
At night, you can hang a small flashlight or Cyalume underneath the kite to aid visibility.
The metallic surface also reflects radar and can be detected by aircraft at a distance of 10 miles.

Additionally, the device functions as an emergency first aid splint when inflated around a broken or sprained limb, serves as a thermal vest when inflated and placed inside clothing to act as a body warmer, and provides water storage when used to carry and store water instead of being inflated with air..
Once you are out of immediate danger, assess your situation carefully and plan accordingly, as clear thinking at this initial stage can determine the difference between life and death.

In most cases, remaining where you are is the preferred option, but every situation is unique; factors such as circumstances, environment, conditions, and your individual state significantly impact what can be achieved.
Avoid making rash decisions to walk out of a situation, as this may place you in even greater danger.

The rule of threes helps focus decision-making, especially if you are injured, at risk of further injury, or facing immediate environmental threats: three seconds is the psychological reaction time for making a decision, three minutes is the limit your brain can survive without oxygen before suffering irreparable damage, three hours is the critical survival time in extreme climates without protection, three days is the approximate time you can live without water, and three weeks is the approximate time you can live without food.
The four priorities of survival are protection, location, water, and food, with the specific priority depending on your situation.
If you are no longer in danger from injury or the elements, focus your efforts on establishing a safe location and getting rescued.
Use the SURVIVAL acronym framework to remember what you need to do to remain alive and get rescued..

In a survival situation, seven factors known as the enemies of survival can work against you if you are not mentally prepared: pain, thirst, temperature, fear, fatigue, hunger, and boredom and loneliness.

You can memorize these using the mnemonic "Be Prepared To Face These Hostile Factors." When boredom sets in, you become inactive and lose the ability to deal with your situation effectively, so you need to keep busy.
Loneliness can add to the magnitude of your tasks, leading to a feeling of helplessness.
If you are injured, do not ignore the pain; attend to a minor injury as it could grow into a major problem.
A positive attitude coupled with keeping busy helps to distract the mind from pain.
Thirst is not a good indicator of the body's need for water, so stay ahead of dehydration and prioritize your need for water according to your environment.
You should dress to suit the environment you are in and be aware of the signs and symptoms of temperature-related conditions, such as hypothermia and heat stroke.
Fear is one of our body's greatest survival tools since it can stimulate you so that you are ready to act, however, it can also debilitate.
Fear is good as long as you have control over it, and the key to controlling fear in a survival situation is knowledge.
Tiredness leads to mistakes that, at best, cause frustration, and at worst, may result in injury or death.
Never underestimate the importance of quality rest to your physical and mental well-being.
In a short-term survival situation lasting one to five days, procuring food is not a high priority as long as you drink water and pace yourself to work within your limits.
However, take every opportunity to procure food without expending energy..
Assess the particulars of your surroundings, physical condition, and equipment.
If you are in a group, share tasks and responsibilities.
The true skill of a survivor is to understand what is required and improvise solutions to particular problems.
Determine if you have the skills to be proactive in your own rescue.
In any survival situation, it always helps to know where you are so that you can make the best decisions about what to do and where to go next.
Observe how indigenous people and local wildlife have developed ways of adapting to their environment to survive.
Most people react to a true survival situation through either training or instinct.
Listen to your subconscious survival senses and gut feelings.
Some people without training and equipment have survived the most horrendous situations simply because they had the will to live.
Fear and panic can be formidable enemies, so it is imperative that you have the knowledge and training to counteract them and prevent them from making your situation worse.
Learning basic skills increases your chances of survival; without training, your prospects are down to luck, which is never the best place to start..
Look at how local people dress and act; in hot countries, they leave manual work until the coolest parts of the day and work slowly to reduce sweating and conserve water.
In a jungle, pay attention when animals become quiet or quickly leave an area because danger is usually nearby.
You may start with all the right equipment, but it may get lost, broken, or wear out, so your ability to improvise will help you survive in relative comfort.
Think laterally, like a climber stranded on a mountain who used his camera flash to signal a rescue helicopter.
Knowing your precise location clarifies whether rescuers are likely to find you or if you must rescue yourself, and it gives you an idea of the obstacles you may face.
Channel your efforts into making sure that when rescuers are looking for you, your aids to location are in place.
Whatever has happened, approach your predicament in a calm and rational manner because the situation requires careful thought and planning.
If you act in haste, you may overlook important factors, lose vital equipment, or simply make matters worse, as the saying "Undue haste makes waste" is especially true in a survival situation.
The stories of prisoners of war often reveal what kept them alive: religious beliefs, thoughts of family and friends, or a determination not to let the enemy win.
If the will to live is not there, then just having the knowledge and equipment may not be enough.
If uncontrolled, fear and panic can destroy your ability to make rational decisions, causing you to react to your feelings and imagination rather than to your actual situation.
If you are in a group, your responses can directly affect others; positive responses are productive and can motivate.

Prior preparation is the key to survival: discover what you need to know about the environment you are going to, familiarize yourself with your equipment, and practice your basic skills until they become second nature.
This thorough preparation will help you combat the fear of the unknown and increase your self-confidence.
Every environment has its own particular characteristics such as hot, dry, cold, or wet, so determine what you need to do to adapt to that environment.
Assess how your equipment can be used best and remove yourself and others from danger..
There are three principles for attracting attention: attract, hold, and direct.
To attract, pick a place that maximizes your chances of being seen, such as open or high ground.
To hold, maintain the signal until rescuers indicate they have seen you and try to send critical information via a May Day or Help message.
To direct, do all you can to guide rescuers to your present location.
If you cannot rescue yourself due to injury, being lost, or bad weather, you must use location aids to attract attention.
These aids can save your life, so ensure you know how to use them effectively.
A helicopter on a search pattern may make only one pass over an area before moving on, so you must act decisively within a few minutes.

Location aids include satellites in geostationary earth orbit and low-altitude earth orbit, as well as Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacons used at sea and Emergency Locator Transmitters mostly for use in aircraft..
Choose a cell phone equipped with a GPS unit to fix and track your position, along with a camera to send pictures of your location and any injuries.
Alternatively, purchase a satellite phone connected to the Iridium Satellite Phone System.
When activated, a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) transmits a radio distress signal to two complementary satellite systems known as LEOSAR and GEOSAR, which together form the COSPAS-SARSAT system.
This signal is relayed to the rescue coordination center closest to the beacon's location.
Marine VHF radios are handheld units that transmit and receive on frequencies between 156 to 174 MHz, typically using Channel 16 for international calling and distress.
Your marine radio device should be waterproof, able to float, and kept on charge.
Always take a cell or satellite phone with you when traveling, and bring a marine VHF radio on sea trips.
Personal Locator Beacons are designed for personal use and should always be used responsibly; they are for emergency use only..
To create a dome signal fire that generates enough smoke to be seen from afar, construct a large fire on a raised platform beneath a dome-shaped structure made from bent saplings.
If saplings are unavailable, use poles to form a teepee shape instead.
Bend two long saplings at 90 degrees to each other to create the dome frame.
Prop two parallel long poles up over a log or rock at one end and secure them with four stakes at the other end to steady the platform.
Lash additional poles to each side of the dome for stability.
Drive pegs into the ground to anchor the structure further.
Lay lengths of green wood side by side on the supporting poles.
Prepare tinder, kindling, fuel, and vegetation; fold small dead branches from lower tree trunks into bundles held together by a thin branch or vine.
Stab a branch or vine into the bundle, wrap it around, and tuck it under itself to secure the kindling.
Place the prepared fire on the platform and load it with green vegetation.
Position the vegetation close enough to the fuel below to catch easily when lit, but ensure it is not smothered.
Prepare the initial flame using a lighter or matches to ignite the kindling and turn it into a sustained fire.
Required tools include a knife, saw, poles, long saplings, green vegetation, tinder, kindling, fuel, and a light source such as a lighter or matches..
Build three fires in a recognized formation, either as a triangle or a straight line, ensuring they are at least 65 feet apart.
Site the firebase off the ground to prevent dampness and allow sufficient airflow for ignition.
Keep dry tinder in a waterproof container nearby along with fuel sources such as hexamine tablets, stove fuel, gasoline, paper, or birch bark.
Prepare a witch's broom by stuffing a cleft stick with kindling or bark to quickly light and transfer fire to the signal site.
Leave an access point for lighting the fire but keep it closed to maintain dryness of tinder, kindling, and fuel.
Layer green vegetation on top of the dome to form a roof over the fire platform and keep additional green vegetation nearby to add smoke.
Light the witch's broom from your camp fire, take it to the dome, and use it to ignite the signal fire.
Use kindling to help start the fire.
When smoke billows upward, light a preprepared signal flare taped or tied to a long pole and position the flare as high into the smoke column as possible.
Cover the top of the firebase with green vegetation and other smoky materials to keep the base dry while increasing visibility..
In addition to signal fires, other visual devices can attract rescuers.
Whatever device you use, persist with your signal to hold the rescuers' attention.
To create a highly visible illuminated windmill effect, attach a chemical lightstick containing cyalume to a cord about three feet long and whirl it in front of you.
Depending on local conditions, this signal can be seen up to two miles away or even by an aircraft crew.
While whirling the lightstick, blow your whistle using the International Distress Signal of six blasts over one minute followed by one minute of silence; a reply consists of three short blasts.
For daylight hours only, you can use a tinsel tree when staying in one place for a while.
Cut a silver survival blanket or any reflective material into strips and attach them to a tree so they move in the breeze and catch sunlight, glinting like mirrors..
In an emergency, proceed in the direction indicated by arrows on signs that require medical assistance or other rescue signals.
Signal flares come with one end producing orange smoke for daytime visibility and another end emitting a bright flare for nighttime use; however, the flare end can also be used during the day.
Always follow the manufacturer's instructions printed on the outside of the device and wear gloves to protect your hands from heat.
Keep the flare away from a liferaft when lighting it to prevent damage, and ensure you do not discard any flares until both ends have been used.
Ground-to-air markers can be improvised using items that contrast sharply with the ground, such as orange lifejackets, ensuring the message is large enough to be seen from all directions.
You may use internationally recognized emergency codes or write SOS or HELP in large letters to attract attention.
Always carry a whistle when venturing into the wilderness because sound travels great distances in areas with little noise pollution; tie the whistle around your neck for easy access.
The most recognized signs of distress are a red star shooting up into the sky or a billowing cloud of orange smoke produced by signal flares and rockets.
These devices range from simple handheld units to specialized kits capable of penetrating jungle canopy, and you should hold the flare as high as possible to maximize visibility..
Assess your situation immediately by determining if anyone knows you are missing or where you are, and evaluate whether you can survive in your current location.
If you are in a group, prioritize helping others who are in danger.

You must decide between staying put or moving based on specific conditions: if no one knows you are missing, you must notify people of your plight using any available means, as a rescue party will likely be dispatched if you are missed.
If you cannot survive where you are and there are no physical reasons to remain, you must move to a location offering better survival chances or rescue opportunities.
Get yourself out of cold, wind, and wet conditions to prevent hypothermia; avoid confrontation with animals by moving away from danger; stabilize injuries and apply first aid.
Use cell or satellite phones to inform others of your predicament, and consider using a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) if the situation warrants emergency rescue.
Address the core principles of survival: protection, location, water, and food..
Use your body heat to melt snow, as this process lowers your body temperature and can induce hypothermia.
Breathe air onto cold hands because the moisture in your breath will cool down and conduct heat away from them.
Avoid sitting or lying directly on the cold ground; instead, use available materials to improvise a sitting or sleeping platform.
Select a shelter site away from dangers like avalanches, ensuring it is large enough for you and your equipment.
Incorporate a cold sink and keep your sleeping platform higher than the sink.
Keep cutting and digging tools in the shelter in case heavy snowfall or an avalanche requires you to dig your way out.
Mark the entrance to the shelter so you can find it easily.
Maintain a fire once established, as it can be used to melt snow and provide warmth.

Water is essential for survival, with the human body able to survive only a limited time without it.
Thirst acts as a critical enemy that must be managed carefully.
To obtain water, you can collect rainwater using bamboo, wide-leaf roofs, or drainpipes.
Natural sources include dew, cactus plants, fish, melting snow and ice, and underground water found via vines.
Plants also serve as significant sources of moisture through transpiration bags.
In hot-dry climates, specific techniques like collecting dew from cacti are vital.
You can catch rainwater in various ways to ensure a steady supply.
For those stranded at sea, blood from sea turtles can be used as an emergency water source.
Melting sacks and solar stills are effective methods for extracting water from ice or creating it from humidity.
Distillation processes and reverse-osmosis pumps can also purify available water sources.
Once collected, water must be purified to avoid water-borne diseases.
Boiling is a primary method for disinfection, alongside the use of chlorine tablets, iodine liquid, and potassium permanganate.
Bleach can also be used for purification purposes.
Bottle filters, gravity or pressure filters, tripod filters, and Millbank bag filters provide mechanical filtration options.
Solar stills combine distillation with natural heat to purify water.
UV light is another modern method for disinfecting water supplies.
Desalination techniques allow you to convert saltwater into fresh water when necessary.
Always avoid drinking saltwater directly as it accelerates dehydration.
When planning survival, consider the quantity of water needed and implement rationing strategies to stretch supplies.
In emergency situations, having a sustainment kit with stored water is crucial.
Flash floods can contaminate water sources, so be aware of wildlife dangers that may also affect water availability.
Proper camp craft in adverse weather conditions helps protect your water collection systems.
Always prioritize finding safe water sources before addressing other survival needs like food or shelter..
